GFlowNets offer principled exploration over DAGs, but prior work is token-level; a span-level DAG framework is needed.
AI.method: FoSS builds a dynamic span vocabulary by flexibly segmenting retrieved text into spans, forming a DAG-structured state space. It uses GFlowNets to sample spans, enabling diverse compositional paths. Reward models are designed to promote diversity and quality of generated text.
AI.result: Empirically, FoSS improves MAUVE by up to 12.5% over Transformer for text generation and yields ~3.5% gains on knowledge-intensive tasks. It consistently outperforms strong baselines, and scaling experiments show benefits from larger models, more data, and richer retrieval corpora, while maintaining advantage over baselines.
AI.conclusion: FoSS demonstrates that a DAG-based span-generation framework with GFlowNets can more effectively explore compositional pathways, improving generation quality and generalization, with clear benefits that scale with model size and data.

title: Neural Network Quantum Field Theory from Transformer Architectures
summary: We propose a neural-network construction of Euclidean scalar quantum field theories from transformer attention heads, defining $n$-point correlators by averaging over random network parameters in the NN-QFT framework. For a single attention head, shared random softmax weights couple different width coordinates and induce non-Gaussian field statistics that persist in the infinite-width limit $d_k\to\infty$. We compute the two-point function in an attention-weight representation and show how Euclidean-invariant kernels can be engineered via random-feature token embeddings. We then analyze the connected four-point function and identify an "independence-breaking" contribution, expressible as a covariance over query-key weights, which remains finite at infinite width. Finally, we show that summing many independent heads with standard $1/N_h$ normalization suppresses connected non-Gaussian correlators as $1/N_h$, yielding a Gaussian NN-QFT in the large-head limit.

AI.tldr: A study linking transformer attention heads to Euclidean scalar QFTs; single-head non-Gaussian statistics persist at infinite width, while averaging many heads yields Gaussian NN-QFT; analyzes two- and four-point functions and random-feature embeddings for Euclidean invariance.
AI.motivation: Investigate how neural architectures, specifically transformer attention heads with random parameters, can construct Euclidean scalar quantum field theories, and how Gaussianity of correlators emerges in large-width/head limits.
AI.method: Model uses random softmax weights to couple width coordinates; computes two-point functions in an attention-weight representation; engineers Euclidean-invariant kernels via random-feature token embeddings; analyzes connected four-point functions and identifies a finite independence-breaking covariance over query-key weights; studies the effect of summing many independent heads with 1/N_h normalization.
AI.result: Two-point functions can be realized; single-head non-Gaussian statistics persist even as width d_k→∞; a finite covariance term persists in the four-point connected function; summing many independent heads suppresses connected non-Gaussian correlators as 1/N_h, leading to a Gaussian NN-QFT in the large-head limit.
AI.conclusion: The NN-QFT framework can realize Euclidean scalar QFTs with tunable Gaussianity: non-Gaussian effects from a single head vanish in the large-head limit due to averaging, yielding Gaussian statistics; random-feature embeddings enable invariant kernels; independence-breaking terms remain finite but are suppressed with more heads.

title: Flow-Enabled Generalization to Human Demonstrations in Few-Shot Imitation Learning
summary: Imitation Learning (IL) enables robots to learn complex skills from demonstrations without explicit task modeling, but it typically requires large amounts of demonstrations, creating significant collection costs. Prior work has investigated using flow as an intermediate representation to enable the use of human videos as a substitute, thereby reducing the amount of required robot demonstrations. However, most prior work has focused on the flow, either on the object or on specific points of the robot/hand, which cannot describe the motion of interaction. Meanwhile, relying on flow to achieve generalization to scenarios observed only in human videos remains limited, as flow alone cannot capture precise motion details. Furthermore, conditioning on scene observation to produce precise actions may cause the flow-conditioned policy to overfit to training tasks and weaken the generalization indicated by the flow. To address these gaps, we propose SFCrP, which includes a Scene Flow prediction model for Cross-embodiment learning (SFCr) and a Flow and Cropped point cloud conditioned Policy (FCrP). SFCr learns from both robot and human videos and predicts any point trajectories. FCrP follows the general flow motion and adjusts the action based on observations for precision tasks. Our method outperforms SOTA baselines across various real-world task settings, while also exhibiting strong spatial and instance generalization to scenarios seen only in human videos.

AI.tldr: SFCrP combines Scene Flow prediction for cross-embodiment learning (SFCr) with a Flow and Cropped point cloud conditioned Policy (FCrP) to improve imitation learning from both robot and human videos, addressing limitations of flow-only methods and improving precision and generalization; claims SOTA performance and strong generalization to human-only seen scenarios.
AI.motivation: Reduce the demonstration burden in imitation learning by leveraging cross-embodiment data (robot and human videos). Flow-based methods alone struggle to describe interaction motion and generalize when conditioned on scene; need methods that capture full scene dynamics and precise motion.
AI.method: Two-part approach: (1) SFCr: Scene Flow prediction model trained on robot and human videos to predict trajectories for any point, enabling cross-embodiment understanding of motion; (2) FCrP: Flow and Cropped point cloud conditioned Policy that follows flow motion and refines actions using current observations (cropped point clouds) for precise tasks; together enabling cross-embodiment imitation with improved precision and generalization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows SFCrP outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across multiple real-world tasks and exhibits strong spatial and instance generalization to scenarios seen only in human videos.
AI.conclusion: SFCrP effectively addresses prior gaps by integrating scene-flow-based cross-embodiment learning with a flow-conditioned policy that uses object-centric observations, yielding enhanced imitation with better precision and cross-domain generalization.

title: ArtisanGS: Interactive Tools for Gaussian Splat Selection with AI and Human in the Loop
summary: Representation in the family of 3D Gaussian Splats (3DGS) are growing into a viable alternative to traditional graphics for an expanding number of application, including recent techniques that facilitate physics simulation and animation. However, extracting usable objects from in-the-wild captures remains challenging and controllable editing techniques for this representation are limited. Unlike the bulk of emerging techniques, focused on automatic solutions or high-level editing, we introduce an interactive suite of tools centered around versatile Gaussian Splat selection and segmentation. We propose a fast AI-driven method to propagate user-guided 2D selection masks to 3DGS selections. This technique allows for user intervention in the case of errors and is further coupled with flexible manual selection and segmentation tools. These allow a user to achieve virtually any binary segmentation of an unstructured 3DGS scene. We evaluate our toolset against the state-of-the-art for Gaussian Splat selection and demonstrate their utility for downstream applications by developing a user-guided local editing approach, leveraging a custom Video Diffusion Model. With flexible selection tools, users have direct control over the areas that the AI can modify. Our selection and editing tools can be used for any in-the-wild capture without additional optimization.

AI.tldr: An interactive framework for 3D Gaussian Splats (3DGS) segmentation/editing that propagates 2D user masks to 3DGS, augmented with manual tools, enabling flexible binary segmentation and user-guided edits via a Video Diffusion Model, without requiring extra optimization.
AI.motivation: 3D Gaussian splats are a promising representation, but extracting and editing objects from unconstrained, real-world captures is challenging. Most recent work focuses on automatic or high-level editing, leaving a gap for fine-grained, interactive control over 3DGS.
AI.method: Introduce a fast AI-driven pipeline that propagates user-guided 2D selection masks to 3DGS selections; supplement with flexible manual selection/segmentation tools; enable a user-guided local editing workflow that leverages a custom Video Diffusion Model; evaluate against state-of-the-art Gaussian Splat selection; no extra optimization required for in-the-wild captures.
AI.result: The toolset enables virtually any binary segmentation of unstructured 3DGS scenes and demonstrates utility for downstream editing; competitive performance relative to state-of-the-art Gaussian Splat selection; provides direct user control over regions that AI can modify in downstream edits.
AI.conclusion: An interactive, versatile segmentation/editing suite for 3DGS that broadens practical use-cases by combining AI-assisted mask propagation with manual tools and a local editing pipeline, applicable to in-the-wild captures without additional optimization.

title: Neuro-symbolic Action Masking for Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) may explore infeasible actions during training and execution. Existing approaches assume a symbol grounding function that maps high-dimensional states to consistent symbolic representations and a manually specified action masking techniques to constrain actions. In this paper, we propose Neuro-symbolic Action Masking (NSAM), a novel framework that automatically learn symbolic models, which are consistent with given domain constraints of high-dimensional states, in a minimally supervised manner during the DRL process. Based on the learned symbolic model of states, NSAM learns action masks that rules out infeasible actions. NSAM enables end-to-end integration of symbolic reasoning and deep policy optimization, where improvements in symbolic grounding and policy learning mutually reinforce each other. We evaluate NSAM on multiple domains with constraints, and experimental results demonstrate that NSAM significantly improves sample efficiency of DRL agent while substantially reducing constraint violations.

AI.tldr: Neuro-symbolic Action Masking (NSAM) automatically learns symbolic state models aligned with domain constraints from high-dimensional inputs to prune infeasible actions during DRL, improving sample efficiency and reducing constraint violations.
AI.motivation: DRL agents often explore infeasible actions due to opaque state-to-symbol mappings and manually crafted masks; automatic, minimally supervised symbolic grounding can enforce constraints while co-evolving with policy learning.
AI.method: NSAM learns a symbolic model of states that is consistent with domain constraints, using minimal supervision during DRL; from this model, it derives action masks to exclude infeasible actions; the framework integrates symbolic reasoning with deep policy optimization so improvements in grounding and policy reinforce each other.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across constrained domains shows NSAM reduces constraint violations and improves sample efficiency of DRL agents.
AI.conclusion: NSAM provides a practical, end-to-end neuro-symbolic approach to action masking, enabling mutual enhancement of symbolic grounding and policy learning under domain constraints.

title: How Much Reasoning Do Retrieval-Augmented Models Add beyond LLMs? A Benchmarking Framework for Multi-Hop Inference over Hybrid Knowledge
summary: Large language models (LLMs) continue to struggle with knowledge-intensive questions that require up-to-date information and multi-hop reasoning. Augmenting LLMs with hybrid external knowledge, such as unstructured text and structured knowledge graphs, offers a promising alternative to costly continual pretraining. As such, reliable evaluation of their retrieval and reasoning capabilities becomes critical. However, many existing benchmarks increasingly overlap with LLM pretraining data, which means answers or supporting knowledge may already be encoded in model parameters, making it difficult to distinguish genuine retrieval and reasoning from parametric recall. We introduce HybridRAG-Bench, a framework for constructing benchmarks to evaluate retrieval-intensive, multi-hop reasoning over hybrid knowledge. HybridRAG-Bench automatically couples unstructured text and structured knowledge graph representations derived from recent scientific literature on arXiv, and generates knowledge-intensive question-answer pairs grounded in explicit reasoning paths. The framework supports flexible domain and time-frame selection, enabling contamination-aware and customizable evaluation as models and knowledge evolve. Experiments across three domains (artificial intelligence, governance and policy, and bioinformatics) demonstrate that HybridRAG-Bench rewards genuine retrieval and reasoning rather than parametric recall, offering a principled testbed for evaluating hybrid knowledge-augmented reasoning systems. We release our code and data at github.com/junhongmit/HybridRAG-Bench.

AI.tldr: HybridRAG-Bench is a framework for evaluating retrieval-intensive, multi-hop reasoning over hybrid knowledge (unstructured text + graphs) using arXiv-derived data, with contamination-aware evaluation and multi-domain testing, showing improved discrimination between genuine retrieval and parametric recall.
AI.motivation: To address the evaluation gap where LLMs may rely on memorized training data, hindering assessment of retrieval and reasoning over external knowledge; provide a controllable, contamination-aware benchmark for hybrid knowledge augmentation.
AI.method: Automatically pair unstructured text with structured knowledge graphs derived from recent literature on arXiv; generate knowledge-intensive QA with explicit reasoning paths; allow domain/timeframe selection for contamination-aware evaluation; release code and data.
AI.result: Across artificial intelligence, governance/policy, and bioinformatics, the benchmark rewards genuine retrieval and structured reasoning over memorized recall, serving as a principled testbed for hybrid knowledge-augmented systems.
AI.conclusion: HybridRAG-Bench offers a customizable, time-aware evaluation framework and dataset for measuring hybrid knowledge retrieval and reasoning, with open-source release to promote broad adoption.

title: Pitch Angle Control of a Magnetically Actuated Capsule Robot with Nonlinear FEA-based MPC and EKF Multisensory Fusion
comment: This version is submitted for review at IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
summary: Magnetically actuated capsule robots promise minimally invasive diagnosis and therapy in the gastrointestinal (GI) tract, but existing systems largely neglect control of capsule pitch, a degree of freedom critical for contact-rich interaction with inclined gastric walls. This paper presents a nonlinear, model-based framework for magnetic pitch control of an ingestible capsule robot actuated by a four-coil electromagnetic array. Angle-dependent magnetic forces and torques acting on embedded permanent magnets are characterized using three-dimensional finite-element simulations and embedded as lookup tables in a control-oriented rigid-body pitching model with rolling contact and actuator dynamics. A constrained model predictive controller (MPC) is designed to regulate pitch while respecting hardware-imposed current and slew-rate limits. Experiments on a compliant stomach-inspired surface demonstrate robust pitch reorientation from both horizontal and upright configurations, achieving about three to five times faster settling and reduced oscillatory motion than on-off control. Furthermore, an extended Kalman filter (EKF) fusing inertial sensing with intermittent visual measurements enables stable closed-loop control when the camera update rate is reduced from 30 Hz to 1 Hz, emulating clinically realistic imaging constraints. These results establish finite-element-informed MPC with sensor fusion as a scalable strategy for pitch regulation, controlled docking, and future multi-degree-of-freedom capsule locomotion.

AI.tldr: Nonlinear, model-based pitch control for magnetically actuated ingestible capsules using FEM-informed MPC and sensor fusion; demonstrates faster pitch reorientation and robust control under low-rate imaging.
AI.motivation: GI capsule robots require reliable pitch control for contact with inclined gastric walls; existing systems largely neglect pitch control and face imaging constraints.
AI.method: 3D FEM to characterize angle-dependent magnetic forces/torques; embed as lookup tables in a rigid-body pitching model with rolling contact and actuator dynamics; design constrained MPC with current and slew-rate limits; experiments on a compliant stomach-inspired surface; EKF fusing inertial sensing with intermittent visual measurements to maintain closed-loop control at reduced camera rates.
AI.result: Robust pitch reorientation from horizontal and upright; 3–5× faster settling and reduced oscillations versus on–off control; EKF enables stable closed-loop control at 1 Hz vision; demonstrates finite-element-informed MPC with sensor fusion as a scalable strategy for pitch regulation and docking.
AI.conclusion: Finite-element-informed MPC with sensor fusion provides a scalable approach for pitch regulation, docking, and future multi-DOF capsule locomotion.

title: When the Prompt Becomes Visual: Vision-Centric Jailbreak Attacks for Large Image Editing Models
summary: Recent advances in large image editing models have shifted the paradigm from text-driven instructions to vision-prompt editing, where user intent is inferred directly from visual inputs such as marks, arrows, and visual-text prompts. While this paradigm greatly expands usability, it also introduces a critical and underexplored safety risk: the attack surface itself becomes visual. In this work, we propose Vision-Centric Jailbreak Attack (VJA), the first visual-to-visual jailbreak attack that conveys malicious instructions purely through visual inputs. To systematically study this emerging threat, we introduce IESBench, a safety-oriented benchmark for image editing models. Extensive experiments on IESBench demonstrate that VJA effectively compromises state-of-the-art commercial models, achieving attack success rates of up to 80.9% on Nano Banana Pro and 70.1% on GPT-Image-1.5. To mitigate this vulnerability, we propose a training-free defense based on introspective multimodal reasoning, which substantially improves the safety of poorly aligned models to a level comparable with commercial systems, without auxiliary guard models and with negligible computational overhead. Our findings expose new vulnerabilities, provide both a benchmark and practical defense to advance safe and trustworthy modern image editing systems. Warning: This paper contains offensive images created by large image editing models.

AI.tldr: Introduces Vision-Centric Jailbreak Attack (VJA), a visual-to-visual attack against vision-based image editing, along with IESBench benchmark and a defense via introspective multimodal reasoning; shows high attack success and practical mitigation.
AI.motivation: Safety risk arises when the attack surface of vision-prompt editing becomes visual; need systematic study of how visual inputs (marks, arrows, visuals) can convey malicious instructions to image-editing models.
AI.method: Propose VJA and build IESBench; conduct extensive experiments on state-of-the-art models; develop a training-free defense based on introspective multimodal reasoning to align models more safely without extra guard models.
AI.result: VJA achieves up to 80.9% attack success on Nano Banana Pro and 70.1% on GPT-Image-1.5; the defense substantially improves safety of poorly aligned models to commercial levels with negligible overhead.
AI.conclusion: Reveals new vulnerabilities in vision-based image editing; provides a safety benchmark and a practical defense; advances safe and trustworthy modern image editing systems; note: the paper includes offensive visual content.

title: To Think or Not To Think, That is The Question for Large Reasoning Models in Theory of Mind Tasks
summary: Theory of Mind (ToM) assesses whether models can infer hidden mental states such as beliefs, desires, and intentions, which is essential for natural social interaction. Although recent progress in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) has boosted step-by-step inference in mathematics and coding, it is still underexplored whether this benefit transfers to socio-cognitive skills. We present a systematic study of nine advanced Large Language Models (LLMs), comparing reasoning models with non-reasoning models on three representative ToM benchmarks. The results show that reasoning models do not consistently outperform non-reasoning models and sometimes perform worse. A fine-grained analysis reveals three insights. First, slow thinking collapses: accuracy significantly drops as responses grow longer, and larger reasoning budgets hurt performance. Second, moderate and adaptive reasoning benefits performance: constraining reasoning length mitigates failure, while distinct success patterns demonstrate the necessity of dynamic adaptation. Third, option matching shortcut: when multiple choice options are removed, reasoning models improve markedly, indicating reliance on option matching rather than genuine deduction. We also design two intervention approaches: Slow-to-Fast (S2F) adaptive reasoning and Think-to-Match (T2M) shortcut prevention to further verify and mitigate the problems. With all results, our study highlights the advancement of LRMs in formal reasoning (e.g., math, code) cannot be fully transferred to ToM, a typical task in social reasoning. We conclude that achieving robust ToM requires developing unique capabilities beyond existing reasoning methods.

AI.tldr: Nine-LLM study reveals that reasoning-based LLMs do not consistently outperform non-reasoning ones on Theory of Mind tasks; longer reasoning can hurt; adaptive/moderate reasoning helps; option-matching bias evident; interventions S2F and T2M proposed; robust ToM requires new capabilities beyond current reasoning methods.
AI.motivation: To test whether reasoning capabilities in LRMs generalize to socio-cognitive tasks (Theory of Mind) and identify failure modes and remedies.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of nine advanced LLMs across three ToM benchmarks, comparing reasoning vs non-reasoning variants; analyze effect of reasoning length; test option-removal effects; implement Slow-to-Fast (S2F) and Think-to-Match (T2M) interventions.
AI.result: Reasoning models do not consistently outperform non-reasoning ones; longer/greedier reasoning budgets degrade accuracy; adaptive/moderate reasoning improves performance; removing incorrect options reduces reliance on option matching, suggesting genuine deduction is not occurring; S2F and T2M interventions validate improvement and mitigation of issues.
AI.conclusion: Transfer of formal-reasoning gains to ToM is not guaranteed; developing robust ToM requires capabilities beyond existing reasoning strategies.

title: Rank-Accuracy Trade-off for LoRA: A Gradient-Flow Analysis
summary: Previous empirical studies have shown that LoRA achieves accuracy comparable to full-parameter methods on downstream fine-tuning tasks, even for rank-1 updates. By contrast, the theoretical underpinnings of the dependence of LoRA's accuracy on update rank remain relatively unexplored. In this work, we compare the accuracy of rank-r LoRA updates against full-parameter updates for fine-tuning tasks from a dynamical systems perspective. We perform gradient flow analysis in both full-rank and low-rank regimes to establish explicit relationships between rank and accuracy for two loss functions under LoRA. While gradient flow equations for LoRA are presented in prior work, we rigorously derive their form and show that they are identical for simultaneous and sequential LoRA parameter updates. We then use the resulting dynamical system equations to obtain closed-form relationships between LoRA rank and accuracy for trace-squared and Frobenius-norm low-rank approximation loss functions.

AI.tldr: Rank-r LoRA updates can match full-parameter fine-tuning accuracy; the work uses gradient-flow analysis in full-rank and low-rank regimes to derive explicit rank–accuracy relations for two losses, and shows simultaneous vs sequential LoRA updates share identical dynamics.
AI.motivation: To understand how LoRA update rank influences fine-tuning accuracy from a dynamical-systems perspective, addressing a theoretical gap in the behavior of rank-r LoRA updates.
AI.method: Perform gradient-flow analysis for LoRA in both full-rank and low-rank regimes; derive the dynamical system equations; prove equivalence of simultaneous and sequential LoRA parameter updates; obtain closed-form relationships between LoRA rank and accuracy for trace-squared and Frobenius-norm loss functions.
AI.result: Derived gradient-flow equations for LoRA that are identical for simultaneous and sequential updates; established explicit, closed-form relationships between LoRA rank and accuracy for the two loss functions (trace-squared and Frobenius-norm low-rank approximations).
AI.conclusion: A dynamical-systems framework yields quantitative links between update rank and fine-tuning accuracy, providing analytical guidance for selecting LoRA rank under the studied losses; empirical validation would still be needed to assess generality beyond the modeled losses and settings.

title: Free-Flying Crew Cooperative Robots on the ISS: A Joint Review of Astrobee, CIMON, and Int-Ball Operations
comment: Author's version of a manuscript accepted at the 2025 International Conference on Space Robotics (iSpaRo25). (c)IEEE
summary: Intra-vehicular free-flying robots are anticipated to support various work in human spaceflight while working side-by-side with astronauts. Such example of robots includes NASA's Astrobee, DLR's CIMON, and JAXA's Int-Ball, which are deployed on the International Space Station. This paper presents the first joint analyses of these robot's shared experiences, co-authored by their development and operation team members. Despite the different origins and design philosophies, the development and operations of these platforms encountered various convergences. Hence, this paper presents a detailed overview of these robots, presenting their objectives, design, and onboard operations. Hence, joint lessons learned across the lifecycle are presented, from design to on-orbit operations. These lessons learned are anticipated to serve for future development and research as design recommendations.

AI.tldr: Joint cross-platform analysis of NASA Astrobee, DLR CIMON, and JAXA Int-Ball reveals convergences in design and operations; provides lifecycle lessons and design recommendations.
AI.motivation: To synthesize experiences from multiple free-flying intra-vehicular robots in ISS to inform future designs.
AI.method: Qualitative joint analysis across platforms, compiling objectives, hardware/software design, and onboard operations; identifying common themes and lessons across lifecycle from design to on-orbit activities.
AI.result: Identified convergences across origins and design philosophies; produced a detailed overview and cross-platform lessons learned; offered design recommendations.
AI.conclusion: Lifecycle-learned insights can guide future space-robot development and research, improving interoperability and reliability.

title: DEGMC: Denoising Diffusion Models Based on Riemannian Equivariant Group Morphological Convolutions
summary: In this work, we address two major issues in recent Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM): {\bf 1)} geometric key feature extraction and {\bf 2)} network equivariance. Since the DDPM prediction network relies on the U-net architecture, which is theoretically only translation equivariant, we introduce a geometric approach combined with an equivariance property of the more general Euclidean group, which includes rotations, reflections, and permutations. We introduce the notion of group morphological convolutions in Riemannian manifolds, which are derived from the viscosity solutions of first-order Hamilton-Jacobi-type partial differential equations (PDEs) that act as morphological multiscale dilations and erosions. We add a convection term to the model and solve it using the method of characteristics. This helps us better capture nonlinearities, represent thin geometric structures, and incorporate symmetries into the learning process. Experimental results on the MNIST, RotoMNIST, and CIFAR-10 datasets show noticeable improvements compared to the baseline DDPM model.

AI.tldr: Proposes geometric key feature extraction and network equivariance for DDPMs using group morphological convolutions on Riemannian manifolds, derived from Hamilton-Jacobi PDEs with a convection term; shows improvements on MNIST, RotoMNIST, and CIFAR-10 over baseline DDPM.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of U-Net–based DDPMs that are only translation equivariant by incorporating broader Euclidean group symmetries (rotations, reflections, permutations) and to capture nonlinear geometric structures essential for denoising and generation.
AI.method: Introduce group morphological convolutions on Riemannian manifolds derived from viscosity solutions of first-order Hamilton-Jacobi-type PDEs, acting as multiscale dilations and erosions. Add a convection term and solve via the method of characteristics. Integrate these PDE-inspired, symmetry-aware operations into the diffusion model framework to enhance feature extraction and equivariance.
AI.result: Experimental results on MNIST, Rotated MNIST (RotoMNIST), and CIFAR-10 show noticeable improvements compared to the baseline DDPM model.
AI.conclusion: The work blends geometric PDE-based morphological operations with group-equivariant learning to strengthen DDPMs’ ability to capture thin, nonlinear geometric structures while respecting Euclidean symmetries, yielding improved generative/denoising performance on standard benchmarks.

title: OmniSapiens: A Foundation Model for Social Behavior Processing via Heterogeneity-Aware Relative Policy Optimization
summary: To develop socially intelligent AI, existing approaches typically model human behavioral dimensions (e.g., affective, cognitive, or social attributes) in isolation. Although useful, task-specific modeling often increases training costs and limits generalization across behavioral settings. Recent reasoning RL methods facilitate training a single unified model across multiple behavioral tasks, but do not explicitly address learning across different heterogeneous behavioral data. To address this gap, we introduce Heterogeneity-Aware Relative Policy Optimization (HARPO), an RL method that balances leaning across heterogeneous tasks and samples. This is achieved by modulating advantages to ensure that no single task or sample carries disproportionate influence during policy optimization. Using HARPO, we develop and release Omnisapiens-7B 2.0, a foundation model for social behavior processing. Relative to existing behavioral foundation models, Omnisapiens-7B 2.0 achieves the strongest performance across behavioral tasks, with gains of up to +16.85% and +9.37% on multitask and held-out settings respectively, while producing more explicit and robust reasoning traces. We also validate HARPO against recent RL methods, where it achieves the most consistently strong performance across behavioral tasks.

AI.tldr: HARPO introduces heterogeneity-aware relative policy optimization to balance learning across heterogeneous behavioral tasks and samples, mitigating dominance of any single task or sample in policy updates; demonstrated on Omnisapiens-7B 2.0 with strong multitask and held-out performance gains and robust reasoning traces.
AI.motivation: Current approaches either model behavioral dimensions in isolation or train a single model across tasks but neglect heterogeneity across diverse behavioral data, leading to higher training costs and limited generalization. A method that explicitly balances learning across heterogeneous tasks and samples is needed to improve generalization and efficiency in socially intelligent AI.
AI.method: HARPO modulates advantages in policy optimization to ensure balanced influence from heterogeneous tasks and samples, enabling learning across diverse behavioral data. This approach is used to train Omnisapiens-7B 2.0, a foundation model for social behavior processing.
AI.result: Omnisapiens-7B 2.0 achieves the strongest performance among behavioral foundation models, with gains up to 16.85% on multitask settings and 9.37% on held-out settings, and exhibits more explicit and robust reasoning traces. HARPO also shows the most consistently strong performance across behavioral tasks when compared with recent RL methods.
AI.conclusion: HARPO effectively addresses cross-task and cross-sample heterogeneity in reinforcement learning for social behavior processing, improving generalization and robustness; Omnisapiens-7B 2.0 stands as a strong behavioral foundation model enabled by HARPO.

title: ELROND: Exploring and decomposing intrinsic capabilities of diffusion models
summary: A single text prompt passed to a diffusion model often yields a wide range of visual outputs determined solely by stochastic process, leaving users with no direct control over which specific semantic variations appear in the image. While existing unsupervised methods attempt to analyze these variations via output features, they omit the underlying generative process. In this work, we propose a framework to disentangle these semantic directions directly within the input embedding space. To that end, we collect a set of gradients obtained by backpropagating the differences between stochastic realizations of a fixed prompt that we later decompose into meaningful steering directions with either Principal Components Analysis or Sparse Autoencoder. Our approach yields three key contributions: (1) it isolates interpretable, steerable directions for precise, fine-grained control over a single concept; (2) it effectively mitigates mode collapse in distilled models by reintroducing lost diversity; and (3) it establishes a novel estimator for concept complexity under a specific model, based on the dimensionality of the discovered subspace.

AI.tldr: Disentangles semantic directions in diffusion-model prompts by gradient-based analysis in embedding space, using PCA or sparse autoencoders, enabling precise control and improving diversity; introduces a concept-complexity estimator.
AI.motivation: Single-text prompts yield highly varied outputs driven by stochasticity; there is limited understanding or control over the underlying semantic variations and the generative process.
AI.method: Collect gradients by backpropagating the differences between stochastic realizations of a fixed prompt. Decompose these gradients into steerable directions in the input embedding space using Principal Components Analysis or a Sparse Autoencoder.
AI.result: Isolates interpretable, steerable directions for fine-grained control of a single concept; mitigates mode collapse in distilled models by reintroducing diversity; proposes a novel estimator for concept complexity based on the subspace dimensionality.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables direct embedding-space disentanglement of semantic directions in diffusion models, offering precise control, restored diversity, and a new metric for concept complexity.

title: 3D-Printed Anisotropic Soft Magnetic Coating for Directional Rolling of a Magnetically Actuated Capsule Robot
comment: Submitted for review at IEEE/ASME Advanced Intelligenet Mechatronics Conference (2026)
summary: Capsule robots are promising tools for minimally invasive diagnostics and therapy, with applications from gastrointestinal endoscopy to targeted drug delivery and biopsy sampling. Conventional magnetic capsule robots embed bulky permanent magnets at both ends, reducing the usable cavity by about 10-20 mm and limiting integration of functional modules. We propose a compact, 3D-printed soft capsule robot with a magnetic coating that replaces internal magnets, enabling locomotion via a thin, functional shell while preserving the entire interior cavity as a continuous volume for medical payloads. The compliant silicone-magnetic composite also improves swallowability, even with a slightly larger capsule size. Magnetostatic simulations and experiments confirm that programmed NSSN/SNNS pole distributions provide strong anisotropy and reliable torque generation, enabling stable bidirectional rolling, omnidirectional steering, climbing on 7.5 degree inclines, and traversal of 5 mm protrusions. Rolling motion is sustained when the magnetic field at the capsule reaches at least 0.3 mT, corresponding to an effective actuation depth of 30 mm in our setup. Future work will optimize material composition, coating thickness, and magnetic layouts to enhance force output and durability, while next-generation robotic-arm-based field generators with closed-loop feedback will address nonlinearities and expand maneuverability. Together, these advances aim to transition coating-based capsule robots toward reliable clinical deployment and broaden their applications in minimally invasive diagnostics and therapy.

AI.tldr: Coated soft capsule robot with magnetic shell replaces internal magnets, preserving interior cavity for payloads while enabling robust, bidirectional locomotion; demonstrates 0.3 mT actuation and 30 mm effective depth, with agile steering, incline climbing, and protrusion traversal; future work on materials and closed-loop control for clinical deployment.
AI.motivation: Address size and integration limitations of magnet-equipped capsules by eliminating bulky end magnets and maintaining interior volume for payloads, while preserving locomotion and swallowability.
AI.method: 3D-printed silicone capsule with magnetic coating forming NSSN/SNNS pole patterns on a thin shell; magnetostatic simulations and experiments verify anisotropy and torque; full-body actuation vs internal magnets.
AI.result: Stable bidirectional rolling, omnidirectional steering, climbing 7.5° inclines, traversal of 5 mm protrusions; actuation threshold 0.3 mT; effective actuation depth ~30 mm.
AI.conclusion: Coating-based capsules show promise for clinical deployment; future work to optimize materials, coating thickness, magnetic layouts, and implement closed-loop, robotic-arm field generators to expand maneuverability and address nonlinearities.

title: XSPLAIN: XAI-enabling Splat-based Prototype Learning for Attribute-aware INterpretability
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has rapidly become a standard for high-fidelity 3D reconstruction, yet its adoption in multiple critical domains is hindered by the lack of interpretability of the generation models as well as classification of the Splats. While explainability methods exist for other 3D representations, like point clouds, they typically rely on ambiguous saliency maps that fail to capture the volumetric coherence of Gaussian primitives. We introduce XSPLAIN, the first ante-hoc, prototype-based interpretability framework designed specifically for 3DGS classification. Our approach leverages a voxel-aggregated PointNet backbone and a novel, invertible orthogonal transformation that disentangles feature channels for interpretability while strictly preserving the original decision boundaries. Explanations are grounded in representative training examples, enabling intuitive ``this looks like that'' reasoning without any degradation in classification performance. A rigorous user study (N=51) demonstrates a decisive preference for our approach: participants selected XSPLAIN explanations 48.4\% of the time as the best, significantly outperforming baselines $(p<0.001)$, showing that XSPLAIN provides transparency and user trust. The source code for this work is available at: https://github.com/Solvro/ml-splat-xai

AI.tldr: Introduces XSPLAIN, an ante-hoc, prototype-based interpretability framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) classification that preserves accuracy while providing human-friendly explanations grounded in representative training examples.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of interpretable, volumetric explanations for 3D Gaussian splats and the need for explainability in critical domains without degrading classification performance.
AI.method: Proposes an interpretable pipeline built on a voxel-aggregated PointNet backbone. Uses a novel invertible orthogonal transformation to disentangle feature channels for interpretability while preserving decision boundaries. Explanations are prototype-based and grounded in representative training examples. Evaluation includes a user study (N=51) and a code release.
AI.result: XSPLAIN explanations are preferred by users (selected as best 48.4% of the time, p<0.001) with no degradation in classifier performance. Demonstrates improved transparency and user trust in 3DGS classification.
AI.conclusion: First ante-hoc, prototype-based interpretability framework specifically designed for 3DGS classification. Provides human-understandable explanations rooted in training prototypes without sacrificing accuracy; supported by user study and code availability.

title: Spend Search Where It Pays: Value-Guided Structured Sampling and Optimization for Generative Recommendation
summary: Generative recommendation via autoregressive models has unified retrieval and ranking into a single conditional generation framework. However, fine-tuning these models with Reinforcement Learning (RL) often suffers from a fundamental probability-reward mismatch. Conventional likelihood-dominated decoding (e.g., beam search) exhibits a myopic bias toward locally probable prefixes, which causes two critical failures: (1) insufficient exploration, where high-reward items in low-probability branches are prematurely pruned and rarely sampled, and (2) advantage compression, where trajectories sharing high-probability prefixes receive highly correlated rewards with low within-group variance, yielding a weak comparative signal for RL. To address these challenges, we propose V-STAR, a Value-guided Sampling and Tree-structured Advantage Reinforcement framework. V-STAR forms a self-evolving loop via two synergistic components. First, a Value-Guided Efficient Decoding (VED) is developed to identify decisive nodes and selectively deepen high-potential prefixes. This improves exploration efficiency without exhaustive tree search. Second, we propose Sibling-GRPO, which exploits the induced tree topology to compute sibling-relative advantages and concentrates learning signals on decisive branching decisions. Extensive experiments on both offline and online datasets demonstrate that V-STAR outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, delivering superior accuracy and candidate-set diversity under strict latency constraints.

AI.tldr: V-STAR proposes a value-guided sampling framework with tree-structured reinforcement learning for generative recommender systems to address probability-reward mismatch in RL fine-tuning. It introduces Value-Guided Efficient Decoding (VED) and Sibling-GRPO to improve exploration and learning signals, achieving better accuracy and diversity under latency constraints.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning fine-tuning of autoregressive generative recommender systems suffers from probability-reward mismatch and myopic decoding (beam search). This leads to insufficient exploration of high-reward but low-probability items and compressed advantages across similar trajectories.
AI.method: Introduce V-STAR with two components: (1) Value-Guided Efficient Decoding (VED) which identifies decisive nodes and selectively deepens high-potential prefixes to balance exploration with efficiency; (2) Sibling-GRPO, which leverages the tree structure to compute sibling-relative advantages, focusing learning on decisive branching decisions within the generation tree.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on offline and online datasets shows that V-STAR outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, delivering higher accuracy and greater candidate-set diversity while satisfying strict latency constraints.
AI.conclusion: V-STAR effectively mitigates probability-reward mismatch in RL-fine-tuned generative recommender models by coupling value-guided decoding with tree-structured advantage learning, yielding improved accuracy and diversity without excessive computation.

title: Temper-Then-Tilt: Principled Unlearning for Generative Models through Tempering and Classifier Guidance
summary: We study machine unlearning in large generative models by framing the task as density ratio estimation to a target distribution rather than supervised fine-tuning. While classifier guidance is a standard approach for approximating this ratio and can succeed in general, we show it can fail to faithfully unlearn with finite samples when the forget set represents a sharp, concentrated data distribution. To address this, we introduce Temper-Then-Tilt Unlearning (T3-Unlearning), which freezes the base model and applies a two-step inference procedure: (i) tempering the base distribution to flatten high-confidence spikes, and (ii) tilting the tempered distribution using a lightweight classifier trained to distinguish retain from forget samples. Our theoretical analysis provides finite-sample guarantees linking the surrogate classifier's risk to unlearning error, proving that tempering is necessary to successfully unlearn for concentrated distributions. Empirical evaluations on the TOFU benchmark show that T3-Unlearning improves forget quality and generative utility over existing baselines, while training only a fraction of the parameters with a minimal runtime.

AI.tldr: Introduces T3-Unlearning for large generative models, a temper-then-tilt method that freezes the base model and uses a two-stage inference to unlearn concentrated data; provides finite-sample guarantees and empirical improvements on TOFU.
AI.motivation: When the forget set represents a sharp, concentrated data distribution, standard density-ratio estimation via classifier guidance can fail to unlearn with finite samples; a robust, sample-efficient approach with theoretical guarantees is needed.
AI.method: Freeze the base model and perform two-step inference: (i) temper the base distribution to flatten high-confidence spikes, and (ii) tilt the tempered distribution using a lightweight classifier trained to distinguish retain from forget samples; derive finite-sample guarantees linking the surrogate classifier's risk to unlearning error.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on the TOFU benchmark shows improved forget quality and generative utility over baselines, while training only a fraction of parameters and with minimal runtime.
AI.conclusion: Tempering is necessary for successful unlearning of concentrated distributions; T3-Unlearning is effective and efficient in practice.

title: A Unified Experimental Architecture for Informative Path Planning: from Simulation to Deployment with GuadalPlanner
summary: The evaluation of informative path planning algorithms for autonomous vehicles is often hindered by fragmented execution pipelines and limited transferability between simulation and real-world deployment. This paper introduces a unified architecture that decouples high-level decision-making from vehicle-specific control, enabling algorithms to be evaluated consistently across different abstraction levels without modification. The proposed architecture is realized through GuadalPlanner, which defines standardized interfaces between planning, sensing, and vehicle execution. It is an open and extensible research tool that supports discrete graph-based environments and interchangeable planning strategies, and is built upon widely adopted robotics technologies, including ROS2, MAVLink, and MQTT. Its design allows the same algorithmic logic to be deployed in fully simulated environments, software-in-the-loop configurations, and physical autonomous vehicles using an identical execution pipeline. The approach is validated through a set of experiments, including real-world deployment on an autonomous surface vehicle performing water quality monitoring with real-time sensor feedback.

AI.tldr: Unified GuadalPlanner architecture decouples high-level planning from vehicle control, enabling consistent evaluation across simulation and real-world deployment via standardized interfaces.
AI.motivation: Fragmented evaluation pipelines and limited transferability between simulation and real-world deployment hinder rigorous assessment of informative path planning algorithms; a unified, decoupled architecture facilitates consistent evaluation across abstraction levels.
AI.method: Introduce GuadalPlanner with standardized interfaces among planning, sensing, and vehicle execution; supports discrete graph-based environments; interchangeable planning strategies; open and extensible; built on ROS2, MAVLink, and MQTT; enables the same algorithmic logic to run in fully simulated, software‑in‑the‑loop, and physical vehicles with an identical execution pipeline.
AI.result: Validation through experiments, including real-world deployment on an autonomous surface vehicle performing water quality monitoring with real-time sensor feedback; demonstrates consistent evaluation across abstraction levels and practical viability.
AI.conclusion: A unified, decoupled architecture improves transferability and comparability of informative path planning algorithms; GuadalPlanner provides a practical, extensible platform enabling cross-domain deployment of planning logic across simulation and hardware.

title: PMMA: The Polytechnique Montreal Mobility Aids Dataset
comment: Submitted to the journal IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, under review
summary: This study introduces a new object detection dataset of pedestrians using mobility aids, named PMMA. The dataset was collected in an outdoor environment, where volunteers used wheelchairs, canes, and walkers, resulting in nine categories of pedestrians: pedestrians, cane users, two types of walker users, whether walking or resting, five types of wheelchair users, including wheelchair users, people pushing empty wheelchairs, and three types of users pushing occupied wheelchairs, including the entire pushing group, the pusher and the person seated on the wheelchair. To establish a benchmark, seven object detection models (Faster R-CNN, CenterNet, YOLOX, DETR, Deformable DETR, DINO, and RT-DETR) and three tracking algorithms (ByteTrack, BOT-SORT, and OC-SORT) were implemented under the MMDetection framework. Experimental results show that YOLOX, Deformable DETR, and Faster R-CNN achieve the best detection performance, while the differences among the three trackers are relatively small. The PMMA dataset is publicly available at https://doi.org/10.5683/SP3/XJPQUG, and the video processing and model training code is available at https://github.com/DatasetPMMA/PMMA.

AI.tldr: New outdoor PMMA dataset for pedestrians with mobility aids (9 fine-grained classes); benchmarked with 7 detectors and 3 trackers in MMDetection; YOLOX, Deformable DETR, and Faster R-CNN perform best in detection; trackers show similar performance; dataset and code are publicly available.
AI.motivation: Address safety-critical recognition of pedestrians using mobility aids, a relatively underrepresented group in existing benchmarks, to improve detection and tracking in urban environments.
AI.method: Collect outdoor video data with volunteers using wheelchairs, canes, and walkers; define nine pedestrian categories (including various wheelchair and pusher/occupied configurations); annotate and benchmark seven detectors (Faster R-CNN, CenterNet, YOLOX, DETR, Deformable DETR, DINO, RT-DETR) and three trackers (ByteTrack, BOT-SORT, OC-SORT) within the MMDetection framework; report comparative performance and provide access to dataset and code.
AI.result: Best detection performance achieved by YOLOX, Deformable DETR, and Faster R-CNN; three trackers yield relatively small performance gaps; PMMA dataset publicly accessible; video processing and training code available.
AI.conclusion: PMMA offers a comprehensive, fine-grained benchmark for pedestrian detection/tracking involving mobility aids, enabling research on safety-critical scenarios and real-world deployment; potential future work includes addressing class imbalance, improving cross-domain generalization, and exploring deployment considerations.

title: Integrating Generative AI-enhanced Cognitive Systems in Higher Education: From Stakeholder Perceptions to a Conceptual Framework considering the EU AI Act
summary: Many staff and students in higher education have adopted generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) tools in their work and study. GenAI is expected to enhance cognitive systems by enabling personalized learning and streamlining educational services. However, stakeholders perceptions of GenAI in higher education remain divided, shaped by cultural, disciplinary, and institutional contexts. In addition, the EU AI Act requires universities to ensure regulatory compliance when deploying cognitive systems. These developments highlight the need for institutions to engage stakeholders and tailor GenAI integration to their needs while addressing concerns. This study investigates how GenAI is perceived within the disciplines of Information Technology and Electrical Engineering (ITEE). Using a mixed-method approach, we surveyed 61 staff and 37 students at the Faculty of ITEE, University of Oulu. The results reveal both shared and discipline-specific themes, including strong interest in programming support from GenAI and concerns over response quality, privacy, and academic integrity. Drawing from these insights, the study identifies a set of high-level requirements and proposes a conceptual framework for responsible GenAI integration. Disciplinary-specific requirements reinforce the importance of stakeholder engagement when integrating GenAI into higher education. The high-level requirements and the framework provide practical guidance for universities aiming to harness GenAI while addressing stakeholder concerns and ensuring regulatory compliance.

AI.tldr: The study analyzes perceptions of GenAI in IT&E disciplines at a Finnish university using a mixed-method survey (61 staff, 37 students). It finds shared and discipline-specific themes, notably programming-support interest and concerns about response quality, privacy, and academic integrity, and proposes high-level requirements and a conceptual framework for responsible GenAI integration with emphasis on stakeholder engagement and regulatory compliance.
AI.motivation: Growing GenAI adoption in higher education and the EU AI Act create a need to understand stakeholder perspectives across disciplines and to guide responsible, compliant implementation.
AI.method: Mixed-method design based on a survey of 61 staff and 37 students in the Faculty of Information Technology and Electrical Engineering at the University of Oulu; qualitative/theme-based analysis to identify shared and disciplinary themes; development of high-level requirements and a conceptual framework.
AI.result: Identifies both shared and discipline-specific themes, including strong interest in programming support from GenAI and concerns about response quality, privacy, and academic integrity. The findings inform a set of high-level requirements and a proposed conceptual framework for responsible GenAI integration.
AI.conclusion: High-level requirements and the conceptual framework offer practical guidance for universities to harness GenAI while addressing stakeholder concerns and ensuring regulatory compliance; disciplinary contexts intensify the need for stakeholder engagement in responsible implementation.

title: Internalizing Meta-Experience into Memory for Guided Reinforcement Learning in Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as an effective approach for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). Despite its efficacy, RLVR faces a meta-learning bottleneck: it lacks mechanisms for error attribution and experience internalization intrinsic to the human learning cycle beyond practice and verification, thereby limiting fine-grained credit assignment and reusable knowledge formation. We term such reusable knowledge representations derived from past errors as meta-experience. Based on this insight, we propose Meta-Experience Learning (MEL), a novel framework that incorporates self-distilled meta-experience into the model's parametric memory. Building upon standard RLVR, we introduce an additional design that leverages the LLM's self-verification capability to conduct contrastive analysis on paired correct and incorrect trajectories, identify the precise bifurcation points where reasoning errors arise, and summarize them into generalizable meta-experience. The meta-experience is further internalized into the LLM's parametric memory by minimizing the negative log-likelihood, which induces a language-modeled reward signal that bridges correct and incorrect reasoning trajectories and facilitates effective knowledge reuse. Experimental results demonstrate that MEL achieves consistent improvements on benchmarks, yielding 3.92%--4.73% Pass@1 gains across varying model sizes.

AI.tldr: Meta-Experience Learning (MEL) extends RLVR by learning reusable 'meta-experiences' from error bifurcations, internalizing them into a parametric memory. It uses the LLM's self-verification to contrast correct/incorrect trajectories and summarizes bifurcation points into meta-experience, which is optimized via negative log-likelihood to produce a language-model reward signal that guides knowledge reuse. Empirically, MEL achieves consistent Pass@1 gains across model sizes.
AI.motivation: To overcome the meta-learning bottleneck in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) by enabling explicit error attribution and reusable knowledge formation, akin to human learning cycles.
AI.method: Extend RLVR with self-distilled meta-experience (MEL): (1) perform contrastive analysis on paired correct/incorrect trajectories using self-verification; (2) identify bifurcation points where reasoning errors arise; (3) summarize these into generalizable meta-experience; (4) internalize meta-experience into the model's parametric memory by minimizing negative log-likelihood, effectively creating a language-model-based reward signal that links correct and incorrect reasoning.
AI.result: MEL yields consistent improvements on benchmarks, with Pass@1 gains in the range of 3.92%–4.73% across varying model sizes.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating meta-experience into RLVR through self-distillation and memory integration enables finer-grained credit assignment and reusable knowledge, leading to robust performance gains.

title: Omnidirectional Dual-Arm Aerial Manipulator with Proprioceptive Contact Localization for Landing on Slanted Roofs
comment: Accepted into 2026 International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) in Vienna
summary: Operating drones in urban environments often means they need to land on rooftops, which can have different geometries and surface irregularities. Accurately detecting roof inclination using conventional sensing methods, such as vision-based or acoustic techniques, can be unreliable, as measurement quality is strongly influenced by external factors including weather conditions and surface materials. To overcome these challenges, we propose a novel unmanned aerial manipulator morphology featuring a dual-arm aerial manipulator with an omnidirectional 3D workspace and extended reach. Building on this design, we develop a proprioceptive contact detection and contact localization strategy based on a momentum-based torque observer. This enables the UAM to infer the inclination of slanted surfaces blindly - through physical interaction - prior to touchdown. We validate the approach in flight experiments, demonstrating robust landings on surfaces with inclinations of up to 30.5 degrees and achieving an average surface inclination estimation error of 2.87 degrees over 9 experiments at different incline angles.

AI.tldr: A dual-arm unmanned aerial manipulator (UAM) with an omnidirectional 3D workspace and extended reach enables blind surface inclination estimation via a momentum-based torque observer for proprioceptive contact detection, improving landings on sloped rooftops.
AI.motivation: Urban rooftop landings require reliable surface inclination assessment. Conventional sensing (vision, acoustics) is unreliable due to weather and material variability. A proprioceptive, contact-based strategy can infer inclination before touchdown to enhance landing robustness.
AI.method: Introduce a dual-arm UAM morphology with an extended reach and omnidirectional workspace. Develop a momentum-based torque observer to detect and localize contact proprioceptively, allowing inference of roof inclination prior to touchdown.
AI.result: Flight experiments show robust landings on surfaces up to 30.5° incline and an average inclination estimation error of 2.87° across 9 experiments at different angles.
AI.conclusion: The proposed contact-based perception enables reliable touchdown on sloped surfaces, potentially improving urban UAM operations by reducing dependence on external sensing and enabling safer, more robust landings.

title: Colorimeter-Supervised Skin Tone Estimation from Dermatoscopic Images for Fairness Auditing
comment: Preprint submitted to Computer Methods and Programs in Biomedicine
summary: Neural-network-based diagnosis from dermatoscopic images is increasingly used for clinical decision support, yet studies report performance disparities across skin tones. Fairness auditing of these models is limited by the lack of reliable skin-tone annotations in public dermatoscopy datasets. We address this gap with neural networks that predict Fitzpatrick skin type via ordinal regression and the Individual Typology Angle (ITA) via color regression, using in-person Fitzpatrick labels and colorimeter measurements as targets. We further leverage extensive pretraining on synthetic and real dermatoscopic and clinical images. The Fitzpatrick model achieves agreement comparable to human crowdsourced annotations, and ITA predictions show high concordance with colorimeter-derived ITA, substantially outperforming pixel-averaging approaches. Applying these estimators to ISIC 2020 and MILK10k, we find that fewer than 1% of subjects belong to Fitzpatrick types V and VI. We release code and pretrained models as an open-source tool for rapid skin-tone annotation and bias auditing. This is, to our knowledge, the first dermatoscopic skin-tone estimation neural network validated against colorimeter measurements, and it supports growing evidence of clinically relevant performance gaps across skin-tone groups.

AI.tldr: Neural networks estimate Fitzpatrick skin type (ordinal regression) and ITA (color regression) from dermatoscopic images to enable fairness auditing, validated against colorimeter and crowdsourced labels; shows underrepresentation of darker skin types and provides open-source tools.
AI.motivation: Fill the lack of reliable skin-tone annotations in public dermatoscopic datasets to assess and mitigate performance disparities across skin tones in AI dermatology.
AI.method: Train models using in-person Fitzpatrick labels and colorimeterITAs as targets, cast Fitzpatrick as ordinal regression, ITA as color regression; extensive pretraining on synthetic/real dermatoscopic and clinical images; evaluate on ISIC 2020 and MILK10k datasets; release code and pretrained models.
AI.result: Fitzpatrick predictions align with human crowdsourced annotations; ITA predictions align with colorimeter ITA, outperforming pixel-averaging baselines; estimate that <1% of subjects are Fitzpatrick types V/VI in ISIC 2020 and MILK10k; open-source tools for skin-tone annotation and bias auditing.
AI.conclusion: First dermatoscopic skin-tone estimation NN validated against colorimeter measurements; supports evidence of clinically relevant performance gaps across skin-tone groups; enables rapid, scalable fairness auditing in dermatoscopic AI.

title: See, Plan, Snap: Evaluating Multimodal GUI Agents in Scratch
summary: Block-based programming environments such as Scratch play a central role in low-code education, yet evaluating the capabilities of AI agents to construct programs through Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) remains underexplored. We introduce ScratchWorld, a benchmark for evaluating multimodal GUI agents on program-by-construction tasks in Scratch. Grounded in the Use-Modify-Create pedagogical framework, ScratchWorld comprises 83 curated tasks spanning four distinct problem categories: Create, Debug, Extend, and Compute. To rigorously diagnose the source of agent failures, the benchmark employs two complementary interaction modes: primitive mode requires fine-grained drag-and-drop manipulation to directly assess visuomotor control, while composite mode uses high-level semantic APIs to disentangle program reasoning from GUI execution. To ensure reliable assessment, we propose an execution-based evaluation protocol that validates the functional correctness of the constructed Scratch programs through runtime tests within the browser environment. Extensive experiments across state-of-the-art multimodal language models and GUI agents reveal a substantial reasoning--acting gap, highlighting persistent challenges in fine-grained GUI manipulation despite strong planning capabilities.

AI.tldr: Introduces ScratchWorld, a multimodal GUI benchmark for Scratch program-by-construction tasks with two interaction modes and execution-based evaluation, revealing a gap between reasoning and acting in current AI agents.
AI.motivation: To evaluate AI agents' ability to construct programs via GUIs and bridge the gap between planning and visuomotor execution in low-code education.
AI.method: 83 tasks across four categories (Create, Debug, Extend, Compute); two interaction modes: primitive (drag-and-drop) for visuomotor control and composite (high-level APIs) for separating program reasoning from GUI execution; execution-based browser tests for functional correctness; extensive experiments with state-of-the-art multimodal models and GUI agents.
AI.result: Identifies a substantial reasoning–acting gap: strong planning yet persistent challenges in fine-grained GUI manipulation.
AI.conclusion: ScratchWorld provides a rigorous benchmark to diagnose and drive progress in multimodal GUI agents for program-by-construction tasks and can guide future tool design and evaluation protocols.

title: Self-Evolving Recommendation System: End-To-End Autonomous Model Optimization With LLM Agents
summary: Optimizing large-scale machine learning systems, such as recommendation models for global video platforms, requires navigating a massive hyperparameter search space and, more critically, designing sophisticated optimizers, architectures, and reward functions to capture nuanced user behaviors. Achieving substantial improvements in these areas is a non-trivial task, traditionally relying on extensive manual iterations to test new hypotheses. We propose a self-evolving system that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs), specifically those from Google's Gemini family, to autonomously generate, train, and deploy high-performing, complex model changes within an end-to-end automated workflow. The self-evolving system is comprised of an Offline Agent (Inner Loop) that performs high-throughput hypothesis generation using proxy metrics, and an Online Agent (Outer Loop) that validates candidates against delayed north star business metrics in live production. Our agents act as specialized Machine Learning Engineers (MLEs): they exhibit deep reasoning capabilities, discovering novel improvements in optimization algorithms and model architecture, and formulating innovative reward functions that target long-term user engagement. The effectiveness of this approach is demonstrated through several successful production launches at YouTube, confirming that autonomous, LLM-driven evolution can surpass traditional engineering workflows in both development velocity and model performance.

AI.tldr: Autonomous, LLM-driven self-evolving system for large-scale ML optimization, combining Offline (Inner Loop) hypothesis generation with proxy metrics and Online (Outer Loop) live validation, demonstrated by production launches at YouTube, outperforming traditional workflows in velocity and performance.
AI.motivation: Optimizing large-scale ML systems (e.g., recommender models) involves enormous hyperparameter spaces and complex design decisions. Manual iteration is slow; an automated, intelligent framework is needed to generate, train, and deploy high-performing model changes.
AI.method: Utilize LLMs (Gemini family) as specialized ML Engineers (MLEs) in a two-loop setup: Offline Agent (Inner Loop) performs high-throughput hypothesis generation using proxy metrics; Online Agent (Outer Loop) validates candidates against delayed north-star business metrics in live production; end-to-end automated workflow leading to deployment decisions.
AI.result: Multiple successful production launches at YouTube, showing that autonomous, LLM-driven evolution can surpass traditional engineering workflows in development velocity and model performance.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous, LLM-driven evolution is a viable approach for accelerating optimization and deployment of complex ML systems and can reduce manual engineering effort, with demonstrated production-level efficacy.

title: Say, Dream, and Act: Learning Video World Models for Instruction-Driven Robot Manipulation
summary: Robotic manipulation requires anticipating how the environment evolves in response to actions, yet most existing systems lack this predictive capability, often resulting in errors and inefficiency. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) provide high-level guidance, they cannot explicitly forecast future states, and existing world models either predict only short horizons or produce spatially inconsistent frames. To address these challenges, we propose a framework for fast and predictive video-conditioned action. Our approach first selects and adapts a robust video generation model to ensure reliable future predictions, then applies adversarial distillation for fast, few-step video generation, and finally trains an action model that leverages both generated videos and real observations to correct spatial errors. Extensive experiments show that our method produces temporally coherent, spatially accurate video predictions that directly support precise manipulation, achieving significant improvements in embodiment consistency, spatial referring ability, and task completion over existing baselines. Codes & Models will be released.

AI.tldr: A framework for fast, predictive video-conditioned manipulation that integrates robust video generation, adversarial distillation for speed, and an action model that fuses generated and real observations to correct spatial errors, yielding temporally coherent and spatially accurate predictions that improve embodiment, spatial referencing, and task success.
AI.motivation: Current robotic prediction systems struggle to forecast environment dynamics; VLMs guide at a high level but lack explicit future state forecasts, while world models are often short-horizon or spatially inconsistent. A predictive video-conditioned approach is needed to reduce errors and improve manipulation efficiency.
AI.method: 1) Select/adapt a robust video generation model to produce reliable future predictions. 2) Apply adversarial distillation to enable fast, few-step video generation. 3) Train an action model that uses both generated videos and real observations to correct spatial errors during manipulation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows temporally coherent, spatially accurate video predictions that support precise manipulation, with significant improvements in embodiment consistency, spatial referring ability, and task completion over baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables fast, predictive video-conditioned actions for robotic manipulation and will release codes and models, indicating strong potential for improving real-time manipulation reliability.

title: ERGO: Excess-Risk-Guided Optimization for High-Fidelity Monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Generating 3D content from a single image remains a fundamentally challenging and ill-posed problem due to the inherent absence of geometric and textural information in occluded regions. While state-of-the-art generative models can synthesize auxiliary views to provide additional supervision, these views inevitably contain geometric inconsistencies and textural misalignments that propagate and amplify artifacts during 3D reconstruction. To effectively harness these imperfect supervisory signals, we propose an adaptive optimization framework guided by excess risk decomposition, termed ERGO. Specifically, ERGO decomposes the optimization losses in 3D Gaussian splatting into two components, i.e., excess risk that quantifies the suboptimality gap between current and optimal parameters, and Bayes error that models the irreducible noise inherent in synthesized views. This decomposition enables ERGO to dynamically estimate the view-specific excess risk and adaptively adjust loss weights during optimization. Furthermore, we introduce geometry-aware and texture-aware objectives that complement the excess-risk-derived weighting mechanism, establishing a synergistic global-local optimization paradigm. Consequently, ERGO demonstrates robustness against supervision noise while consistently enhancing both geometric fidelity and textural quality of the reconstructed 3D content. Extensive experiments on the Google Scanned Objects dataset and the OmniObject3D dataset demonstrate the superiority of ERGO over existing state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: ERGO: an adaptive optimization framework for 3D reconstruction from a single image that uses excess-risk decomposition to weight supervision signals from imperfect synthesized views; includes geometry-aware and texture-aware objectives; shows robust improvements in geometry and texture on Google Scanned Objects and OmniObject3D.
AI.motivation: Single-view 3D reconstruction is ill-posed due to lack of geometry and texture in occluded regions. Generated auxiliary views provide supervision but introduce geometric/textural inconsistencies that propagate into 3D reconstructions. A principled weighting mechanism for noisy supervision signals is needed.
AI.method: Decompose 3D Gaussian splatting losses into excess risk (suboptimality gap) and Bayes error (irreducible noise). Dynamically estimate view-specific excess risk and adapt loss weights during optimization. Introduce geometry-aware and texture-aware objectives to complement the weighting, forming a global-local optimization framework.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Google Scanned Objects and OmniObject3D datasets show ERGO outperforms state-of-the-art methods, improving both geometric fidelity and textural quality.
AI.conclusion: ERGO provides robustness to supervision noise and enhances 3D reconstruction quality by combining excess-risk guided weighting with geometry- and texture-aware objectives in a local-global optimization scheme.

title: SynergyKGC: Reconciling Topological Heterogeneity in Knowledge Graph Completion via Topology-Aware Synergy
comment: 10 pages, 5 tables, 7 figures. This work introduces the Active Synergy mechanism and Identity Anchoring for Knowledge Graph Completion. Code: https://github.com/XuechengZou-2001/SynergyKGC-main
summary: Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) fundamentally hinges on the coherent fusion of pre-trained entity semantics with heterogeneous topological structures to facilitate robust relational reasoning. However, existing paradigms encounter a critical "structural resolution mismatch," failing to reconcile divergent representational demands across varying graph densities, which precipitates structural noise interference in dense clusters and catastrophic representation collapse in sparse regions. We present SynergyKGC, an adaptive framework that advances traditional neighbor aggregation to an active Cross-Modal Synergy Expert via relation-aware cross-attention and semantic-intent-driven gating. By coupling a density-dependent Identity Anchoring strategy with a Double-tower Coherent Consistency architecture, SynergyKGC effectively reconciles topological heterogeneity while ensuring representational stability across training and inference phases. Systematic evaluations on two public benchmarks validate the superiority of our method in significantly boosting KGC hit rates, providing empirical evidence for a generalized principle of resilient information integration in non-homogeneous structured data.

AI.tldr: SynergyKGC introduces an adaptive cross-modal framework for knowledge graph completion that reconciles topological heterogeneity via a density-aware identity anchoring and a dual-tower architecture with cross-modal synergy, achieving improved hit rates on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Knowledge Graph Completion struggles with a structural resolution mismatch: dense regions inject structural noise while sparse regions cause representation collapse. A unified, stable integration of pre-trained entity semantics with heterogeneous topology is needed.
AI.method: A Cross-Modal Synergy Expert with relation-aware cross-attention and semantic-intent-driven gating, augmented by a density-dependent Identity Anchoring strategy and a Double-tower Coherent Consistency architecture to reconcile topological heterogeneity and stabilize representations.
AI.result: Systematic evaluations on two public benchmarks show significant improvements in KGC hit rates, supporting the idea of resilient information integration in non-homogeneous structured data.
AI.conclusion: SynergyKGC reconciles topological heterogeneity and maintains representational stability across training and inference, suggesting a general principle for robust integration of semantic and structural signals in non-uniform graphs.

title: From Representational Complementarity to Dual Systems: Synergizing VLM and Vision-Only Backbones for End-to-End Driving
comment: 22 pages (10 pages main text + 12 pages appendix), 18 figures
