summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) driving augments end-to-end (E2E) planning with language-enabled backbones, yet it remains unclear what changes beyond the usual accuracy--cost trade-off. We revisit this question with 3--RQ analysis in RecogDrive by instantiating the system with a full VLM and vision-only backbones, all under an identical diffusion Transformer planner. RQ1: At the backbone level, the VLM can introduce additional subspaces upon the vision-only backbones. RQ2: This unique subspace leads to a different behavioral in some long-tail scenario: the VLM tends to be more aggressive whereas ViT is more conservative, and each decisively wins on about 2--3% of test scenarios; With an oracle that selects, per scenario, the better trajectory between the VLM and ViT branches, we obtain an upper bound of 93.58 PDMS. RQ3: To fully harness this observation, we propose HybridDriveVLA, which runs both ViT and VLM branches and selects between their endpoint trajectories using a learned scorer, improving PDMS to 92.10. Finally, DualDriveVLA implements a practical fast--slow policy: it runs ViT by default and invokes the VLM only when the scorer's confidence falls below a threshold; calling the VLM on 15% of scenarios achieves 91.00 PDMS while improving throughput by 3.2x. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: Comparative analysis of Vision-Language Models (VLM) vs vision-only backbones in Vision-Language-Action driving, under a shared diffusion-based planner, revealing a distinct behavioral subspace and long-tail differences; introduces HybridDriveVLA and DualDriveVLA to leverage complementary strengths, achieving notable PDMS gains and improved throughput.
AI.motivation: To understand how incorporating a VLM backbone changes dynamics beyond standard accuracy-cost trade-offs, and to exploit any complementary strengths via hybrid architectures for improved driving performance.
AI.method: Instantiate the driving system with both full VLM and vision-only backbones using an identical diffusion Transformer planner. Conduct 3 research-question (RQ) analyses: (RQ1) backbone-level subspaces; (RQ2) long-tail behavioral differences and per-scenario wins; (RQ3) design and evaluate hybrid architectures (HybridDriveVLA, DualDriveVLA) and learned scorers. Compare to an oracle upper bound and report PDMS metrics. Code will be released.
AI.result: RQ1: The VLM introduces additional subspaces beyond vision-only backbones. RQ2: The subspace yields different behaviors in some long-tail cases (VLM more aggressive, ViT more conservative); each wins on about 2–3% of test scenarios; with an oracle selecting the better trajectory per scenario, upper bound PDMS is 93.58. RQ3: HybridDriveVLA runs both branches and uses a learned scorer to pick endpoint trajectories, improving PDMS to 92.10. DualDriveVLA implements a fast-slow policy: ViT by default, VLM invoked when scorer confidence is low; with VLM called on 15% of scenarios, PDMS is 91.00 and throughput improves by 3.2x.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating a VLM backbone creates a complementary subspace that, when coupled with a learned selection mechanism, can enhance driving performance. Hybrid and fast-slow architectures effectively balance accuracy and efficiency, and code release will enable reproducibility.

title: A Low-Rank Defense Method for Adversarial Attack on Diffusion Models
summary: Recently, adversarial attacks for diffusion models as well as their fine-tuning process have been developed rapidly. To prevent the abuse of these attack algorithms from affecting the practical application of diffusion models, it is critical to develop corresponding defensive strategies. In this work, we propose an efficient defensive strategy, named Low-Rank Defense (LoRD), to defend the adversarial attack on Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs). LoRD introduces the merging idea and a balance parameter, combined with the low-rank adaptation (LoRA) modules, to detect and defend the adversarial samples. Based on LoRD, we build up a defense pipeline that applies the learned LoRD modules to help diffusion models defend against attack algorithms. Our method ensures that the LDM fine-tuned on both adversarial and clean samples can still generate high-quality images. To demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, we conduct extensive experiments on facial and landscape images, and our method shows significantly better defense performance compared to the baseline methods.

AI.tldr: A defense framework LoRD using low-rank adaptation to defend Latent Diffusion Models against adversarial attacks, by merging LoRA modules with a balance parameter to detect/mitigate adversarial samples, preserving high-quality generation.
AI.motivation: Adversarial attacks on diffusion models and their fine-tuning pose practical risks; existing defenses may degrade image quality or be inefficient; there is a need for an effective, lightweight defense for LDMs that maintains generation quality.
AI.method: Introduce Low-Rank Defense (LoRD) that merges LoRA modules with a balance parameter to detect and defend adversarial samples, integrated into Latent Diffusion Models. Train the LDM on a mixture of adversarial and clean samples and apply a defense pipeline that uses the learned LoRD modules to mitigate attacks while preserving generation quality.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on facial and landscape images show that LoRD achieves significantly better defense performance than baseline methods while maintaining high-quality image generation.
AI.conclusion: LoRD provides an efficient, effective defense for LDMs against adversarial manipulation, leveraging low-rank adaptation and a merging mechanism to defend against attacks without sacrificing output quality.

title: Reinforcing Chain-of-Thought Reasoning with Self-Evolving Rubrics
summary: Despite chain-of-thought (CoT) playing crucial roles in LLM reasoning, directly rewarding it is difficult: training a reward model demands heavy human labeling efforts, and static RMs struggle with evolving CoT distributions and reward hacking. These challenges motivate us to seek an autonomous CoT rewarding approach that requires no human annotation efforts and can evolve gradually. Inspired by recent self-evolving training methods, we propose \textbf{RLCER} (\textbf{R}einforcement \textbf{L}earning with \textbf{C}oT Supervision via Self-\textbf{E}volving \textbf{R}ubrics), which enhances the outcome-centric RLVR by rewarding CoTs with self-proposed and self-evolving rubrics. We show that self-proposed and self-evolving rubrics provide reliable CoT supervision signals even without outcome rewards, enabling RLCER to outperform outcome-centric RLVR. Moreover, when used as in-prompt hints, these self-proposed rubrics further improve inference-time performance.

AI.tldr: Introduces RLCER, a self-evolving rubric-based reward mechanism for chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning in RL, enabling autonomous CoT supervision without human labels; it surpasses outcome-centric RLVR and can be used as in-prompt hints.
AI.motivation: Traditional reward models require labeled data and struggle with shifting CoT distributions and reward gaming; an autonomous, self-supervised approach is desired.
AI.method: Proposes RLCER where rubrics are proposed by the model and evolve over time; these rubrics supervise CoTs in outcome-agnostic and outcome-aware ways; integrates into RLVR; rubrics can be injected as in-prompt hints.
AI.result: Empirical evidence shows improvement over outcome-centric RLVR; self-proposed rubrics provide reliable supervision signals without outcome rewards; adding them as in-prompt hints improves inference performance.
AI.conclusion: Self-evolving rubrics enable scalable, annotation-free CoT supervision; enhance robustness to distribution shifts and reward hacking; extendable to in-context hints.

title: Frame-Level Internal Tool Use for Temporal Grounding in Audio LMs
summary: Large audio language models are increasingly used for complex audio understanding tasks, but they struggle with temporal tasks that require precise temporal grounding, such as word alignment and speaker diarization. The standard approach, where we generate timestamps as sequences of text tokens, is computationally expensive and prone to hallucination, especially when processing audio lengths outside the model's training distribution. In this work, we propose frame-level internal tool use, a method that trains audio LMs to use their own internal audio representations to perform temporal grounding directly. We introduce a lightweight prediction mechanism trained via two objectives: a binary frame classifier and a novel inhomogeneous Poisson process (IHP) loss that models temporal event intensity. Across word localization, speaker diarization, and event localization tasks, our approach outperforms token-based baselines. Most notably, it achieves a >50x inference speedup and demonstrates robust length generalization, maintaining high accuracy on out-of-distribution audio durations where standard token-based models collapse completely.

AI.tldr: Frame-level internal tool use for audio LMs enables temporal grounding using internal frame representations, yielding >50x inference speedup and strong length generalization, outperforming token-based baselines on word localization, speaker diarization, and event localization.
AI.motivation: Temporal grounding tasks require precise timing and robust generalization to longer or out-of-distribution audio clips. Token-based timestamp generation is expensive and prone to hallucinations, motivating a more efficient, robust approach.
AI.method: Train a lightweight predictor that uses internal audio frame representations to perform temporal grounding. Use two objectives: (1) a binary frame classifier to detect event-bearing frames, and (2) an inhomogeneous Poisson process (IHP) loss to model the temporal intensity of events. This frame-level mechanism bypasses token-based timestamp generation.
AI.result: Outperforms token-based baselines across word localization, speaker diarization, and event localization. Achieves >50x inference speedup and robust length generalization, maintaining high accuracy on out-of-distribution audio durations where token-based models fail.
AI.conclusion: Frame-level internal tool use is a promising paradigm for efficient and robust temporal grounding in audio LMs, enabling significant speedups and better generalization across varying durations.

title: Biomimetic Mantaray robot toward the underwater autonomous -- Experimental verification of swimming and diving by flapping motion -
summary: This study presents the development and experimental verification of a biomimetic manta ray robot for underwater autonomous exploration. Inspired by manta rays, the robot uses flapping motion for propulsion to minimize seabed disturbance and enhance efficiency compared to traditional screw propulsion. The robot features pectoral fins driven by servo motors and a streamlined control box to reduce fluid resistance. The control system, powered by a Raspberry Pi 3B, includes an IMU and pressure sensor for real-time monitoring and control. Experiments in a pool assessed the robot's swimming and diving capabilities. Results show stable swimming and diving motions with PD control. The robot is suitable for applications in environments like aquariums and fish nurseries, requiring minimal disturbance and efficient maneuverability. Our findings demonstrate the potential of bio-inspired robotic designs to improve ecological monitoring and underwater exploration.

AI.tldr: Bio-inspired manta ray robot with flapping pectoral fins; PD-controlled swimming validated in pool; aims for low-disturbance, efficient underwater exploration.
AI.motivation: Achieve efficient, low-disturbance propulsion for ecological monitoring and underwater exploration, addressing seabed disturbance and energy efficiency limitations of traditional screw propulsion.
AI.method: Design and fabrication of a manta ray–inspired robot with servo-driven pectoral fins and a streamlined control box. A Raspberry Pi 3B-based control system with an IMU and pressure sensor enables real-time monitoring and control. A PD controller governs swimming, with pool experiments evaluating propulsion and diving capabilities.
AI.result: The robot demonstrated stable swimming and diving motions under PD control in pool tests, with reduced fluid disturbance and enhanced maneuverability relative to conventional propellers.
AI.conclusion: Bio-inspired propulsion shows promise for ecological monitoring and underwater exploration by reducing disturbance and improving efficiency; future work could optimize energy use, depth control, and robustness for varied underwater environments.

title: Flow Matching with Uncertainty Quantification and Guidance
summary: Despite the remarkable success of sampling-based generative models such as flow matching, they can still produce samples of inconsistent or degraded quality. To assess sample reliability and generate higher-quality outputs, we propose uncertainty-aware flow matching (UA-Flow), a lightweight extension of flow matching that predicts the velocity field together with heteroscedastic uncertainty. UA-Flow estimates per-sample uncertainty by propagating velocity uncertainty through the flow dynamics. These uncertainty estimates act as a reliability signal for individual samples, and we further use them to steer generation via uncertainty-aware classifier guidance and classifier-free guidance. Experiments on image generation show that UA-Flow produces uncertainty signals more highly correlated with sample fidelity than baseline methods, and that uncertainty-guided sampling further improves generation quality.

AI.tldr: UA-Flow extends flow matching with per-sample heteroscedastic uncertainty in the velocity field, propagates this uncertainty through flow dynamics, and uses it to guide sampling via uncertainty-aware classifier guidance and classifier-free guidance, yielding better sample fidelity and reliability signals.
AI.motivation: Flow matching often produces samples with inconsistent or degraded quality. Per-sample uncertainty can serve as a reliability signal and enable uncertainty-guided generation to boost output quality.
AI.method: Predict velocity field and per-sample heteroscedastic uncertainty; propagate the velocity uncertainty through the flow dynamics to obtain per-sample uncertainty estimates; apply uncertainty-aware classifier guidance and classifier-free guidance to steer generation.
AI.result: Uncertainty signals correlate more strongly with sample fidelity than baseline methods; uncertainty-guided sampling further improves generation quality on image generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware flow matching provides reliable uncertainty signals for samples and improves overall generation quality, with a lightweight extension suitable for integration into existing flow-based generative models.

title: Can LLMs Cook Jamaican Couscous? A Study of Cultural Novelty in Recipe Generation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate and shape cultural content, ranging from narrative writing to artistic production. While these models demonstrate impressive fluency and generative capacity, prior work has shown that they also exhibit systematic cultural biases, raising concerns about stereotyping, homogenization, and the erasure of culturally specific forms of expression. Understanding whether LLMs can meaningfully align with diverse cultures beyond the dominant ones remains a critical challenge. In this paper, we study cultural adaptation in LLMs through the lens of cooking recipes, a domain in which culture, tradition, and creativity are tightly intertwined. We build on the \textit{GlobalFusion} dataset, which pairs human recipes from different countries according to established measures of cultural distance. Using the same country pairs, we generate culturally adapted recipes with multiple LLMs, enabling a direct comparison between human and LLM behavior in cross-cultural content creation. Our analysis shows that LLMs fail to produce culturally representative adaptations. Unlike humans, the divergence of their generated recipes does not correlate with cultural distance. We further provide explanations for this gap. We show that cultural information is weakly preserved in internal model representations, that models inflate novelty in their production by misunderstanding notions such as creativity and tradition, and that they fail to identify adaptation with its associated countries and to ground it in culturally salient elements such as ingredients. These findings highlight fundamental limitations of current LLMs for culturally oriented generation and have important implications for their use in culturally sensitive applications.

AI.tldr: LLMs struggle to provide culturally representative adaptations in cross-cultural recipe generation; unlike humans, their adaptations do not track cultural distance and they exhibit weak grounding in cultural specifics.
AI.motivation: Understand whether LLMs can meaningfully align with diverse cultures beyond dominant ones, a key challenge given documented biases and homogenization in LLM outputs.
AI.method: Utilize the GlobalFusion dataset of paired human recipes across countries; generate culturally adapted recipes with multiple LLMs; compare LLM outputs to human adaptations; analyze correlation with cultural distance; investigate internal representations, novelty, and grounding in ingredients and country associations.
AI.result: LLMs fail to produce culturally representative adaptations; their divergence from the target culture does not correlate with cultural distance; cultural information is weakly preserved in internal representations; models inflate perceived novelty by misinterpreting concepts like creativity and tradition; they struggle to identify the adaptation with its associated country and to ground it in culturally salient elements such as ingredients.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs have fundamental limitations for culturally oriented generation; careful consideration is needed for their use in culturally sensitive applications, and improvements are required in grounding, alignment, and culturally aware evaluation.

title: Blockwise Advantage Estimation for Multi-Objective RL with Verifiable Rewards
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) assigns a single scalar advantage to all tokens in a completion. For structured generations with explicit segments and objectives, this couples unrelated reward signals across segments, leading to objective interference and misattributed credit. We propose Blockwise Advantage Estimation, a family of GRPO-compatible methods that assigns each objective its own advantage and applies it only to the tokens in the corresponding text block, reducing reliance on hand-designed scalar rewards and scaling naturally to additional objectives. A key challenge is estimating advantages for later blocks whose rewards are conditioned on sampled prefixes; standard unbiased approaches require expensive nested rollouts from intermediate states. Concretely, we introduce an Outcome-Conditioned Baseline that approximates intermediate state values using only within-group statistics by stratifying samples according to a prefix-derived intermediate outcome. On math tasks with uncertainty estimation, our method mitigates reward interference, is competitive with a state-of-the-art reward-designed approach, and preserves test-time gains from confidence-weighted ensembling. More broadly, it provides a modular recipe for optimizing sequential objectives in structured generations without additional rollouts.

AI.tldr: Blockwise Advantage Estimation (BAE) extends Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to per-block objectives, reducing cross-block credit misattribution and enabling modular, scalable optimization for structured generations.
AI.motivation: In structured generations with explicit segments and multiple objectives, using a single scalar advantage couplesRewards across segments, causing interference and misattribution of credit.
AI.method: Introduce Blockwise Advantage Estimation: assign each objective its own advantage and apply it only to tokens within the corresponding text block. Combine with an Outcome-Conditioned Baseline that estimates intermediate-state values using within-group statistics by stratifying samples by a prefix-derived intermediate outcome, avoiding costly nested rollouts.
AI.result: On math tasks with uncertainty estimation, the approach mitigates reward interference, is competitive with state-of-the-art reward-designed methods, and preserves test-time gains from confidence-weighted ensembling.
AI.conclusion: Provides a modular, scalable recipe for optimizing sequential objectives in structured generations without requiring extra rollouts, and can accommodate additional objectives.

title: Safe mobility support system using crowd mapping and avoidance route planning using VLM
summary: Autonomous mobile robots offer promising solutions for labor shortages and increased operational efficiency. However, navigating safely and effectively in dynamic environments, particularly crowded areas, remains challenging. This paper proposes a novel framework that integrates Vision-Language Models (VLM) and Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) to generate dynamic crowd-density maps (``Abstraction Maps'') for autonomous robot navigation. Our approach utilizes VLM's capability to recognize abstract environmental concepts, such as crowd densities, and represents them probabilistically via GPR. Experimental results from real-world trials on a university campus demonstrated that robots successfully generated routes avoiding both static obstacles and dynamic crowds, enhancing navigation safety and adaptability.

AI.tldr: A framework combining Vision-Language Models and Gaussian Process Regression to produce probabilistic Abstraction Maps of crowd density for autonomous navigation, validated in campus trials.
AI.motivation: Navigating in dynamic, crowded environments is challenging; traditional methods struggle with uncertainty and rapidly changing crowds; abstraction via VLMs can provide high-level cues fed into probabilistic planners.
AI.method: Use Vision-Language Models to recognize abstract concepts like crowd density from visual data, then represent these concepts probabilistically with Gaussian Process Regression to form Abstraction Maps; integrate these maps into navigation to avoid static obstacles and crowds; validate through real-world campus experiments.
AI.result: Real-world campus trials demonstrated successful route generation that avoids static obstacles and dynamic crowds, improving safety and adaptability.
AI.conclusion: The approach shows that combining VLM-based abstractions with probabilistic regression can enhance robust, dynamic navigation; suggests extensions to other abstract scene attributes and improvements in real-time computation.

title: Conditional Uncertainty-Aware Political Deepfake Detection with Stochastic Convolutional Neural Networks
summary: Recent advances in generative image models have enabled the creation of highly realistic political deepfakes, posing risks to information integrity, public trust, and democratic processes. While automated deepfake detectors are increasingly deployed in moderation and investigative pipelines, most existing systems provide only point predictions and fail to indicate when outputs are unreliable, being an operationally critical limitation in high-stakes political contexts. This work investigates conditional, uncertainty-aware political deepfake detection using stochastic convolutional neural networks within an empirical, decision-oriented reliability framework. Rather than treating uncertainty as a purely Bayesian construct, it is evaluated through observable criteria, including calibration quality, proper scoring rules, and its alignment with prediction errors under both global and confidence-conditioned analyses. A politically focused binary image dataset is constructed via deterministic metadata filtering from a large public real-synthetic corpus. Two pretrained CNN backbones (ResNet-18 and EfficientNet-B4) are fully fine-tuned for classification. Deterministic inference is compared with single-pass stochastic prediction, Monte Carlo dropout with multiple forward passes, temperature scaling, and ensemble-based uncertainty surrogates. Evaluation reports ROC-AUC, thresholded confusion matrices, calibration metrics, and generator-disjoint out-of-distribution performance. Results demonstrate that calibrated probabilistic outputs and uncertainty estimates enable risk-aware moderation policies. A systematic confidence-band analysis further clarifies when uncertainty provides operational value beyond predicted confidence, delineating both the benefits and limitations of uncertainty-aware deepfake detection in political settings.

AI.tldr: Examines uncertainty-aware political deepfake detection using stochastic CNNs; shows calibrated probabilities and uncertainty estimates aid risk-aware moderation, with systematic confidence-band analyses and OOD considerations.
AI.motivation: Political deepfakes threaten information integrity and democratic processes; existing detectors lack uncertainty reporting, which is critical in high-stakes contexts.
AI.method: Fine-tune ResNet-18 and EfficientNet-B4 on a politically focused binary image dataset derived from a real-synthetic corpus; compare deterministic inference, single-pass stochastic prediction, MC dropout, temperature scaling, and ensembles; evaluate ROC-AUC, confusion matrices, calibration metrics, and generator-disjoint OOD performance; analyze both global and confidence-conditioned uncertainties.
AI.result: Calibrated probabilistic outputs and uncertainty estimates enable risk-aware moderation; confidence-band analysis clarifies when uncertainty adds operational value; results outline benefits and limitations of uncertainty-aware detection.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware deepfake detection can provide actionable reliability signals in political contexts, but must be applied with awareness of calibration limits and OOD challenges; this approach supports more informed moderation decisions beyond point predictions.

title: CLI-Gym: Scalable CLI Task Generation via Agentic Environment Inversion
summary: Agentic coding requires agents to effectively interact with runtime environments, e.g., command line interfaces (CLI), so as to complete tasks like resolving dependency issues, fixing system problems, etc. But it remains underexplored how such environment-intensive tasks can be obtained at scale to enhance agents' capabilities. To address this, based on an analogy between the Dockerfile and the agentic task, we propose to employ agents to simulate and explore environment histories, guided by execution feedback. By tracing histories of a healthy environment, its state can be inverted to an earlier one with runtime failures, from which a task can be derived by packing the buggy state and the corresponding error messages. With our method, named CLI-Gym, a total of 1,655 environment-intensive tasks are derived, being the largest collection of its kind. Moreover, with curated successful trajectories, our fine-tuned model, named LiberCoder, achieves substantial absolute improvements of +21.1% (to 46.1%) on Terminal-Bench, outperforming various strong baselines. To our knowledge, this is the first public pipeline for scalable derivation of environment-intensive tasks.

AI.tldr: CLI-Gym derives environment-intensive tasks by inverting histories of healthy environments to create buggy states, enabling scalable task generation (1,655 tasks); LiberCoder, fine-tuned on these tasks, achieves strong gains on Terminal-Bench (46.1% absolute) and surpasses baselines, representing the first public scalable pipeline for such tasks.
AI.motivation: Addresses the scarcity of scalable, environment-rich tasks needed to train and evaluate agents that interact with CLI/runtime environments; leverages an analogy to Dockerfile to structure task derivation.
AI.method: Trace histories of healthy environments, invert to earlier failing states with runtime errors, and pack buggy state plus error messages to derive tasks. Build CLI-Gym to generate 1,655 environment-intensive tasks. Curate successful trajectories and fine-tune a model (LiberCoder).
AI.result: Created 1,655 environment-intensive tasks; LiberCoder improves to 46.1% (absolute +21.1 points) on Terminal-Bench, outperforming strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Presents the first public pipeline for scalable derivation of environment-intensive tasks and demonstrates notable gains from task-informed fine-tuning on agentic performance.

title: Risk-Equalized Differentially Private Synthetic Data: Protecting Outliers by Controlling Record-Level Influence
summary:
When synthetic data is released, some individuals are harder to protect than others. A patient with a rare disease combination or a transaction with unusual characteristics stands out from the crowd. Differential privacy provides worst-case guarantees, but empirical attacks -- particularly membership inference -- succeed far more often against such outliers, especially under moderate privacy budgets and with auxiliary information.
  This paper introduces risk-equalized DP synthesis, a framework that prioritizes protection for high-risk records by reducing their influence on the learned generator. The mechanism operates in two stages: first, a small privacy budget estimates each record's "outlierness"; second, a DP learning procedure weights each record inversely to its risk score. Under Gaussian mechanisms, a record's privacy loss is proportional to its influence on the output -- so deliberately shrinking outliers' contributions yields tighter per-instance privacy bounds for precisely those records that need them most.
  We prove end-to-end DP guarantees via composition and derive closed-form per-record bounds for the synthesis stage (the scoring stage adds a uniform per-record term). Experiments on simulated data with controlled outlier injection show that risk-weighting substantially reduces membership inference success against high-outlierness records; ablations confirm that targeting -- not random downweighting -- drives the improvement. On real-world benchmarks (Breast Cancer, Adult, German Credit), gains are dataset-dependent, highlighting the interplay between scorer quality and synthesis pipeline.

AI.tldr: Risk-equalized DP synthesis stitches a two-stage process that protects high-risk records more by downweighting their influence in the generator, yielding per-record DP bounds and reduced membership inference risk, with dataset-dependent gains.
AI.motivation: Outliers (rare diseases, unusual transactions) pose heightened privacy risk when releasing synthetic data. Standard DP provides worst-case guarantees but empirical attacks exploit high-risk records; a nonuniform privacy budget can better protect such outliers.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (1) scoring stage estimates each record's outlierness under a small privacy budget; (2) synthesis stage weights records inversely to their risk score in a Gaussian-DP generator. Privacy loss scales with a record's influence; shrinking outliers' contribution tightens per-record DP bounds via composition. Provides closed-form per-record bounds for synthesis and a uniform per-record term for scoring.
AI.result: Experiments with simulated data and controlled outliers show reduced membership inference success for high-risk records due to risk-weighting. Ablations show that targeted downweighting—not random downweighting—drives the improvement. On real-world data (Breast Cancer, Adult, German Credit), gains vary by dataset, indicating the importance of scorer quality and interaction with the synthesis pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Risk-equalized DP synthesis can offer tighter, per-record privacy for high-risk individuals by strategically downweighting their influence; effectiveness hinges on the accuracy of the risk scorer and the characteristics of the data; further work should explore scorer design and broader DP settings.

title: Design, Development, and Use of Maya Robot as an Assistant for the Therapy/Education of Children with Cancer: a Pilot Study
summary: This study centers around the design and implementation of the Maya Robot, a portable elephant-shaped social robot, intended to engage with children undergoing cancer treatment. Initial efforts were devoted to enhancing the robot's facial expression recognition accuracy, achieving a 98% accuracy through deep neural networks. Two subsequent preliminary exploratory experiments were designed to advance the study's objectives. The first experiment aimed to compare pain levels experienced by children during the injection process, with and without the presence of the Maya robot. Twenty-five children, aged 4 to 9, undergoing cancer treatment participated in this counterbalanced study. The paired T-test results revealed a significant reduction in perceived pain when the robot was actively present in the injection room. The second experiment sought to assess perspectives of hospitalized children and their mothers during engagement with Maya through a game. Forty participants, including 20 children aged 4 to 9 and their mothers, were involved. Post Human-Maya Interactions, UTAUT questionnaire results indicated that children experienced significantly less anxiety than their parents during the interaction and game play. Notably, children exhibited higher trust levels in both the robot and the games, presenting a statistically significant difference in trust levels compared to their parents (P-value < 0.05). This preliminary exploratory study highlights the positive impact of utilizing Maya as an assistant for therapy/education in a clinical setting, particularly benefiting children undergoing cancer treatment. The findings underscore the potential of social robots in pediatric healthcare contexts, emphasizing improved pain management and emotional well-being among young patients.

AI.tldr: Design and preliminary evaluation of the Maya robot for pediatric oncology care, showing high facial expression recognition accuracy and potential to reduce pain and anxiety while increasing trust during games; two small exploratory studies suggest positive benefits but require replication.
AI.motivation: Address pediatric patients' pain and emotional distress during cancer treatment and enhance therapeutic/educational engagement through a social robot.
AI.method: 1) Design and implement Maya, an elephant-shaped portable social robot; improve facial expression recognition to 98% using deep neural networks. 2) Experiment 1 (n=25, ages 4–9): counterbalanced within-subjects test of pain during injections with vs without Maya; paired t-test. 3) Experiment 2 (n=40; 20 children + 20 mothers): Human–Maya interaction via a game; post-interaction assessments using UTAUT to compare anxiety and trust between children and parents.
AI.result: Facial expression recognition accuracy reached 98%. Experiment 1 showed a significant reduction in perceived pain when Maya was present (paired t-test). Experiment 2 found children had significantly lower anxiety than parents; children showed higher trust in Maya and the games (P<0.05).
AI.conclusion: Preliminary evidence indicates Maya can support therapy/education in pediatric oncology settings, potentially improving pain management and emotional well-being. Findings justify further rigorous trials to establish efficacy and generalizability.

title: Monte Carlo Maximum Likelihood Reconstruction for Digital Holography with Speckle
summary: In coherent imaging, speckle is statistically modeled as multiplicative noise, posing a fundamental challenge for image reconstruction. While maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) provides a principled framework for speckle mitigation, its application to coherent imaging system such as digital holography with finite apertures is hindered by the prohibitive cost of high-dimensional matrix inversion, especially at high resolutions. This computational burden has prevented the use of MLE-based reconstruction with physically accurate aperture modeling. In this work, we propose a randomized linear algebra approach that enables scalable MLE optimization without explicit matrix inversions in gradient computation. By exploiting the structural properties of sensing matrix and using conjugate gradient for likelihood gradient evaluation, the proposed algorithm supports accurate aperture modeling without the simplifying assumptions commonly imposed for tractability. We term the resulting method projected gradient descent with Monte Carlo estimation (PGD-MC). The proposed PGD-MC framework (i) demonstrates robustness to diverse and physically accurate aperture models, (ii) achieves substantial improvements in reconstruction quality and computational efficiency, and (iii) scales effectively to high-resolution digital holography. Extensive experiments incorporating three representative denoisers as regularization show that PGD-MC provides a flexible and effective MLE-based reconstruction framework for digital holography with finite apertures, consistently outperforming prior Plug-and-Play model-based iterative reconstruction methods in both accuracy and speed. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Computational-Imaging-RU/MC_Maximum_Likelihood_Digital_Holography_Speckle.

AI.tldr: A scalable MLE-based reconstruction for digital holography with finite apertures using randomized linear algebra (PGD-MC); avoids explicit matrix inversions via CG gradients and Monte Carlo gradient estimation; robust to realistic aperture models and faster than prior Plug-and-Play methods.
AI.motivation: Speckle in coherent imaging is multiplicative; MLE is principled but computationally prohibitive for high-resolution holography with accurate aperture modeling. A scalable solution is needed to enable physically accurate reconstructions without simplifying assumptions.
AI.method: Randomized linear algebra approach that uses conjugate gradient for likelihood gradient evaluation and Monte Carlo estimation to approximate gradients, enabling projected gradient descent (PGD-MC) without explicit large matrix inversions. The framework supports diverse finite-aperture models and incorporates three representative denoisers as regularizers (Plug-and-Play style).
AI.result: Demonstrates robustness to diverse, physically accurate aperture models; achieves substantial improvements in reconstruction quality and computational efficiency; scales effectively to high-resolution digital holography; outperforms prior Plug-and-Play model-based iterative methods in accuracy and speed; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: PGD-MC provides a flexible and effective MLE-based reconstruction framework for digital holography with finite apertures, combining scalable optimization with accurate physical modeling and denoising priors; promising for high-resolution, physically faithful holographic imaging.

title: GameDevBench: Evaluating Agentic Capabilities Through Game Development
summary: Despite rapid progress on coding agents, progress on their multimodal counterparts has lagged behind. A key challenge is the scarcity of evaluation testbeds that combine the complexity of software development with the need for deep multimodal understanding. Game development provides such a testbed as agents must navigate large, dense codebases while manipulating intrinsically multimodal assets such as shaders, sprites, and animations within a visual game scene. We present GameDevBench, the first benchmark for evaluating agents on game development tasks. GameDevBench consists of 132 tasks derived from web and video tutorials. Tasks require significant multimodal understanding and are complex -- the average solution requires over three times the amount of lines of code and file changes compared to prior software development benchmarks. Agents still struggle with game development, with the best agent solving only 54.5% of tasks. We find a strong correlation between perceived task difficulty and multimodal complexity, with success rates dropping from 46.9% on gameplay-oriented tasks to 31.6% on 2D graphics tasks. To improve multimodal capability, we introduce two simple image and video-based feedback mechanisms for agents. Despite their simplicity, these methods consistently improve performance, with the largest change being an increase in Claude Sonnet 4.5's performance from 33.3% to 47.7%. We release GameDevBench publicly to support further research into agentic game development.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark, GameDevBench, evaluates multimodal agents on game development tasks (132 tasks from tutorials); agents underperform (best ~54.5%); simple image/video feedback improves results (Claude Sonnet 4.5: 33.3%→47.7%); dataset released for research.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of evaluation testbeds that combine software development complexity with multimodal understanding; games provide challenging, multimodal-rich environments requiring navigation of codebases and assets (shaders, sprites, animations) in a visual scene.
AI.method: Introduce GameDevBench with 132 tasks drawn from web/video tutorials; tasks demand substantial multimodal understanding and large code/file-change requirements (average >3x more LOC/file edits than prior benchmarks); assess agents with baseline models; add two simple image and video-based feedback mechanisms to improve multimodal capabilities.
AI.result: Best agent solves 54.5% of tasks. Positive correlation between perceived task difficulty and multimodal complexity: success 46.9% on gameplay-oriented tasks vs 31.6% on 2D graphics tasks. Image/video feedback mechanisms improve performance; Claude Sonnet 4.5 improves from 33.3% to 47.7%.
AI.conclusion: GameDevBench is publicly released to spur research in agentic game development and to highlight remaining multimodal-capability gaps; simple feedback channels can yield meaningful gains in multimodal tasks.

title: Modeling Programming Skills with Source Code Embeddings for Context-aware Exercise Recommendation
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, to be published in LAK26: 16th International Learning Analytics and Knowledge Conference (LAK 2026)
summary: In this paper, we propose a context-aware recommender system that models students' programming skills using embeddings of the source code they submit throughout a course. These embeddings predict students' skills across multiple programming topics, producing profiles that are matched to the skills required by unseen homework problems. To generate recommendations, we compute the cosine similarity between student profiles and problem skill vectors, ranking exercises according to their alignment with each student's current abilities. We evaluated our approach using real data from students and exercises in an introductory programming course at our university. First, we assessed the effectiveness of our source code embeddings for predicting skills, comparing them with token-based and graph-based alternatives. Results showed that Jina embeddings outperformed TF-IDF, CodeBERT-cpp, and GraphCodeBERT across most skills. Additionally, we evaluated the system's ability to recommend exercises aligned with weekly course content by analyzing student submissions collected over seven course offerings. Our approach consistently produced more suitable recommendations than baselines based on correctness or solution time, indicating that predicted programming skills provide a stronger signal for problem recommendation.

AI.tldr: Context-aware recommender for programming education using source-code embeddings to model multi-topic skills and match students to unseen problems via cosine similarity. Empirical evaluation shows Jina embeddings outperform token-based and graph-based baselines (TF-IDF, CodeBERT-cpp, GraphCodeBERT) in predicting skills and guiding exercise recommendations across seven course offerings.
AI.motivation: Improve personalization of problem selection by explicitly modeling students' evolving programming skills from their submitted code, enabling recommendations that align with weekly content and students' current abilities.
AI.method: Build per-student skill profiles from embeddings of submitted source code across a course. Represent problems as skill vectors over topics. Recommend problems by ranking cosine similarity between student profiles and problem vectors. Evaluate embeddings (Jina, TF-IDF, CodeBERT-cpp, GraphCodeBERT) on skill prediction and recommendational quality using real data from an introductory course and seven offerings.
AI.result: Jina embeddings outperformed TF-IDF, CodeBERT-cpp, and GraphCodeBERT on most skills for skill prediction. The system also produced more suitable exercise recommendations than baselines based on correctness or solution time.
AI.conclusion: Embedding-based representations of code submissions provide a strong signal for modeling programming skills and guiding adaptive problem recommendations, with demonstrated effectiveness across multiple course offerings.

title: Developing Neural Network-Based Gaze Control Systems for Social Robots
summary: During multi-party interactions, gaze direction is a key indicator of interest and intent, making it essential for social robots to direct their attention appropriately. Understanding the social context is crucial for robots to engage effectively, predict human intentions, and navigate interactions smoothly. This study aims to develop an empirical motion-time pattern for human gaze behavior in various social situations (e.g., entering, leaving, waving, talking, and pointing) using deep neural networks based on participants' data. We created two video clips-one for a computer screen and another for a virtual reality headset-depicting different social scenarios. Data were collected from 30 participants: 15 using an eye-tracker and 15 using an Oculus Quest 1 headset. Deep learning models, specifically Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Transformers, were used to analyze and predict gaze patterns. Our models achieved 60% accuracy in predicting gaze direction in a 2D animation and 65% accuracy in a 3D animation. Then, the best model was implemented onto the Nao robot; and 36 new participants evaluated its performance. The feedback indicated overall satisfaction, with those experienced in robotics rating the models more favorably.

AI.tldr: Moderate but promising study predicting gaze direction with LSTM/Transformers in 2D/3D social scenarios; small dataset, early-stage robot deployment; user study shows positive feedback.
AI.motivation: To enable social robots to infer human gaze and adapt attention in social interactions, improving engagement and interpretation of intentions.
AI.method: Collect gaze data from 30 participants (eye-tracker and VR headset), create 2 video stimuli (2D screen, 3D VR), train LSTM and Transformer models to predict gaze direction; evaluate on 2D/3D animations; deploy best model on NAO and test with 36 participants.
AI.result: Best models achieved ~60% accuracy in 2D and ~65% in 3D gaze direction prediction; robot deployment had higher favorable ratings from robotics-experienced users.
AI.conclusion: Approach shows potential for gaze-aware social interaction in robots, but limited by small sample sizes and lack of baseline; further work needed on generalization, real-time performance, and detailed evaluation.

title: Comp2Comp: Open-Source Software with FDA-Cleared Artificial Intelligence Algorithms for Computed Tomography Image Analysis
comment: Adrit Rao, Malte Jensen, Andrea T. Fisher, Louis Blankemeier: Co-first authors. Oliver Aalami, Akshay S. Chaudhari: Co-senior authors
summary: Artificial intelligence allows automatic extraction of imaging biomarkers from already-acquired radiologic images. This paradigm of opportunistic imaging adds value to medical imaging without additional imaging costs or patient radiation exposure. However, many open-source image analysis solutions lack rigorous validation while commercial solutions lack transparency, leading to unexpected failures when deployed. Here, we report development and validation for two of the first fully open-sourced, FDA-510(k)-cleared deep learning pipelines to mitigate both challenges: Abdominal Aortic Quantification (AAQ) and Bone Mineral Density (BMD) estimation are both offered within the Comp2Comp package for opportunistic analysis of computed tomography scans. AAQ segments the abdominal aorta to assess aneurysm size; BMD segments vertebral bodies to estimate trabecular bone density and osteoporosis risk. AAQ-derived maximal aortic diameters were compared against radiologist ground-truth measurements on 258 patient scans enriched for abdominal aortic aneurysms from four external institutions. BMD binary classifications (low vs. normal bone density) were compared against concurrent DXA scan ground truths obtained on 371 patient scans from four external institutions. AAQ had an overall mean absolute error of 1.57 mm (95% CI 1.38-1.80 mm). BMD had a sensitivity of 81.0% (95% CI 74.0-86.8%) and specificity of 78.4% (95% CI 72.3-83.7%). Comp2Comp AAQ and BMD demonstrated sufficient accuracy for clinical use. Open-sourcing these algorithms improves transparency of typically opaque FDA clearance processes, allows hospitals to test the algorithms before cumbersome clinical pilots, and provides researchers with best-in-class methods.

AI.tldr: Two open-source, FDA-510(k)-cleared DL pipelines (AAQ for abdominal aorta measurement; BMD for vertebral density) are developed within Comp2Comp for opportunistic CT analysis. External validation shows AAQ MAE of 1.57 mm and BMD sensitivity 81% with specificity 78.4%, supporting clinical use. Open-sourcing aims to improve transparency and testing prior to clinical pilots.
AI.motivation: Address the need for transparent, rigorously validated, open-source imaging tools to mitigate opaque regulatory clearance and unreliable performance of commercial solutions in opportunistic imaging.
AI.method: Developed two DL pipelines within the Comp2Comp package: AAQ segments the abdominal aorta to measure aneurysm size; BMD segments vertebral bodies to estimate trabecular bone density and osteoporosis risk. Validation used external institutions: 258 CT scans for AAQ against radiologist measurements; 371 CT scans for BMD against simultaneous DXA ground truths. Evaluation metrics: MAE for AAQ; sensitivity and specificity for BMD binary classification.
AI.result: AAQ showed a mean absolute error of 1.57 mm (95% CI 1.38–1.80). BMD achieved 81.0% sensitivity (95% CI 74.0–86.8%) and 78.4% specificity (95% CI 72.3–83.7%). Both pipelines cleared FDA-510(k) and are open-sourced within Comp2Comp.
AI.conclusion: The AAQ and BMD pipelines provide accurate, transparent, and testable tools for opportunistic CT analysis, supporting clinical readiness and research replication by making FDA-cleared methods openly available.

title: Kernel-Based Learning of Chest X-ray Images for Predicting ICU Escalation among COVID-19 Patients
summary: Kernel methods have been extensively utilized in machine learning for classification and prediction tasks due to their ability to capture complex non-linear data patterns. However, single kernel approaches are inherently limited, as they rely on a single type of kernel function (e.g., Gaussian kernel), which may be insufficient to fully represent the heterogeneity or multifaceted nature of real-world data. Multiple kernel learning (MKL) addresses these limitations by constructing composite kernels from simpler ones and integrating information from heterogeneous sources. Despite these advances, traditional MKL methods are primarily designed for continuous outcomes. We extend MKL to accommodate the outcome variable belonging to the exponential family, representing a broader variety of data types, and refer to our proposed method as generalized linear models with integrated multiple additive regression with kernels (GLIMARK). Empirically, we demonstrate that GLIMARK can effectively recover or approximate the true data-generating mechanism. We have applied it to a COVID-19 chest X-ray dataset, predicting binary outcomes of ICU escalation and extracting clinically meaningful features, underscoring the practical utility of this approach in real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: GLIMARK extends multiple kernel learning to generalized linear models with additive kernel components for exponential-family outcomes; demonstrates on COVID-19 chest X-ray data for ICU escalation prediction; shows capacity to recover/approximate the true data-generating mechanism and extract clinically meaningful features.
AI.motivation: Single-kernel MKL is limited for heterogeneous data and non-Gaussian outcomes. There is a need to handle diverse data types (binary, count, etc.) and integrate information from multiple sources (e.g., imaging, clinical data). The work proposes a generalized framework to combine multiple kernels within a GLM for exponential-family outcomes, enabling broader applicability and interpretability.
AI.method: Construct a composite kernel as a weighted sum of base kernels and embed it within a generalized linear model with an additive kernel regression structure (GLIMARK). Learn kernel weights and GLM parameters under an exponential-family likelihood, enabling non-Gaussian outcomes (e.g., binary). Use regularization to promote sparsity/interpretability of kernel contributions. Evaluate on a COVID-19 chest X-ray dataset for predicting ICU escalation and extract kernel-based feature importances.
AI.result: GLIMARK can recover or closely approximate the underlying data-generating mechanism. Applied to the COVID-19 X-ray dataset, it achieves effective prediction of ICU escalation and yields features with clinical interpretability, demonstrating practical utility.
AI.conclusion: Extends MKL to exponential-family outcomes via generalized linear additive kernel modeling. Enables principled integration of heterogeneous kernel information with interpretable kernel contributions. Validated on real-world medical imaging data, suggesting broad applicability and potential for improved predictive performance across non-continuous outcomes.

title: Stability Analysis of Geometric Control for a Canonical Class of Underactuated Aerial Vehicles with Spurious Forces
summary: Standard geometric control relies on force-moment decoupling, an assumption that breaks down in many aerial platforms due to spurious forces naturally induced by control moments. While strategies for such coupled systems have been validated experimentally, a rigorous theoretical certification of their stability is currently missing. This work fills this gap by providing the first formal stability analysis for a generic class of floating rigid bodies subject to spurious forces. We introduce a canonical model and construct a Lyapunov-based proof establishing local exponential stability of the hovering equilibrium. Crucially, the analysis explicitly addresses the structural challenges - specifically the induced non-minimum-phase behavior - that prevent the application of standard cascade arguments.

AI.tldr: First formal stability analysis for floating rigid bodies with spurious forces; Lyapunov-based proof of local exponential stability for hovering equilibrium; tackles non-minimum-phase behavior that blocks cascade approaches.
AI.motivation: Fill the theoretical gap: provide a rigorous stability certification for aerial systems where force-moment coupling via spurious forces invalidates standard decoupled models.
AI.method: Introduce a canonical model of a floating rigid body subject to spurious forces; construct a Lyapunov function and prove local exponential stability of the hovering equilibrium, with explicit handling of non-minimum-phase structure that limits cascade arguments.
AI.result: Establishes a local exponential stability certificate for the hovering equilibrium under the proposed model; provides a formal stability framework for coupled force-moment systems with spurious forces.
AI.conclusion: Presents the first formal stability analysis for this class, yielding a rigorous stability guarantee and illuminating how non-minimum-phase effects can be accommodated within Lyapunov-based proofs; sets the stage for broader theoretical development and potential experimental validation.

title: HII-DPO: Eliminate Hallucination via Accurate Hallucination-Inducing Counterfactual Images
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable success across diverse multimodal tasks but remain vulnerable to hallucinations rooted in inherent language bias. Despite recent progress, existing hallucination mitigation methods often overlook the underlying hallucination patterns driven by language bias. In this work, we design a novel pipeline to accurately synthesize Hallucination-Inducing Images (HIIs). Using synthesized HIIs, we reveal a consistent scene-conditioned hallucination pattern: models tend to mention objects that are highly typical of the scene even when visual evidence is removed. To quantify the susceptibility of VLMs to this hallucination pattern, we establish the Masked-Object-Hallucination (MOH) benchmark to rigorously evaluate existing state-of-the-art alignment frameworks. Finally, we leverage HIIs to construct high-quality preference datasets for fine-grained alignment. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach effectively mitigates hallucinations while preserving general model capabilities. Specifically, our method achieves up to a 38% improvement over the current state-of-the-art on standard hallucination benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Introduces Hallucination-Inducing Images (HIIs) and a Masked-Object-Hallucination (MOH) benchmark to diagnose and mitigate language-bias hallucinations in Vision-Language Models, revealing a scene-conditioned pattern and achieving up to 38% improvement on standard benchmarks while preserving model capability.
AI.motivation: VLMs often hallucinate due to intrinsic language biases; existing mitigation methods overlook underlying hallucination patterns. A targeted pipeline to synthesize HIIs and quantify susceptibility is needed to guide fine-grained alignment and robust evaluation.
AI.method: Proposes a pipeline to synthesize HIIs. Uses HIIs to reveal a consistent scene-conditioned hallucination pattern where models mention scene-typical objects even without visual evidence. Introduces MOH to rigorously evaluate susceptibility of VLMs to this pattern. Leverages HIIs to construct high-quality preference datasets for fine-grained alignment and retrains/aligns models to reduce hallucinations while preserving capabilities.
AI.result: Empirical results show effective mitigation of hallucinations with preserved general capabilities. The approach achieves up to a 38% improvement over the current state-of-the-art on standard hallucination benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline provides a practical framework for diagnosing and mitigating language-bias-induced hallucinations in VLMs, introducing HIIs and MOH as evaluation and alignment resources. It demonstrates strong mitigation performance, with potential for generalization, though further work is needed on scalability and broader domain coverage.

title: From Classical to Topological Neural Networks Under Uncertainty
summary: This chapter explores neural networks, topological data analysis, and topological deep learning techniques, alongside statistical Bayesian methods, for processing images, time series, and graphs to maximize the potential of artificial intelligence in the military domain. Throughout the chapter, we highlight practical applications spanning image, video, audio, and time-series recognition, fraud detection, and link prediction for graphical data, illustrating how topology-aware and uncertainty-aware models can enhance robustness, interpretability, and generalization.

AI.tldr: Survey of topology-informed AI (neural networks, TDA, topological deep learning) with Bayesian methods for images, time series, and graphs, targeting military applications; emphasizes robustness, interpretability, and generalization via topology and uncertainty awareness.
AI.motivation: In military AI, robustness, reliability, and interpretability are critical; topology and Bayesian uncertainty can improve these aspects across modalities.
AI.method: Literature synthesis and conceptual integration of neural nets, TDA, topological deep learning, and Bayesian statistics; discussion of applications including image/video/audio/time-series, fraud detection, and link prediction; emphasis on topology-aware and uncertainty-aware models.
AI.result: Conceptual framework and illustrative applications; no explicit empirical results reported in abstract; demonstrates potential performance benefits and robustness gains through topology and uncertainty-aware modeling.
AI.conclusion: Topology-aware and uncertainty-aware models hold promise for improving robustness, interpretability, and generalization of military AI across modalities and graph data; warrants further development and empirical validation.

title: RADAR: Benchmarking Vision-Language-Action Generalization via Real-World Dynamics, Spatial-Physical Intelligence, and Autonomous Evaluation
summary: VLA models have achieved remarkable progress in embodied intelligence; however, their evaluation remains largely confined to simulations or highly constrained real-world settings. This mismatch creates a substantial reality gap, where strong benchmark performance often masks poor generalization in diverse physical environments. We identify three systemic shortcomings in current benchmarking practices that hinder fair and reliable model comparison. (1) Existing benchmarks fail to model real-world dynamics, overlooking critical factors such as dynamic object configurations, robot initial states, lighting changes, and sensor noise. (2) Current protocols neglect spatial--physical intelligence, reducing evaluation to rote manipulation tasks that do not probe geometric reasoning. (3) The field lacks scalable fully autonomous evaluation, instead relying on simplistic 2D metrics that miss 3D spatial structure or on human-in-the-loop systems that are costly, biased, and unscalable. To address these limitations, we introduce RADAR (Real-world Autonomous Dynamics And Reasoning), a benchmark designed to systematically evaluate VLA generalization under realistic conditions. RADAR integrates three core components: (1) a principled suite of physical dynamics; (2) dedicated tasks that explicitly test spatial reasoning and physical understanding; and (3) a fully autonomous evaluation pipeline based on 3D metrics, eliminating the need for human supervision. We apply RADAR to audit multiple state-of-the-art VLA models and uncover severe fragility beneath their apparent competence. Performance drops precipitously under modest physical dynamics, with the expectation of 3D IoU declining from 0.261 to 0.068 under sensor noise. Moreover, models exhibit limited spatial reasoning capability. These findings position RADAR as a necessary bench toward reliable and generalizable real-world evaluation of VLA models.

AI.tldr: RADAR proposes a realistic, autonomous benchmark to evaluate Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in embodied AI, addressing biases in current benchmarks by simulating real-world dynamics, testing spatial reasoning, and using autonomous 3D metrics; initial results reveal severe fragility and limited spatial reasoning under modest dynamics.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarking practices for VLA models misrepresent real-world generalization by focusing on simulations, constrained settings, and 2D human-in-the-loop evaluations. There is a need for a principled, scalable benchmark that captures 3D dynamics and spatial understanding to reliably compare models.
AI.method: RADAR integrates (1) a principled suite of physical dynamics, (2) dedicated tasks that test spatial reasoning and physical understanding, and (3) a fully autonomous evaluation pipeline using 3D metrics, removing human supervision.
AI.result: Across state-of-the-art VLA models, performance drops under modest physical dynamics; IoU declines from 0.261 to 0.068 under sensor noise, indicating fragility and limited spatial reasoning.
AI.conclusion: RADAR provides a necessary benchmark for reliable, generalizable real-world evaluation of VLA models, enabling fairer comparison and highlighting gaps in current models’ robustness and 3D understanding.

title: Towards Remote Sensing Change Detection with Neural Memory
comment: accepted by IEEE Transactions on Geoscience & Remote Sensing
summary: Remote sensing change detection is essential for environmental monitoring, urban planning, and related applications. However, current methods often struggle to capture long-range dependencies while maintaining computational efficiency. Although Transformers can effectively model global context, their quadratic complexity poses scalability challenges, and existing linear attention approaches frequently fail to capture intricate spatiotemporal relationships. Drawing inspiration from the recent success of Titans in language tasks, we present ChangeTitans, the Titans-based framework for remote sensing change detection. Specifically, we propose VTitans, the first Titans-based vision backbone that integrates neural memory with segmented local attention, thereby capturing long-range dependencies while mitigating computational overhead. Next, we present a hierarchical VTitans-Adapter to refine multi-scale features across different network layers. Finally, we introduce TS-CBAM, a two-stream fusion module leveraging cross-temporal attention to suppress pseudo-changes and enhance detection accuracy. Experimental evaluations on four benchmark datasets (LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, LEVIR-CD+, and SYSU-CD) demonstrate that ChangeTitans achieves state-of-the-art results, attaining \textbf{84.36\%} IoU and \textbf{91.52\%} F1-score on LEVIR-CD, while remaining computationally competitive.

AI.tldr: ChangeTitans: a Titans-based framework for remote sensing change detection that combines a neural-memory, segmented local attention backbone (VTitans) with a multi-scale VTitans-Adapter and a cross-temporal TS-CBAM fusion module to capture long-range dependencies efficiently, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Remote sensing change detection requires modeling long-range spatiotemporal dependencies efficiently. Standard Transformers suffer from quadratic complexity, and linear attention methods often miss intricate relationships. A memory-augmented, segmented attention approach inspired by Titans aims to balance global context with computational efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce VTitans, a Titans-based vision backbone enhanced with neural memory and segmented local attention; add a hierarchical VTitans-Adapter to refine multi-scale features across layers; implement TS-CBAM, a two-stream fusion module that uses cross-temporal attention to suppress pseudo-changes and improve accuracy.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on four benchmarks (LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, LEVIR-CD+, SYSU-CD) with IoU 84.36% and F1-score 91.52% on LEVIR-CD, while maintaining competitive computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The ChangeTitans framework effectively models long-range dependencies in remote sensing change detection with improved accuracy and efficiency, offering a scalable solution for multi-temporal analysis.

title: Linear-LLM-SCM: Benchmarking LLMs for Coefficient Elicitation in Linear-Gaussian Causal Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown potential in identifying qualitative causal relations, but their ability to perform quantitative causal reasoning -- estimating effect sizes that parametrize functional relationships -- remains underexplored in continuous domains. We introduce Linear-LLM-SCM, a plug-and-play benchmarking framework for evaluating LLMs on linear Gaussian structural causal model (SCM) parametrization when the DAG is given. The framework decomposes a DAG into local parent-child sets and prompts an LLM to produce a regression-style structural equation per node, which is aggregated and compared against available ground-truth parameters. Our experiments show several challenges in such benchmarking tasks, namely, strong stochasticity in the results in some of the models and susceptibility to DAG misspecification via spurious edges in the continuous domains. Across models, we observe substantial variability in coefficient estimates for some settings and sensitivity to structural and semantic perturbations, highlighting current limitations of LLMs as quantitative causal parameterizers. We also open-sourced the benchmarking framework so that researchers can utilize their DAGs and any off-the-shelf LLMs plug-and-play for evaluation in their domains effortlessly.

AI.tldr: A plug-and-play framework (Linear-LLM-SCM) to evaluate LLMs on learning linear-Gaussian SCM parameters from a given DAG; finds variability and fragility to misspecification; open-sourced.
AI.motivation: To assess whether LLMs can perform quantitative causal reasoning in continuous domains, specifically estimating regression coefficients in linear-Gaussian structural causal models given a DAG.
AI.method: Decompose the DAG into local parent–child sets, prompt an LLM to output a regression-style structural equation per node, aggregate results, and compare coefficients against ground-truth parameters; evaluated as a plug-and-play benchmark with any off-the-shelf LLM; framework is open-sourced.
AI.result: Across models, results show strong stochasticity, susceptibility to DAG misspecification via spurious edges in continuous domains, substantial variability in coefficient estimates, and sensitivity to structural/semantic perturbations; indicates current limitations of LLMs as quantitative causal parameterizers.
AI.conclusion: LLMs currently exhibit limited reliability for precise quantitative causal parameterization in continuous domains; the proposed benchmarking framework enables systematic evaluation and encourages development of more robust LLM-based causal parameterizers.

title: Scaling World Model for Hierarchical Manipulation Policies
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are promising for generalist robot manipulation but remain brittle in out-of-distribution (OOD) settings, especially with limited real-robot data. To resolve the generalization bottleneck, we introduce a hierarchical Vision-Language-Action framework \our{} that leverages the generalization of large-scale pre-trained world model for robust and generalizable VIsual Subgoal TAsk decomposition VISTA. Our hierarchical framework \our{} consists of a world model as the high-level planner and a VLA as the low-level executor. The high-level world model first divides manipulation tasks into subtask sequences with goal images, and the low-level policy follows the textual and visual guidance to generate action sequences. Compared to raw textual goal specification, these synthesized goal images provide visually and physically grounded details for low-level policies, making it feasible to generalize across unseen objects and novel scenarios. We validate both visual goal synthesis and our hierarchical VLA policies in massive out-of-distribution scenarios, and the performance of the same-structured VLA in novel scenarios could boost from 14% to 69% with the guidance generated by the world model. Results demonstrate that our method outperforms previous baselines with a clear margin, particularly in out-of-distribution scenarios. Project page: \href{https://vista-wm.github.io/}{https://vista-wm.github.io}

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Vision-Language-Action (VISTA) combines a world-model high-level planner with a low-level VLA executor. It synthesizes subgoal goal images to ground manipulation, improving generalization in out-of-distribution (OOD) settings and unseen objects, with performance gains from 14% to 69% using world-model-guided goals and strong baselines performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome brittleness of VLA models in OOD and data-scarce regimes by leveraging a pre-trained world model to generate visually grounded subgoals that steer low-level manipulation policies.
AI.method: Propose a two-tier architecture: a world model generates subtask sequences with goal images; a VLA policy uses textual and visual guidance to execute action sequences. Visual goal synthesis provides richer grounding than raw text alone. Evaluate under large OOD scenarios and compare against baselines; project page provided.
AI.result: The approach yields substantial improvement in OOD generalization, with same-structured VLA performance improving from 14% to 69% when guided by world-model-generated goals; outperforms prior baselines by clear margins.
AI.conclusion: Grounding hierarchical VLA with synthesized goal images from a world model enhances robustness and generalization to unseen objects and scenarios; demonstrates the effectiveness of goal-image grounding and hierarchical planning for manipulation tasks.

title: End-to-End LiDAR optimization for 3D point cloud registration
comment: 36th British Machine Vision Conference 2025, {BMVC} 2025, Sheffield, UK, November 24-27, 2025. Project page: https://lvsn.github.io/e2e-lidar-registration/
summary: LiDAR sensors are a key modality for 3D perception, yet they are typically designed independently of downstream tasks such as point cloud registration. Conventional registration operates on pre-acquired datasets with fixed LiDAR configurations, leading to suboptimal data collection and significant computational overhead for sampling, noise filtering, and parameter tuning. In this work, we propose an adaptive LiDAR sensing framework that dynamically adjusts sensor parameters, jointly optimizing LiDAR acquisition and registration hyperparameters. By integrating registration feedback into the sensing loop, our approach optimally balances point density, noise, and sparsity, improving registration accuracy and efficiency. Evaluations in the CARLA simulation demonstrate that our method outperforms fixed-parameter baselines while retaining generalization abilities, highlighting the potential of adaptive LiDAR for autonomous perception and robotic applications.

AI.tldr: An adaptive LiDAR sensing framework that jointly optimizes sensor parameters and registration hyperparameters via a feedback loop, improving 3D point cloud registration accuracy and efficiency over fixed configurations in CARLA simulations.
AI.motivation: Registration with fixed LiDAR configurations is often suboptimal and incurs computational overhead from data processing, just-in-time tuning, and parameter selection. Align sensing with downstream tasks to improve efficiency and accuracy.
AI.method: A closed-loop system where LiDAR acquisition parameters (e.g., density, range, noise characteristics) and registration hyperparameters are optimized jointly. Registration feedback informs adjustments to sensing, balancing point density, noise, and sparsity to optimize registration performance. Evaluation conducted in CARLA to test generalization.
AI.result: The adaptive approach outperforms fixed-parameter baselines in registration accuracy and efficiency, while maintaining generalization to unseen data or scenarios in CARLA.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive LiDAR sensing shows promise for autonomous perception and robotics by reducing data collection and computation while maintaining or improving registration performance; supports broader integration of sensing and perception tasks and motivates real-world deployment and extensions.

title: Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning of Drone Aerobatics by Exploiting Geometric Symmetries
summary: Flight control for autonomous micro aerial vehicles (MAVs) is evolving from steady flight near equilibrium points toward more aggressive aerobatic maneuvers, such as flips, rolls, and Power Loop. Although reinforcement learning (RL) has shown great potential in these tasks, conventional RL methods often suffer from low data efficiency and limited generalization. This challenge becomes more pronounced in multi-task scenarios where a single policy is required to master multiple maneuvers. In this paper, we propose a novel end-to-end multi-task reinforcement learning framework, called GEAR (Geometric Equivariant Aerobatics Reinforcement), which fully exploits the inherent SO(2) rotational symmetry in MAV dynamics and explicitly incorporates this property into the policy network architecture. By integrating an equivariant actor network, FiLM-based task modulation, and a multi-head critic, GEAR achieves both efficiency and flexibility in learning diverse aerobatic maneuvers, enabling a data-efficient, robust, and unified framework for aerobatic control. GEAR attains a 98.85\% success rate across various aerobatic tasks, significantly outperforming baseline methods. In real-world experiments, GEAR demonstrates stable execution of multiple maneuvers and the capability to combine basic motion primitives to complete complex aerobatics.

AI.tldr: A multi-task, symmetry-exploiting RL framework (GEAR) for MAV aerobatics that achieves high data efficiency and robust multi-maneuver performance using an SO(2) equivariant actor, FiLM-based task modulation, and a multi-head critic; reports 98.85% success across tasks and successful real-world demonstrations.
AI.motivation: Tackle data inefficiency and poor generalization of reinforcement learning in autonomous MAV aerobatics, especially in multi-task settings where a single policy must master diverse maneuvers; leverage geometric symmetry to improve learning efficiency and transfer.
AI.method: Propose GEAR, an end-to-end multi-task reinforcement learning framework that exploits SO(2) rotational symmetry in MAV dynamics. Core components include an equivariant actor network, FiLM-based task modulation for maneuver-specific conditioning, and a multi-head critic to handle multiple tasks. Designed to be data-efficient and flexible for diverse aerobatic maneuvers.
AI.result: GEAR achieves a 98.85% success rate across various aerobatic tasks, significantly outperforming baselines. Real-world experiments show stable execution of multiple maneuvers and the ability to combine basic motion primitives to perform complex aerobatics.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging geometric equivariance and modular conditioning yields a data-efficient, robust, and unified framework for aerobatic control in MAVs, with strong empirical performance in simulation and real-world tests.

title: Characterizing and Optimizing the Spatial Kernel of Multi Resolution Hash Encodings
summary: Multi-Resolution Hash Encoding (MHE), the foundational technique behind Instant Neural Graphics Primitives, provides a powerful parameterization for neural fields. However, its spatial behavior lacks rigorous understanding from a physical systems perspective, leading to reliance on heuristics for hyperparameter selection. This work introduces a novel analytical approach that characterizes MHE by examining its Point Spread Function (PSF), which is analogous to the Green's function of the system. This methodology enables a quantification of the encoding's spatial resolution and fidelity. We derive a closed-form approximation for the collision-free PSF, uncovering inherent grid-induced anisotropy and a logarithmic spatial profile.
