Compared with one-shot codebook-based methods, SUCode exploits semantic-aware, pixel-level codebook representation tailored to heterogeneous underwater degradation. A three-stage training paradigm is employed to represent raw underwater image features to avoid pseudo ground-truth contamination. Gated Channel Attention Module (GCAM) and Frequency-Aware Feature Fusion (FAFF) jointly integrate channel and frequency cues for faithful color restoration and texture recovery. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that SUCode achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming recent UIE methods on both reference and no-reference metrics. The code will be made public available at https://github.com/oucailab/SUCode.

AI.tldr: Proposes SUCode, a semantic-aware underwater image enhancement framework using a semantic-aware discrete codebook and a three-stage training paradigm, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: UIE is ill-posed and global models fail due to highly heterogeneous degradation across semantic regions; there is a need for region-aware, adaptive solutions.
AI.method: Introduces semantic-aware discrete codebook representation for pixel-level degradation; employs a three-stage training paradigm to avoid pseudo ground-truth contamination; uses Gated Channel Attention Module (GCAM) and Frequency-Aware Feature Fusion (FAFF) to fuse channel and frequency cues for reliable color restoration and texture recovery.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks, outperforming recent UIE methods on both reference and no-reference metrics; code will be publicly released.
AI.conclusion: Semantic-aware codebooks combined with GCAM and FAFF enable adaptive, region-aware underwater image enhancement, effectively handling heterogeneous degradation; the approach achieves strong quantitative and qualitative results and the authors provide open-source code.

title: Time-to-Event Transformer to Capture Timing Attention of Events in EHR Time Series
summary:
Automatically discovering personalized sequential events from large-scale time-series data is crucial for enabling precision medicine in clinical research, yet it remains a formidable challenge even for contemporary AI models. For example, while transformers capture rich associations, they are mostly agnostic to event timing and ordering, thereby bypassing potential causal reasoning.
  Intuitively, we need a method capable of evaluating the "degree of alignment" among patient-specific trajectories and identifying their shared patterns, i.e., the significant events in a consistent sequence. This necessitates treating timing as a true \emph{computable} dimension, allowing models to assign ``relative timestamps'' to candidate events beyond their observed physical times.
  In this work, we introduce LITT, a novel Timing-Transformer architecture that enables temporary alignment of sequential events on a virtual ``relative timeline'', thereby enabling \emph{event-timing-focused attention} and personalized interpretations of clinical trajectories. Its interpretability and effectiveness are validated on real-world longitudinal EHR data from 3,276 breast cancer patients to predict the onset timing of cardiotoxicity-induced heart disease. Furthermore, LITT outperforms both the benchmark and state-of-the-art survival analysis methods on public datasets, positioning it as a significant step forward for precision medicine in clinical AI.

AI.tldr: LITT: a Timing-Transformer that aligns sequential events on a virtual relative timeline to capture timing-sensitive patterns in patient trajectories; validated on EHR data for predicting cardiotoxicity timing and outperforms baselines on public survival datasets.
AI.motivation: Transformers excel at learning associations but largely ignore event timing/order; a computable temporal dimension is needed to identify shared, causally relevant sequences across patients for precision medicine.
AI.method: LITT introduces a Timing-Transformer architecture that enables temporary alignment of events on a virtual relative timeline, enabling event-timing-focused attention and interpretable, personalized trajectory analysis.
AI.result: Validated on real-world EHRs from 3,276 breast cancer patients for predicting onset timing of cardiotoxicity-induced heart disease; outperforms benchmark and state-of-the-art survival analyses on public datasets.
AI.conclusion: Timing-aware, interpretable trajectory modeling with LITT represents a substantive advancement for clinical AI in precision medicine, improving both interpretability and predictive performance.

title: (MGS)$^2$-Net: Unifying Micro-Geometric Scale and Macro-Geometric Structure for Cross-View Geo-Localization
summary: Cross-view geo-localization (CVGL) is pivotal for GNSS-denied UAV navigation but remains brittle under the drastic geometric misalignment between oblique aerial views and orthographic satellite references. Existing methods predominantly operate within a 2D manifold, neglecting the underlying 3D geometry where view-dependent vertical facades (macro-structure) and scale variations (micro-scale) severely corrupt feature alignment. To bridge this gap, we propose (MGS)$^2$, a geometry-grounded framework. The core of our innovation is the Macro-Geometric Structure Filtering (MGSF) module. Unlike pixel-wise matching sensitive to noise, MGSF leverages dilated geometric gradients to physically filter out high-frequency facade artifacts while enhancing the view-invariant horizontal plane, directly addressing the domain shift. To guarantee robust input for this structural filtering, we explicitly incorporate a Micro-Geometric Scale Adaptation (MGSA) module. MGSA utilizes depth priors to dynamically rectify scale discrepancies via multi-branch feature fusion. Furthermore, a Geometric-Appearance Contrastive Distillation (GACD) loss is designed to strictly discriminate against oblique occlusions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that (MGS)$^2$ achieves state-of-the-art performance, recording a Recall@1 of 97.5\% on University-1652 and 97.02\% on SUES-200. Furthermore, the framework exhibits superior cross-dataset generalization against geometric ambiguity. The code is available at: \href{https://github.com/GabrielLi1473/MGS-Net}{https://github.com/GabrielLi1473/MGS-Net}.

AI.tldr: Proposes MGS^2: a geometry-grounded cross-view geo-localization framework that filters macro-geometric structure and adapts micro-geometric scale using depth priors, aided by a geometric-appearance contrastive distillation; achieves state-of-the-art Recall@1 on University-1652 and SUES-200 with strong cross-dataset generalization; code released.
AI.motivation: CVGL is brittle due to drastic 3D misalignment between oblique aerial views and orthographic satellite references. Existing 2D-manifold methods neglect 3D geometry, particularly view-dependent macro-structure (facades) and micro-scale variations, causing feature misalignment.
AI.method: Macro-Geometric Structure Filtering (MGSF) uses dilated geometric gradients to filter out high-frequency facade artifacts and enhance the view-invariant horizontal plane. Micro-Geometric Scale Adaptation (MGSA) leverages depth priors to dynamically rectify scale discrepancies via multi-branch feature fusion. Geometric-Appearance Contrastive Distillation (GACD) loss enforces discrimination against oblique occlusions. End-to-end framework with these modules to improve cross-view matching; code available at the provided repo.
AI.result: Experimentally achieves Recall@1 of 97.5% on University-1652 and 97.02% on SUES-200, with strong cross-dataset generalization against geometric ambiguity; code released.
AI.conclusion: The geometry-grounded approach effectively mitigates 3D misalignment in cross-view geo-localization, surpassing prior 2D-centric methods and demonstrating robust generalization. This framework is well-suited for GNSS-denied UAV navigation and related cross-view localization tasks.

title: Enhancing YOLOv11n for Reliable Child Detection in Noisy Surveillance Footage
summary: This paper presents a practical and lightweight solution for enhancing child detection in low-quality surveillance footage, a critical component in real-world missing child alert and daycare monitoring systems. Building upon the efficient YOLOv11n architecture, we propose a deployment-ready pipeline that improves detection under challenging conditions including occlusion, small object size, low resolution, motion blur, and poor lighting commonly found in existing CCTV infrastructures. Our approach introduces a domain-specific augmentation strategy that synthesizes realistic child placements using spatial perturbations such as partial visibility, truncation, and overlaps, combined with photometric degradations including lighting variation and noise. To improve recall of small and partially occluded instances, we integrate Slicing Aided Hyper Inference (SAHI) at inference time. All components are trained and evaluated on a filtered, child-only subset of the Roboflow Daycare dataset. Compared to the baseline YOLOv11n, our enhanced system achieves a mean Average Precision at 0.5 IoU (mAP@0.5) of 0.967 and a mean Average Precision averaged over IoU thresholds from 0.5 to 0.95 (mAP@0.5:0.95) of 0.783, yielding absolute improvements of 0.7 percent and 2.3 percent, respectively, without architectural changes. Importantly, the entire pipeline maintains compatibility with low-power edge devices and supports real-time performance, making it particularly well suited for low-cost or resource-constrained industrial surveillance deployments. The example augmented dataset and the source code used to generate it are available at: https://github.com/html-ptit/Data-Augmentation-YOLOv11n-child-detection

AI.tldr: Lightweight, deployment-ready YOLOv11n-based child detector enhanced by domain-specific augmentation and SAHI for better recall under occlusion, blur, and low-res conditions; achieves state-of-the-art-like mAP on Roboflow Daycare subset with real-time edge compatibility.
AI.motivation: Need for reliable child detection in low-quality surveillance to support missing child alerts and daycare monitoring; real-world CCTV conditions present occlusion, small size, blur, and poor lighting; existing detectors struggle without architectural changes.
AI.method: Introduce domain-specific augmentation simulating partial visibility, truncation, overlaps, and photometric degradations; integrate Slicing Aided Hyper Inference (SAHI) at inference to boost small/occluded detections; base on YOLOv11n; train/evaluate on filtered child subset of Roboflow Daycare; no architectural changes; pipeline edge-friendly.
AI.result: mAP@0.5 = 0.967 (improvement +0.7pp) and mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.783 (improvement +2.3pp) over baseline YOLOv11n; real-time performance on low-power devices.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates practical gains via data-centric augmentation and SAHI without changing model architecture; suitable for low-cost surveillance; dataset and code available online.

title: Affordances Enable Partial World Modeling with LLMs
summary: Full models of the world require complex knowledge of immense detail. While pre-trained large models have been hypothesized to contain similar knowledge due to extensive pre-training on vast amounts of internet scale data, using them directly in a search procedure is inefficient and inaccurate. Conversely, partial models focus on making high quality predictions for a subset of state and actions: those linked through affordances that achieve user intents~\citep{khetarpal2020can}. Can we posit large models as partial world models? We provide a formal answer to this question, proving that agents achieving task-agnostic, language-conditioned intents necessarily possess predictive partial-world models informed by affordances. In the multi-task setting, we introduce distribution-robust affordances and show that partial models can be extracted to significantly improve search efficiency. Empirical evaluations in tabletop robotics tasks demonstrate that our affordance-aware partial models reduce the search branching factor and achieve higher rewards compared to full world models.

AI.tldr: Large pretrained models can function as partial world models via affordances. The paper formalizes and proves that task-agnostic, language-conditioned intents entail predictive partial-world models informed by affordances, introduces distribution-robust affordances, and shows that such partial models improve search efficiency. Empirically, in tabletop robotics, affordance-aware partial models reduce search branching and yield higher rewards than full world models.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between full world models and large pretrained models by showing that large models can serve as efficient, partial world models through affordances, enabling better planning/search with language-conditioned tasks.
AI.method: Formal theoretical analysis proving the necessity of predictive partial-world models for task-agnostic language-conditioned intents; formalize affordances and distribution-robust affordances; develop extraction of partial models; empirical evaluation in tabletop robotics to compare search efficiency and rewards against full models.
AI.result: Theoretically, task-agnostic language-conditioned intents imply predictive partial-world models informed by affordances; practically, partial models can be extracted to improve search efficiency; empirically, affordance-aware partial models reduce branching factor and achieve higher rewards than full world models in tabletop robotics tasks.
AI.conclusion: Large models can serve as effective partial world models when guided by affordances; this enhances search efficiency and planning in multi-task, language-conditioned settings, with strong empirical support in robotics experiments.

title: Colorful Talks with Graphs: Human-Interpretable Graph Encodings for Large Language Models
summary: Graph problems are fundamentally challenging for large language models (LLMs). While LLMs excel at processing unstructured text, graph tasks require reasoning over explicit structure, permutation invariance, and computationally complex relationships, creating a mismatch with the representations of text-based models. Our work investigates how LLMs can be effectively applied to graph problems despite these barriers. We introduce a human-interpretable structural encoding strategy for graph-to-text translation that injects graph structure directly into natural language prompts. Our method involves computing a variant of Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL) similarity classes and maps them to human-like color tokens rather than numeric labels. The key insight is that semantically meaningful and human-interpretable cues may be more effectively processed by LLMs than opaque symbolic encoding. Experimental results on multiple algorithmic and predictive graph tasks show the considerable improvements by our method on both synthetic and real-world datasets. By capturing both local and global-range dependencies, our method enhances LLM performance especially on graph tasks that require reasoning over global graph structure.

AI.tldr: Proposes a graph-to-text encoding via WL-based similarity classes mapped to humane color tokens to inject graph structure into LLM prompts, improving graph reasoning on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: LLMs excel at text but struggle with explicit graph structure, permutation invariance, and complex relations. A human-interpretable encoding that preserves structure may bridge this gap.
AI.method: Compute a variant of Weisfeiler-Lehman similarity classes and map them to color tokens; translate graph structure into natural language prompts (graph-to-text); capture local and global dependencies.
AI.result: Significant improvements across multiple algorithmic and predictive graph tasks on synthetic and real-world datasets; enhanced ability to reason about both local and global graph structure.
AI.conclusion: Human-interpretable, semantically meaningful cues (color tokens) enable better LLM processing of graph tasks, suggesting broader applicability of structured prompting via WL-inspired encodings.

title: From Steering to Pedalling: Do Autonomous Driving VLMs Generalize to Cyclist-Assistive Spatial Perception and Planning?
summary: Cyclists often encounter safety-critical situations in urban traffic, highlighting the need for assistive systems that support safe and informed decision-making. Recently, vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong performance on autonomous driving benchmarks, suggesting their potential for general traffic understanding and navigation-related reasoning. However, existing evaluations are predominantly vehicle-centric and fail to assess perception and reasoning from a cyclist-centric viewpoint. To address this gap, we introduce CyclingVQA, a diagnostic benchmark designed to probe perception, spatio-temporal understanding, and traffic-rule-to-lane reasoning from a cyclist's perspective. Evaluating 31+ recent VLMs spanning general-purpose, spatially enhanced, and autonomous-driving-specialized models, we find that current models demonstrate encouraging capabilities, while also revealing clear areas for improvement in cyclist-centric perception and reasoning, particularly in interpreting cyclist-specific traffic cues and associating signs with the correct navigational lanes. Notably, several driving-specialized models underperform strong generalist VLMs, indicating limited transfer from vehicle-centric training to cyclist-assistive scenarios. Finally, through systematic error analysis, we identify recurring failure modes to guide the development of more effective cyclist-assistive intelligent systems.

AI.tldr: CyclingVQA introduces a cyclist-centric VQA benchmark to evaluate perception and reasoning with 31+ vision-language models, revealing gaps in cyclist-specific perception and lane-sign reasoning and highlighting limited transfer from vehicle-centric training to cyclist-assistive tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing traffic understanding benchmarks and VLM evaluations are vehicle-centric and do not adequately assess perception and reasoning from a cyclist's viewpoint, which is critical for safe urban cycling.
AI.method: Define CyclingVQA to probe perception, spatio-temporal understanding, and traffic-rule-to-lane reasoning from a cyclist perspective. Evaluate 31+ VLMs spanning general-purpose, spatially enhanced, and autonomous-driving-specialized models. Conduct systematic error analysis to identify failure modes.
AI.result: Models show encouraging capabilities but have clear gaps in cyclist-centric perception and reasoning, especially for interpreting cyclist-specific cues and mapping signs to the correct navigational lanes. Driving-specialized models often underperform strong generalist VLMs, indicating limited transfer from vehicle-centric training. Error analysis reveals recurring failure modes to guide future improvements.
AI.conclusion: There is a need to improve cyclist-assistive systems and enhance transfer from vehicle-centric training to cyclist-specific scenarios. Future work should target the identified failure modes and develop models better attuned to cyclist cues and lane-sign associations.

title: Fast Person Detection Using YOLOX With AI Accelerator For Train Station Safety
comment: 6 pages, 8 figures, 2 tables. Presented at 2024 International Electronics Symposium (IES). IEEE DOI: 10.1109/IES63037.2024.10665874
summary: Recently, Image processing has advanced Faster and applied in many fields, including health, industry, and transportation. In the transportation sector, object detection is widely used to improve security, for example, in traffic security and passenger crossings at train stations. Some accidents occur in the train crossing area at the station, like passengers uncarefully when passing through the yellow line. So further security needs to be developed. Additional technology is required to reduce the number of accidents. This paper focuses on passenger detection applications at train stations using YOLOX and Edge AI Accelerator hardware. the performance of the AI accelerator will be compared with Jetson Orin Nano. The experimental results show that the Hailo-8 AI hardware accelerator has higher accuracy than Jetson Orin Nano (improvement of over 12%) and has lower latency than Jetson Orin Nano (reduced 20 ms).

AI.tldr: YOLOX-based passenger detection at train stations evaluated on Edge AI hardware. Hailo-8 outperforms Jetson Orin Nano in accuracy (>12%) and latency (−20 ms).
AI.motivation: Enhance safety in train stations by automatically detecting passengers near tracks to reduce accidents.
AI.method: Object detection using YOLOX deployed on Edge AI accelerators; performance comparison between Hailo-8 and Jetson Orin Nano with experimental evaluation.
AI.result: Hailo-8 achieves higher accuracy and lower latency than Jetson Orin Nano (accuracy improvement >12%; latency reduction ~20 ms).
AI.conclusion: Edge AI accelerators (Hailo-8) show promise for real-time passenger detection at train stations, offering better accuracy and responsiveness than Jetson Orin Nano in this setup.

title: Modular Multi-Task Learning for Chemical Reaction Prediction
summary: Adapting large language models (LLMs) trained on broad organic chemistry to smaller, domain-specific reaction datasets is a key challenge in chemical and pharmaceutical R&D. Effective specialisation requires learning new reaction knowledge while preserving general chemical understanding across related tasks. Here, we evaluate Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) as a parameter-efficient alternative to full fine-tuning for organic reaction prediction on limited, complex datasets. Using USPTO reaction classes and challenging C-H functionalisation reactions, we benchmark forward reaction prediction, retrosynthesis and reagent prediction. LoRA achieves accuracy comparable to full fine-tuning while effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting and better preserving multi-task performance. Both fine-tuning approaches generalise beyond training distributions, producing plausible alternative solvent predictions. Notably, C-H functionalisation fine-tuning reveals that LoRA and full fine-tuning encode subtly different reactivity patterns, suggesting more effective reaction-specific adaptation with LoRA. As LLMs continue to scale, our results highlight the practicality of modular, parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategies for their flexible deployment for chemistry applications.

AI.tldr: LoRA provides parameter-efficient fine-tuning for LLMs in organic reaction prediction, matching full fine-tuning while reducing forgetting and maintaining multi-task generalization.
AI.motivation: Need to specialize broad-scoped LLMs to small, domain-specific reaction datasets without eroding broad chemical knowledge.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation of LoRA vs full fine-tuning on USPTO reaction classes and C–H functionalization; tasks include forward prediction, retrosynthesis, and reagent prediction; analyze accuracy, catastrophic forgetting, out-of-distribution/generalization (alternative solvents); compare reactivity patterns learned.
AI.result: LoRA achieves accuracy comparable to full fine-tuning, mitigates catastrophic forgetting, and better preserves multi-task performance; models generalize to alternative solvents; C–H functionalization reveals subtly different reactivity patterns between LoRA and full fine-tuning; supports modular parameter-efficient fine-tuning for chemistry.
AI.conclusion: LoRA-based, parameter-efficient fine-tuning is practical for chemistry applications and scalable for larger LLMs, enabling effective domain adaptation without sacrificing broader chemistry knowledge.

title: Semi-Supervised Cross-Domain Imitation Learning
comment: Published in Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR)
summary: Cross-domain imitation learning (CDIL) accelerates policy learning by transferring expert knowledge across domains, which is valuable in applications where the collection of expert data is costly. Existing methods are either supervised, relying on proxy tasks and explicit alignment, or unsupervised, aligning distributions without paired data, but often unstable. We introduce the Semi-Supervised CDIL (SS-CDIL) setting and propose the first algorithm for SS-CDIL with theoretical justification. Our method uses only offline data, including a small number of target expert demonstrations and some unlabeled imperfect trajectories. To handle domain discrepancy, we propose a novel cross-domain loss function for learning inter-domain state-action mappings and design an adaptive weight function to balance the source and target knowledge. Experiments on MuJoCo and Robosuite show consistent gains over the baselines, demonstrating that our approach achieves stable and data-efficient policy learning with minimal supervision. Our code is available at~ https://github.com/NYCU-RL-Bandits-Lab/CDIL.

AI.tldr: Introduces Semi-Supervised Cross-Domain Imitation Learning (SS-CDIL) with a theoretically justified algorithm that leverages offline data (a few target-domain expert demonstrations plus unlabeled imperfect trajectories) to learn inter-domain state-action mappings via a cross-domain loss and an adaptive weighting scheme, achieving stable and data-efficient policy learning with empirical gains on MuJoCo and Robosuite; code released.
AI.motivation: Cross-domain imitation learning transfers expert knowledge across domains to reduce data collection costs. Existing methods are either supervised (requiring explicit alignment/proxy tasks) or unsupervised (distribution alignment without paired data) and often suffer from instability. A semi-supervised setting that uses limited target demonstrations and unlabeled trajectories can improve data efficiency and stability.
AI.method: Proposes the first algorithm for SS-CDIL with theoretical justification. Uses only offline data: a small number of target expert demonstrations and unlabeled imperfect trajectories. Introduces a cross-domain loss for learning inter-domain state-action mappings and an adaptive weight function to balance source and target knowledge.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on MuJoCo and Robosuite shows consistent gains over baselines, achieving stable and data-efficient policy learning with minimal supervision. Code is available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: SS-CDIL provides a principled framework for semi-supervised cross-domain imitation, yielding robust transfer and improved data efficiency in challenging robotics domains.

title: Improving Medical Visual Reinforcement Fine-Tuning via Perception and Reasoning Augmentation
summary:
While recent advances in Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) have shown that rule-based reward schemes can enable effective post-training for large language models, their extension to cross-modal, vision-centric domains remains largely underexplored. This limitation is especially pronounced in the medical imaging domain, where effective performance requires both robust visual perception and structured reasoning. In this work, we address this gap by proposing VRFT-Aug, a visual reinforcement fine-tuning framework tailored for the medical domain. VRFT-Aug introduces a series of training strategies designed to augment both perception and reasoning, including prior knowledge injection, perception-driven policy refinement, medically informed reward shaping, and behavioral imitation. Together, these methods aim to stabilize and improve the RFT process.
  Through extensive experiments across multiple medical datasets, we show that our approaches consistently outperform both standard supervised fine-tuning and RFT baselines. Moreover, we provide empirically grounded insights and practical training heuristics that can be generalized to other medical image tasks. We hope this work contributes actionable guidance and fresh inspiration for the ongoing effort to develop reliable, reasoning-capable models for high-stakes medical applications.

AI.tldr: VRFT-Aug is a visual reinforcement fine-tuning framework for medical imaging that combines prior knowledge injection, perception-driven policy refinement, medically informed reward shaping, and behavioral imitation to enhance perception and reasoning, outperforming supervised fine-tuning and standard RFT baselines across medical datasets.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in applying reinforcement-based fine-tuning to vision-centric, high-stakes medical imaging tasks where robust perception and structured reasoning are critical.
AI.method: Introduce VRFT-Aug with strategies including prior knowledge injection, perception-driven policy refinement, medically informed reward shaping, and behavioral imitation, evaluated via extensive experiments on multiple medical image datasets, with practical training heuristics.
AI.result: Consistent improvement over standard supervised fine-tuning and RFT baselines; provides empirically grounded insights and training heuristics; findings generalize to other medical image tasks.
AI.conclusion: VRFT-Aug advances reliable, reasoning-capable medical vision models and offers actionable guidance for applying reinforcement-based fine-tuning in high-stakes medical applications.

title: AI-rithmetic
summary: Modern AI systems have been successfully deployed to win medals at international math competitions, assist with research workflows, and prove novel technical lemmas. However, despite their progress at advanced levels of mathematics, they remain stubbornly bad at basic arithmetic, consistently failing on the simple task of adding two numbers. We present a systematic investigation of this phenomenon. We demonstrate empirically that all frontier models suffer significantly degraded accuracy for integer addition as the number of digits increases. Furthermore, we show that most errors made by these models are highly interpretable and can be attributed to either operand misalignment or a failure to correctly carry; these two error classes explain 87.9%, 62.9%, and 92.4% of Claude Opus 4.1, GPT-5, and Gemini 2.5 Pro errors, respectively. Finally, we show that misalignment errors are frequently related to tokenization, and that carrying errors appear largely as independent random failures.

AI.tldr: Frontier models show digit-count dependent degradation in integer addition; most errors are interpretable as operand misalignment (often due to tokenization) or incorrect carrying; carrying errors appear largely as independent random failures; addressing tokenization and carry-handling could improve arithmetic reliability.
AI.motivation: To diagnose why state-of-the-art AI systems, despite strength on complex tasks, remain poor at basic arithmetic and to identify dominant error modes.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across frontier models (Claude Opus 4.1, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro) evaluating multi-digit integer addition, classifying errors into two main types (operand misalignment and carrying failures), and examining the role of tokenization and randomness in errors; quantifying the prevalence of each error class.
AI.result: Accuracy degrades as the number of digits increases; misalignment and carry errors account for the majority of mistakes (e.g., 87.9% for Claude Opus 4.1, 62.9% for GPT-5, 92.4% for Gemini 2.5 Pro). Misalignment is often linked to tokenization; carrying errors tend to behave as independent random failures.
AI.conclusion: Improving arithmetic reliability will require addressing tokenization-induced misalignment and carry-propagation failures, possibly via improved numeric-token representations, decoding strategies, or targeted training to harden carry handling.

title: Tensor Methods: A Unified and Interpretable Approach for Material Design
summary: When designing new materials, it is often necessary to tailor the material design (with respect to its design parameters) to have some desired properties (e.g. Young's modulus). As the set of design parameters grow, the search space grows exponentially, making the actual synthesis and evaluation of all material combinations virtually impossible. Even using traditional computational methods such as Finite Element Analysis becomes too computationally heavy to search the design space. Recent methods use machine learning (ML) surrogate models to more efficiently determine optimal material designs; unfortunately, these methods often (i) are notoriously difficult to interpret and (ii) under perform when the training data comes from a non-uniform sampling of the design space. We suggest the use of tensor completion methods as an all-in-one approach for interpretability and predictions. We observe classical tensor methods are able to compete with traditional ML in predictions, with the added benefit of their interpretable tensor factors (which are given completely for free, as a result of the prediction). In our experiments, we are able to rediscover physical phenomena via the tensor factors, indicating that our predictions are aligned with the true underlying physics of the problem. This also means these tensor factors could be used by experimentalists to identify potentially novel patterns, given we are able to rediscover existing ones. We also study the effects of both types of surrogate models when we encounter training data from a non-uniform sampling of the design space. We observe more specialized tensor methods that can give better generalization in these non-uniforms sampling scenarios. We find the best generalization comes from a tensor model, which is able to improve upon the baseline ML methods by up to 5% on aggregate $R^2$, and halve the error in some out of distribution regions.

AI.tldr: Tensor completion provides an interpretable, competitive surrogate for material design optimization, robust under non-uniform sampling and capable of rediscovering underlying physics.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional design spaces and expensive simulations make exhaustive search infeasible; ML surrogates lack interpretability and struggle with non-uniform data.
AI.method: Apply classical tensor completion methods to model data as tensors over design parameters; compare prediction accuracy and interpretability with traditional ML; analyze performance under non-uniform sampling; examine components for physical meaning.
AI.result: Tensor methods match or exceed ML performance; offer interpretable factors; can rediscover physical phenomena; specialized tensor methods improve generalization under non-uniform sampling; best tensor model yields up to 5% higher R^2 and halved error in some out-of-distribution regions.
AI.conclusion: Tensor-based surrogates provide a transparent, effective approach for material design optimization, with advantages in interpretability and robustness, and potential to reveal novel patterns and guide experiments.

title: Towards Learning a Generalizable 3D Scene Representation from 2D Observations
summary: We introduce a Generalizable Neural Radiance Field approach for predicting 3D workspace occupancy from egocentric robot observations. Unlike prior methods operating in camera-centric coordinates, our model constructs occupancy representations in a global workspace frame, making it directly applicable to robotic manipulation. The model integrates flexible source views and generalizes to unseen object arrangements without scene-specific finetuning. We demonstrate the approach on a humanoid robot and evaluate predicted geometry against 3D sensor ground truth. Trained on 40 real scenes, our model achieves 26mm reconstruction error, including occluded regions, validating its ability to infer complete 3D occupancy beyond traditional stereo vision methods.

AI.tldr: Generalizable Neural Radiance Field predicts global-workspace 3D occupancy from egocentric observations, enabling manipulation without scene-specific finetuning; trained on real scenes; achieves ~26 mm reconstruction error including occluded regions; validates across unseen arrangements.
AI.motivation: Enable accurate 3D occupancy prediction for robotic manipulation in a global workspace frame using egocentric inputs, avoiding camera-centric representations and scene-specific fine-tuning.
AI.method: Extend neural radiance field concepts to a global workspace coordinate system, integrate flexible source views, train on real scenes, and evaluate against 3D sensor ground truth on a humanoid robot; demonstrates reconstruction of occupancy including occluded regions without finetuning on new scenes.
AI.result: Achieves 26 mm mean reconstruction error for 3D occupancy (including occluded areas) on unseen arrangements; demonstrates successful generalization and complete 3D occupancy inference beyond stereo vision.
AI.conclusion: Generalizable GRF-based occupancy prediction from egocentric observations is feasible for robotic manipulation, enabling direct use of full 3D occupancy in manipulation pipelines without scene-specific fine-tuning.

title: A Vision-Language Foundation Model for Zero-shot Clinical Collaboration and Automated Concept Discovery in Dermatology
summary: Medical foundation models have shown promise in controlled benchmarks, yet widespread deployment remains hindered by reliance on task-specific fine-tuning. Here, we introduce DermFM-Zero, a dermatology vision-language foundation model trained via masked latent modelling and contrastive learning on over 4 million multimodal data points. We evaluated DermFM-Zero across 20 benchmarks spanning zero-shot diagnosis and multimodal retrieval, achieving state-of-the-art performance without task-specific adaptation. We further evaluated its zero-shot capabilities in three multinational reader studies involving over 1,100 clinicians. In primary care settings, AI assistance enabled general practitioners to nearly double their differential diagnostic accuracy across 98 skin conditions. In specialist settings, the model significantly outperformed board-certified dermatologists in multimodal skin cancer assessment. In collaborative workflows, AI assistance enabled non-experts to surpass unassisted experts while improving management appropriateness. Finally, we show that DermFM-Zero's latent representations are interpretable: sparse autoencoders unsupervisedly disentangle clinically meaningful concepts that outperform predefined-vocabulary approaches and enable targeted suppression of artifact-induced biases, enhancing robustness without retraining. These findings demonstrate that a foundation model can provide effective, safe, and transparent zero-shot clinical decision support.

AI.tldr: DermFM-Zero is a dermatology vision-language foundation model trained with masked latent modelling and contrastive learning on 4M+ multimodal data points, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot performance across 20 benchmarks and in clinician studies, with interpretable latent representations that suppress artifacts without retraining.
AI.motivation: Overcome reliance on task-specific fine-tuning for medical foundation models and enable zero-shot, safe, and transparent clinical decision support in dermatology.
AI.method: Train via masked latent modelling and contrastive learning on over 4 million multimodal data points. Evaluate on 20 zero-shot benchmarks. Conduct three multinational reader studies with >1,100 clinicians. Use sparse autoencoders to disentangle clinically meaningful concepts in the latent space and enable suppression of artifact-induced biases without retraining.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance across 20 benchmarks. In primary care, AI-assisted GPs nearly double differential diagnostic accuracy across 98 skin conditions. In specialists, the model significantly outperforms board-certified dermatologists in multimodal skin cancer assessment. In collaborative workflows, AI assistance helps non-experts surpass unassisted experts and improves management appropriateness. Latent representations are interpretable via sparse autoencoders that disentangle clinically meaningful concepts and allow targeted artifact suppression, enhancing robustness without retraining.
AI.conclusion: A dermatology foundation model can provide effective, safe, and transparent zero-shot clinical decision support without task-specific adaptation.

title: Equivariant Evidential Deep Learning for Interatomic Potentials
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is critical for assessing the reliability of machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) in molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, identifying extrapolation regimes and enabling uncertainty-aware workflows such as active learning for training dataset construction. Existing UQ approaches for MLIPs are often limited by high computational cost or suboptimal performance. Evidential deep learning (EDL) provides a theoretically grounded single-model alternative that determines both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in a single forward pass. However, extending evidential formulations from scalar targets to vector-valued quantities such as atomic forces introduces substantial challenges, particularly in maintaining statistical self-consistency under rotational transformations. To address this, we propose \textit{Equivariant Evidential Deep Learning for Interatomic Potentials} ($\text{e}^2$IP), a backbone-agnostic framework that models atomic forces and their uncertainty jointly by representing uncertainty as a full $3\times3$ symmetric positive definite covariance tensor that transforms equivariantly under rotations. Experiments on diverse molecular benchmarks show that $\text{e}^2$IP provides a stronger accuracy-efficiency-reliability balance than the non-equivariant evidential baseline and the widely used ensemble method. It also achieves better data efficiency through the fully equivariant architecture while retaining single-model inference efficiency.

AI.tldr: An equivariant evidential deep learning framework (e^2IP) for interatomic potentials that models force uncertainty as a 3×3 symmetric positive definite covariance tensor, improving accuracy, efficiency, and reliability over non-equivariant evidential DL and ensembles.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) for machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) in molecular dynamics (MD) is crucial for reliability, extrapolation detection, and uncertainty-aware workflows. Existing UQ methods are computationally expensive or limited. Extending evidential deep learning (EDL) from scalar targets to vector quantities like forces requires maintaining rotational self-consistency. A backbone-agnostic, equivariant approach is needed to jointly predict forces and their uncertainties.
AI.method: Propose e^2IP, a backbone-agnostic framework that jointly models atomic forces and their uncertainty by predicting a full 3×3 symmetric positive definite covariance tensor that transforms equivariantly under rotations. Builds on evidential DL to capture both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in a single forward pass, ensuring rotational self-consistency.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on diverse molecular benchmarks shows that e^2IP achieves a superior balance of accuracy, efficiency, and reliability compared with a non-equivariant evidential baseline and with ensemble methods. The fully equivariant architecture also yields better data efficiency while maintaining single-model inference capability.
AI.conclusion: e^2IP provides principled, efficient uncertainty quantification for MLIPs in MD, delivering improved performance and data efficiency through a backbone-agnostic, rotation-equivariant design that jointly models forces and their covariance uncertainty.

title: Experimental Demonstration of Online Learning-Based Concept Drift Adaptation for Failure Detection in Optical Networks
comment: Accepted at Optical Fiber Communications Conference 2026 (OFC 2026)
summary: We present a novel online learning-based approach for concept drift adaptation in optical network failure detection, achieving up to a 70% improvement in performance over conventional static models while maintaining low latency.

AI.tldr: An online learning method for concept drift in optical network failure detection yields up to 70% performance gains over static models with low latency.
AI.motivation: Static models degrade in the presence of concept drift in optical network failure data, leading to reduced detection accuracy and more false alarms. There is a need for real-time adaptation that maintains low inference latency in dynamic networks.
AI.method: An online learning-based drift-adaptation framework that updates the detector incrementally on streaming data, with drift-awareness mechanisms to maintain accuracy under changing failure patterns while preserving low latency.
AI.result: The approach achieves up to a 70% improvement in performance compared with conventional static models, while keeping inference latency low.
AI.conclusion: Online drift-adaptive detectors are viable for optical networks, delivering substantial performance gains without compromising real-time operation, enabling more reliable failure detection in dynamic environments.

title: Enhancing Predictability of Multi-Tenant DNN Inference for Autonomous Vehicles' Perception
summary: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) rely on sensors and deep neural networks (DNNs) to perceive their surrounding environment and make maneuver decisions in real time. However, achieving real-time DNN inference in the AV's perception pipeline is challenging due to the large gap between the computation requirement and the AV's limited resources. Most, if not all, of existing studies focus on optimizing the DNN inference time to achieve faster perception by compressing the DNN model with pruning and quantization. In contrast, we present a Predictable Perception system with DNNs (PP-DNN) that reduce the amount of image data to be processed while maintaining the same level of accuracy for multi-tenant DNNs by dynamically selecting critical frames and regions of interest (ROIs). PP-DNN is based on our key insight that critical frames and ROIs for AVs vary with the AV's surrounding environment. However, it is challenging to identify and use critical frames and ROIs in multi-tenant DNNs for predictable inference. Given image-frame streams, PP-DNN leverages an ROI generator to identify critical frames and ROIs based on the similarities of consecutive frames and traffic scenarios. PP-DNN then leverages a FLOPs predictor to predict multiply-accumulate operations (MACs) from the dynamic critical frames and ROIs. The ROI scheduler coordinates the processing of critical frames and ROIs with multiple DNN models. Finally, we design a detection predictor for the perception of non-critical frames. We have implemented PP-DNN in an ROS-based AV pipeline and evaluated it with the BDD100K and the nuScenes dataset. PP-DNN is observed to significantly enhance perception predictability, increasing the number of fusion frames by up to 7.3x, reducing the fusion delay by >2.6x and fusion-delay variations by >2.3x, improving detection completeness by 75.4% and the cost-effectiveness by up to 98% over the baseline.

AI.tldr: A dynamic data-reduction framework (PP-DNN) for autonomous-vehicle perception that selects critical frames/ROIs to reduce compute while preserving accuracy, with components ROI generator, FLOPs predictor, ROI scheduler, and non-critical-frame predictor; demonstrates large improvements in fusion frame count, latency, and detection completeness on BDD100K/nuScenes in a ROS-based pipeline.
AI.motivation: Real-time DNN inference in AVs is bottlenecked by the mismatch between computation demand and limited hardware resources. Unlike prior work focusing on model compression, PP-DNN aims for predictable inference by reducing image data processed while maintaining accuracy across multi-tenant DNNs, adapting to environmental context.
AI.method: Identify critical frames and ROIs using an ROI generator based on frame-to-frame similarities and traffic scenarios; predict MACs with a FLOPs predictor for dynamic frames/ROIs; coordinate processing with an ROI scheduler across multiple DNN models; use a detection predictor for non-critical frames; implement within an ROS-based AV pipeline and evaluate on BDD100K and nuScenes datasets.
AI.result: PP-DNN significantly enhances perception predictability: up to 7.3x more fusion frames; fusion delay reduced by >2.6x; fusion-delay variation reduced by >2.3x; detection completeness improved by 75.4%; cost-effectiveness improved up to 98% over baseline.
AI.conclusion: PP-DNN demonstrates a practical approach to predictable, data-driven computation reduction in AV perception, achieving substantial latency and accuracy improvements in multi-tenant DNN settings and validated on standard AV datasets.

title: Eliminating VAE for Fast and High-Resolution Generative Detail Restoration
summary: Diffusion models have attained remarkable breakthroughs in the real-world super-resolution (SR) task, albeit at slow inference and high demand on devices. To accelerate inference, recent works like GenDR adopt step distillation to minimize the step number to one. However, the memory boundary still restricts the maximum processing size, necessitating tile-by-tile restoration of high-resolution images. Through profiling the pipeline, we pinpoint that the variational auto-encoder (VAE) is the bottleneck of latency and memory. To completely solve the problem, we leverage pixel-(un)shuffle operations to eliminate the VAE, reversing the latent-based GenDR to pixel-space GenDR-Pix. However, upscale with x8 pixelshuffle may induce artifacts of repeated patterns. To alleviate the distortion, we propose a multi-stage adversarial distillation to progressively remove the encoder and decoder. Specifically, we utilize generative features from the previous stage models to guide adversarial discrimination. Moreover, we propose random padding to augment generative features and avoid discriminator collapse. We also introduce a masked Fourier space loss to penalize the outliers of amplitude. To improve inference performance, we empirically integrate a padding-based self-ensemble with classifier-free guidance to improve inference scaling. Experimental results show that GenDR-Pix performs 2.8x acceleration and 60% memory-saving compared to GenDR with negligible visual degradation, surpassing other one-step diffusion SR. Against all odds, GenDR-Pix can restore 4K image in only 1 second and 6GB.

AI.tldr: GenDR-Pix eliminates VAE-based latency by shifting diffusion SR to pixel space via pixel-(un)shuffle, with multi-stage adversarial distillation, padding augmentations, and Fourier-based loss, achieving 2.8x speed and 60% memory savings for 4K SR with minimal quality loss.
AI.motivation: Address the memory and latency bottlenecks in diffusion-based SR, especially the VAE bottleneck and tile-wise processing limits for high-resolution images.
AI.method: Replace VAE by pixel-space diffusion using pixel-(un)shuffle; multi-stage adversarial distillation guided by features from prior stages; random padding to diversify features; masked Fourier space loss to penalize amplitude outliers; padding-based self-ensemble and classifier-free guidance to boost inference throughput.
AI.result: GenDR-Pix achieves 2.8x acceleration and 60% memory savings vs GenDR, with negligible visual degradation; outperforms existing one-step diffusion SR methods; capable of restoring 4K images in ~1s on 6GB memory.
AI.conclusion: Pixel-space diffusion with staged adversarial distillation enables fast, memory-efficient SR for high-resolution images without compromising quality, enabling practical 4K SR in real-time.

title: Breaking the Curse of Repulsion: Optimistic Distributionally Robust Policy Optimization for Off-Policy Generative Recommendation
summary: Policy-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) has established itself as the dominant paradigm in generative recommendation for optimizing sequential user interactions. However, when applied to offline historical logs, these methods suffer a critical failure: the dominance of low-quality data induces severe model collapse. We first establish the Divergence Theory of Repulsive Optimization, revealing that negative gradient updates inherently trigger exponential intensity explosion during off-policy training. This theory elucidates the inherent dilemma of existing methods, exposing their inability to reconcile variance reduction and noise imitation. To break this curse, we argue that the solution lies in rigorously identifying the latent high-quality distribution entangled within the noisy behavior policy. Accordingly, we reformulate the objective as an Optimistic Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) problem. Guided by this formulation, we propose Distributionally Robust Policy Optimization (DRPO). We prove that hard filtering is the exact solution to this DRO objective, enabling DRPO to optimally recover high-quality behaviors while strictly discarding divergence-inducing noise. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DRPO achieves state-of-the-art performance on mixed-quality recommendation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Proposes Distributionally Robust Policy Optimization (DRPO) to address off-policy collapse in policy-based RL for recommendations by reframing the objective as an optimistic distributionally robust optimization, with hard filtering proven as the exact solution; achieves state-of-the-art performance on mixed-quality benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Offline historical logs contain a mix of high- and low-quality interactions that cause policy-based RL to collapse due to divergence and noise imitation; there is a need to identify and leverage latent high-quality distribution within noisy data.
AI.method: Formulates the learning objective as an Optimistic Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) problem; introduces DRPO; proves that hard filtering is the exact solution to the DRO objective, enabling suppression of divergence-inducing noise while recovering high-quality behaviors.
AI.result: The approach yields state-of-the-art performance on mixed-quality recommendation benchmarks, outperforming existing methods by effectively filtering noise and stabilizing off-policy training.
AI.conclusion: Hard filtering is the exact solution to the proposed DRO objective, enabling robust recovery of high-quality behaviors in offline, mixed-quality settings; DRPO provides both theoretical justification and empirical gains for distributionally robust policy optimization in recommender systems.

title: VideoSTF: Stress-Testing Output Repetition in Video Large Language Models
summary: Video Large Language Models (VideoLLMs) have recently achieved strong performance in video understanding tasks. However, we identify a previously underexplored generation failure: severe output repetition, where models degenerate into self-reinforcing loops of repeated phrases or sentences. This failure mode is not captured by existing VideoLLM benchmarks, which focus primarily on task accuracy and factual correctness. We introduce VideoSTF, the first framework for systematically measuring and stress-testing output repetition in VideoLLMs. VideoSTF formalizes repetition using three complementary n-gram-based metrics and provides a standardized testbed of 10,000 diverse videos together with a library of controlled temporal transformations. Using VideoSTF, we conduct pervasive testing, temporal stress testing, and adversarial exploitation across 10 advanced VideoLLMs. We find that output repetition is widespread and, critically, highly sensitive to temporal perturbations of video inputs. Moreover, we show that simple temporal transformations can efficiently induce repetitive degeneration in a black-box setting, exposing output repetition as an exploitable security vulnerability. Our results reveal output repetition as a fundamental stability issue in modern VideoLLMs and motivate stability-aware evaluation for video-language systems. Our evaluation code and scripts are available at: https://github.com/yuxincao22/VideoSTF_benchmark.

AI.tldr: VideoLLMs exhibit a severe output repetition failure not captured by current benchmarks. The paper introduces VideoSTF, a framework to measure and stress-test repetition using three n-gram metrics, a 10k-video testbed, and temporal transformations. Findings show repetition is widespread, highly sensitive to temporal input perturbations, and can be triggered adversarially in black-box settings, highlighting a stability vulnerability and need for stability-aware evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current VideoLLMs are evaluated mainly on task accuracy and factual correctness; output repetition is a neglected failure mode that undermines reliability and safety. A systematic evaluation framework is needed to quantify, stress-test, and mitigate repetition.
AI.method: Propose VideoSTF, a framework that (1) formalizes repetition with three complementary n-gram-based metrics, (2) provides a standardized testbed of 10,000 diverse videos, and (3) includes a library of controlled temporal transformations. Conduct pervasive, temporal stress, and adversarial testing across 10 state-of-the-art VideoLLMs.
AI.result: Repetition is widespread in VideoLLMs and highly sensitive to temporal perturbations. Simple temporal transformations can induce repetitive degeneration even in black-box settings, exposing a security vulnerability. The study emphasizes repetition as a fundamental stability issue and demonstrates the need for stability-aware evaluation; code is released.
AI.conclusion: Output repetition is a core stability problem for VideoLLMs. The VideoSTF benchmark enables systematic measurement and stress-testing, guiding future robust design and evaluation of video-language models.

title: A Dual-Stream Physics-Augmented Unsupervised Architecture for Runtime Embedded Vehicle Health Monitoring
summary: Runtime quantification of vehicle operational intensity is essential for predictive maintenance and condition monitoring in commercial and heavy-duty fleets. Traditional metrics like mileage fail to capture mechanical burden, while unsupervised deep learning models detect statistical anomalies, typically transient surface shocks, but often conflate statistical stability with mechanical rest. We identify this as a critical blind spot: high-load steady states, such as hill climbing with heavy payloads, appear statistically normal yet impose significant drivetrain fatigue. To resolve this, we propose a Dual-Stream Architecture that fuses unsupervised learning for surface anomaly detection with macroscopic physics proxies for cumulative load estimation. This approach leverages low-frequency sensor data to generate a multi-dimensional health vector, distinguishing between dynamic hazards and sustained mechanical effort. Validated on a RISC-V embedded platform, the architecture demonstrates low computational overhead, enabling comprehensive, edge-based health monitoring on resource-constrained ECUs without the latency or bandwidth costs of cloud-based monitoring.

AI.tldr: A dual-stream, edge-enabled architecture fuses unsupervised surface anomaly detection with macroscopic physics proxies to estimate cumulative drivetrain load and fatigue, addressing the blind spot where high-load steady states appear normal statistically; validated on a low-power RISC-V platform with low overhead for edge health monitoring.
AI.motivation: Traditional metrics like mileage miss sustained mechanical burden; unsupervised DL often conflates statistical stability with mechanical rest, failing to detect high-load steady states (e.g., hill climbing with heavy payload) that cause fatigue.
AI.method: Introduce a Dual-Stream Architecture combining unsupervised surface anomaly detection with macroscopic physics proxies for cumulative load estimation. Uses low-frequency sensor data to create a multi-dimensional health vector, separating dynamic hazards from sustained mechanical effort, and operates at the edge on resource-constrained ECUs.
AI.result: Demonstrates low computational overhead and edge-based health monitoring capability validated on a RISC-V platform, avoiding cloud latency/bandwidth costs.
AI.conclusion: The approach resolves the blind spot of high-load steady states by integrating physics-informed proxies with surface anomaly detection, enabling robust, edge-based predictive maintenance for fleets with limited compute resources.

title: Gated Removal of Normalization in Transformers Enables Stable Training and Efficient Inference
summary: Normalization is widely viewed as essential for stabilizing Transformer training. We revisit this assumption for pre-norm Transformers and ask to what extent sample-dependent normalization is needed inside Transformer blocks. We introduce TaperNorm, a drop-in replacement for RMSNorm/LayerNorm that behaves exactly like the standard normalizer early in training and then smoothly tapers to a learned sample-independent linear/affine map. A single global gate is held at $g{=}1$ during gate warmup, used to calibrate the scaling branch via EMAs, and then cosine-decayed to $g{=}0$, at which point per-token statistics vanish and the resulting fixed scalings can be folded into adjacent linear projections. Our theoretical and empirical results isolate scale anchoring as the key role played by output normalization: as a (near) $0$-homogeneous map it removes radial gradients at the output, whereas without such an anchor cross-entropy encourages unbounded logit growth (``logit chasing''). We further show that a simple fixed-target auxiliary loss on the pre-logit residual-stream scale provides an explicit alternative anchor and can aid removal of the final normalization layer. Empirically, TaperNorm matches normalized baselines under identical setups while eliminating per-token statistics and enabling these layers to be folded into adjacent linear projections at inference. On an efficiency microbenchmark, folding internal scalings yields up to $1.22\times$ higher throughput in last-token logits mode. These results take a step towards norm-free Transformers while identifying the special role output normalization plays.

AI.tldr: Introduces TaperNorm, a drop-in norm that starts like RMSNorm/LayerNorm and gradually becomes a learned, sample-independent affine map, enabling folding and potential norm-free Transformers while matching baselines and improving efficiency.
AI.motivation: Reassess the necessity of sample-dependent normalization inside pre-norm Transformers and identify the key role of output normalization (scale anchoring) in stabilizing training and preventing logit chasing.
AI.method: Proposes TaperNorm with a global gate g that is 1 during warmup, calibrated via EMA; cosine decay to g=0; sample statistics vanish and final scaling parameters can be folded into adjacent projections; adds a fixed-target auxiliary loss on pre-logit residual-stream scale as an explicit anchor.
AI.result: TaperNorm matches baseline accuracy under identical settings, removes per-token statistics, enables folding of scalings into linear projections, and yields up to 1.22x higher throughput in last-token logits mode; analysis shows scale anchoring is the key.
AI.conclusion: Output normalization plays a special role; achieving norm-free Transformers hinges on anchoring scale; auxiliary loss offers an explicit anchor; practical gains through folding scalings; this work clarifies the role of normalization.

title: Multimodal Priors-Augmented Text-Driven 3D Human-Object Interaction Generation
summary: We address the challenging task of text-driven 3D human-object interaction (HOI) motion generation. Existing methods primarily rely on a direct text-to-HOI mapping, which suffers from three key limitations due to the significant cross-modality gap: (Q1) sub-optimal human motion, (Q2) unnatural object motion, and (Q3) weak interaction between humans and objects. To address these challenges, we propose MP-HOI, a novel framework grounded in four core insights: (1) Multimodal Data Priors: We leverage multimodal data (text, image, pose/object) from large multimodal models as priors to guide HOI generation, which tackles Q1 and Q2 in data modeling. (2) Enhanced Object Representation: We improve existing object representations by incorporating geometric keypoints, contact features, and dynamic properties, enabling expressive object representations, which tackles Q2 in data representation. (3) Multimodal-Aware Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Model: We propose a modality-aware MoE model for effective multimodal feature fusion paradigm, which tackles Q1 and Q2 in feature fusion. (4) Cascaded Diffusion with Interaction Supervision: We design a cascaded diffusion framework that progressively refines human-object interaction features under dedicated supervision, which tackles Q3 in interaction refinement. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that MP-HOI outperforms existing approaches in generating high-fidelity and fine-grained HOI motions.

AI.tldr: MP-HOI proposes a multimodal priors-guided cascaded diffusion framework for text-driven 3D HOI motion generation, featuring enhanced object representation and a multimodal-aware MoE for feature fusion, plus interaction-supervised cascaded diffusion, achieving superior fidelity and interaction realism.
AI.motivation: Bridging the cross-modality gap in text-to-HOI generation to address sub-optimal motion, unnatural object motion, and weak human–object interaction by leveraging multimodal priors, richer object representations, and robust fusion and refinement strategies.
AI.method: Four core insights: (1) use multimodal data priors from large multimodal models as priors; (2) enhance object representation with geometric keypoints, contact features, and dynamic properties; (3) modality-aware mixture-of-experts for multimodal feature fusion; (4) cascaded diffusion with interaction supervision to progressively refine HOI features under supervision.
AI.result: Experiments show MP-HOI outperforms existing approaches in generating high-fidelity and fine-grained HOI motions.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively addresses Q1–Q3 by integrating multimodal priors, richer object representations, a multimodal-aware fusion model, and interaction-supervised cascaded diffusion, yielding improved fidelity and interaction realism.

title: LUCID: Attention with Preconditioned Representations
summary: Softmax-based dot-product attention is a cornerstone of Transformer architectures, enabling remarkable capabilities such as in-context learning. However, as context lengths increase, a fundamental limitation of the softmax function emerges: it tends to diffuse probability mass to irrelevant tokens degrading performance in long-sequence scenarios. Furthermore, attempts to sharpen focus by lowering softmax temperature hinder learnability due to vanishing gradients. We introduce LUCID Attention, an architectural modification that applies a preconditioner to the attention probabilities. This preconditioner, derived from exponentiated key-key similarities, minimizes overlap between the keys in a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space, thus allowing the query to focus on important keys among large number of keys accurately with same computational complexity as standard attention. Additionally, LUCID's preconditioning-based approach to retrieval bypasses the need for low temperature and the learnability problems associated with it. We validate our approach by training ~1 billion parameter language models evaluated on up to 128K tokens. Our results demonstrate significant gains on long-context retrieval tasks, specifically retrieval tasks from BABILong, RULER, SCROLLS and LongBench. For instance, LUCID achieves up to 18% improvement in BABILong and 14% improvement in RULER multi-needle performance compared to standard attention.

AI.tldr: LUCID Attention introduces a preconditioner for attention probabilities based on exponentiated key-key similarities, minimizing key overlap in RKHS to focus the query on important keys with the same computational cost as standard attention, addressing softmax diffusion and gradient issues.
AI.motivation: Softmax-based attention diffuses probability mass in long contexts and sharp-then-learnability through low temperatures hampers training. A scalable solution that improves long-context retrieval without changing computational complexity is needed.
AI.method: Apply a preconditioner to attention probabilities derived from exponentiated key-key similarities. This preconditioner reduces overlap between keys in a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space, enabling the query to concentrate on relevant keys among many keys while preserving the same computational complexity as standard attention.
AI.result: Empirical validation on language models of ~1B parameters evaluated up to 128K tokens shows notable gains on long-context retrieval benchmarks, with up to 18% improvement on BABILong and 14% on RULER multi-needle tasks; improvements also observed on SCROLLS and LongBench.
AI.conclusion: LUCID provides effective long-context retrieval by reweighting attention through a kernel-based preconditioner, avoiding low-temperature training issues and maintaining standard-attention computational costs, with strong empirical gains on established long-context benchmarks.

title: AurigaNet: A Real-Time Multi-Task Network for Enhanced Urban Driving Perception
summary: Self-driving cars hold significant potential to reduce traffic accidents, alleviate congestion, and enhance urban mobility. However, developing reliable AI systems for autonomous vehicles remains a substantial challenge. Over the past decade, multi-task learning has emerged as a powerful approach to address complex problems in driving perception. Multi-task networks offer several advantages, including increased computational efficiency, real-time processing capabilities, optimized resource utilization, and improved generalization. In this study, we present AurigaNet, an advanced multi-task network architecture designed to push the boundaries of autonomous driving perception. AurigaNet integrates three critical tasks: object detection, lane detection, and drivable area instance segmentation. The system is trained and evaluated using the BDD100K dataset, renowned for its diversity in driving conditions. Key innovations of AurigaNet include its end-to-end instance segmentation capability, which significantly enhances both accuracy and efficiency in path estimation for autonomous vehicles. Experimental results demonstrate that AurigaNet achieves an 85.2% IoU in drivable area segmentation, outperforming its closest competitor by 0.7%. In lane detection, AurigaNet achieves a remarkable 60.8% IoU, surpassing other models by more than 30%. Furthermore, the network achieves an mAP@0.5:0.95 of 47.6% in traffic object detection, exceeding the next leading model by 2.9%. Additionally, we validate the practical feasibility of AurigaNet by deploying it on embedded devices such as the Jetson Orin NX, where it demonstrates competitive real-time performance. These results underscore AurigaNet's potential as a robust and efficient solution for autonomous driving perception systems. The code can be found here https://github.com/KiaRational/AurigaNet.

AI.tldr: AurigaNet is a unified multi-task network for autonomous driving perception that jointly handles object detection, lane detection, and drivable area instance segmentation. Trained on BDD100K, it achieves strong IoU and mAP metrics and supports real-time deployment on embedded devices; code is released.
AI.motivation: Leverage multi-task learning to improve perception accuracy, efficiency, and generalization in autonomous driving by unifying multiple perception tasks into a single network.
AI.method: Proposes AurigaNet, a multi-task architecture that jointly performs object detection, lane detection, and drivable area instance segmentation. It emphasizes end-to-end instance segmentation and deployment feasibility on embedded hardware (e.g., Jetson Orin NX) using a shared backbone with task-specific heads and end-to-end optimization. Trained and evaluated on the BDD100K dataset.
AI.result: Quantitative results show drivable area IoU of 85.2% (+0.7 over the closest competitor), lane IoU of 60.8% (over 30% higher than other methods), and mAP@0.5:0.95 of 47.6% (+2.9) for traffic object detection. The approach is demonstrated to run in real time on embedded devices like Jetson Orin NX.
AI.conclusion: AurigaNet demonstrates robust and efficient performance in autonomous driving perception, improving accuracy across detection, lane, and drivable-area segmentation tasks, with practical feasibility for on-board deployment and an open-source release.

title: LakeMLB: Data Lake Machine Learning Benchmark
summary: Modern data lakes have emerged as foundational platforms for large-scale machine learning, enabling flexible storage of heterogeneous data and structured analytics through table-oriented abstractions. Despite their growing importance, standardized benchmarks for evaluating machine learning performance in data lake environments remain scarce. To address this gap, we present LakeMLB (Data Lake Machine Learning Benchmark), designed for the most common multi-source, multi-table scenarios in data lakes. LakeMLB focuses on two representative multi-table scenarios, Union and Join, and provides three real-world datasets for each scenario, covering government open data, finance, Wikipedia, and online marketplaces. The benchmark supports three representative integration strategies: pre-training-based, data augmentation-based, and feature augmentation-based approaches. We conduct extensive experiments with state-of-the-art tabular learning methods, offering insights into their performance under complex data lake scenarios. We release both datasets and code to facilitate rigorous research on machine learning in data lake ecosystems; the benchmark is available at https://github.com/zhengwang100/LakeMLB.

AI.tldr: LakeMLB is a data-lake ML benchmark for multi-table scenarios (Union/Join) with 3 real-world datasets per scenario, 3 integration strategies, and released code.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of standardized benchmarks for ML performance in data lakes and multi-source, multi-table settings.
AI.method: Define two representative scenarios (Union, Join); assemble three real-world datasets for each scenario; implement three integration strategies (pre-training-based, data augmentation-based, feature augmentation-based); evaluate state-of-the-art tabular models.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across modern tabular methods; insights into performance under data-lake complexity; dataset and code release to enable reproducible research.
AI.conclusion: LakeMLB provides a standardized, extensible benchmark to advance ML in data lake ecosystems and guide method development.

title: LightGTS-Cov: Covariate-Enhanced Time Series Forecasting
summary: Time series foundation models are typically pre-trained on large, multi-source datasets; however, they often ignore exogenous covariates or incorporate them via simple concatenation with the target series, which limits their effectiveness in covariate-rich applications such as electricity price forecasting and renewable energy forecasting. We introduce LightGTS-Cov, a covariate-enhanced extension of LightGTS that preserves its lightweight, period-aware backbone while explicitly incorporating both past and future-known covariates. Built on a $\sim$1M-parameter LightGTS backbone, LightGTS-Cov adds only a $\sim$0.1M-parameter MLP plug-in that integrates time-aligned covariates into the target forecasts by residually refining the outputs of the decoding process. Across covariate-aware benchmarks on electricity price and energy generation datasets, LightGTS-Cov consistently outperforms LightGTS and achieves superior performance over other covariate-aware baselines under both settings, regardless of whether future-known covariates are provided. We further demonstrate its practical value in two real-world energy case applications: long-term photovoltaic power forecasting with future weather forecasts and day-ahead electricity price forecasting with weather and dispatch-plan covariates. Across both applications, LightGTS-Cov achieves strong forecasting accuracy and stable operational performance after deployment, validating its effectiveness in real-world industrial settings.

AI.tldr: LightGTS-Cov adds a small MLP plug-in to LightGTS to explicitly incorporate past and future-known covariates via residually refining the decoder outputs, achieving strong covariate-aware forecasting with minimal parameter overhead.
AI.motivation: Current time series foundation models often neglect exogenous covariates or include them simply by concatenation, limiting performance in covariate-rich tasks like electricity price and renewable energy forecasting.
AI.method: Introduce LightGTS-Cov with ~1M parameter backbone and ~0.1M parameter MLP plug-in that aligns covariates time-wise and residually refines decoder outputs; works with/without future-known covariates.
AI.result: Covariate-aware benchmarks show LightGTS-Cov outperforms LightGTS and other baselines across datasets; real-world energy applications demonstrate accurate long-term PV forecasting with weather forecasts and day-ahead electricity price forecasting with weather/dispatch-plan covariates; stable deployment performance.
AI.conclusion: Efficient, effective covariate integration for time-series foundation models; maintains lightweight backbone while improving covariate handling and deployment reliability in industrial settings.

title: Dynamic Frequency Modulation for Controllable Text-driven Image Generation
summary: The success of text-guided diffusion models has established a new image generation paradigm driven by the iterative refinement of text prompts. However, modifying the original text prompt to achieve the expected semantic adjustments often results in unintended global structure changes that disrupt user intent. Existing methods rely on empirical feature map selection for intervention, whose performance heavily depends on appropriate selection, leading to suboptimal stability. This paper tries to solve the aforementioned problem from a frequency perspective and analyzes the impact of the frequency spectrum of noisy latent variables on the hierarchical emergence of the structure framework and fine-grained textures during the generation process. We find that lower-frequency components are primarily responsible for establishing the structure framework in the early generation stage. Their influence diminishes over time, giving way to higher-frequency components that synthesize fine-grained textures. In light of this, we propose a training-free frequency modulation method utilizing a frequency-dependent weighting function with dynamic decay. This method maintains the structure framework consistency while permitting targeted semantic modifications. By directly manipulating the noisy latent variable, the proposed method avoids the empirical selection of internal feature maps. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods, achieving an effective balance between preserving structure and enabling semantic updates.
