AI.tldr: A training-free frequency-modulation approach for text-guided diffusion that directly modulates latent frequencies to preserve global structure while enabling semantic edits, addressing instability from prompt edits and eliminating empirical feature-map selection; it outperforms SOTA.
AI.motivation: Prompt edits in diffusion models often disrupt the image's global structure due to reliance on empirical feature-map interventions. A stable, training-free method that decouples structure from fine textures is needed.
AI.method: Analyze the frequency spectrum of noisy latents to separate structure (low-frequency) and texture (high-frequency) emergence. Propose a frequency-dependent weighting function with dynamic decay applied directly to the latent variables during generation, avoiding internal feature-map selection and training.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show this approach significantly surpasses current state-of-the-art methods, achieving a robust balance between preserving the structural framework and enabling targeted semantic modifications.
AI.conclusion: Frequency-domain latent modulation offers a training-free, structure-preserving, and semantically controllable intervention for text-guided diffusion, outperforming prior feature-map-based methods and reducing unintended global changes.

title: A Unified Theory of Random Projection for Influence Functions
summary:
Influence functions and related data attribution scores take the form of $g^{\top}F^{-1}g^{\prime}$, where $F\succeq 0$ is a curvature operator. In modern overparameterized models, forming or inverting $F\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times d}$ is prohibitive, motivating scalable influence computation via random projection with a sketch $P \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times d}$. This practice is commonly justified via the Johnson--Lindenstrauss (JL) lemma, which ensures approximate preservation of Euclidean geometry for a fixed dataset. However, JL does not address how sketching behaves under inversion. Furthermore, there is no existing theory that explains how sketching interacts with other widely-used techniques, such as ridge regularization and structured curvature approximations.
  We develop a unified theory characterizing when projection provably preserves influence functions. When $g,g^{\prime}\in\text{range}(F)$, we show that: 1) Unregularized projection: exact preservation holds iff $P$ is injective on $\text{range}(F)$, which necessitates $m\geq \text{rank}(F)$; 2) Regularized projection: ridge regularization fundamentally alters the sketching barrier, with approximation guarantees governed by the effective dimension of $F$ at the regularization scale; 3) Factorized influence: for Kronecker-factored curvatures $F=A\otimes E$, the guarantees continue to hold for decoupled sketches $P=P_A\otimes P_E$, even though such sketches exhibit row correlations that violate i.i.d. assumptions. Beyond this range-restricted setting, we analyze out-of-range test gradients and quantify a \emph{leakage} term that arises when test gradients have components in $\ker(F)$. This yields guarantees for influence queries on general test points.
  Overall, this work develops a novel theory that characterizes when projection provably preserves influence and provides principled guidance for choosing the sketch size in practice.

AI.tldr: A unified theory for when sketching preserves influence scores under projection. Unregularized projection preserves exactly if the sketch is injective on the curvature range (m ≥ rank(F)); ridge regularization changes the barrier via the effective dimension of F, altering guarantees; for Kronecker-factored curvatures F = A⊗E, decoupled sketches P = P_A⊗P_E still preserve guarantees despite row correlations; beyond these, a leakage term quantifies error for test gradients outside ker(F); provides guidance on practical sketch size.
AI.motivation: Influence functions and related data-attribution scores require inverting or factorizing curvature operators F, which is prohibitive in overparameterized models. Sketching via a random projection P aims to scale influence computation, but the interaction with inversion, regularization, and structured (Kronecker) approximations is not theoretically understood. The work seeks a unified theory to characterize when projection provably preserves influence and how to choose sketch size accordingly.
AI.method: Develop a theory for influence-preserving projections under sketching. (i) Unregularized case: prove exact preservation of g^T F^{-1} g' iff P is injective on range(F). This implies m ≥ rank(F). (ii) Regularized case: analyze ridge-regularized F^{-1} and show approximation quality depends on the effective dimension of F at the regularization scale. (iii) Factorized curvature: for F = A⊗E, show that decoupled sketches P = P_A⊗P_E preserve guarantees even when row correlations violate i.i.d. assumptions. (iv) Beyond range-restricted setting: introduce a leakage term arising from test gradients with components in ker(F) and derive guarantees for general test points.
AI.result: Provides a principled set of conditions under which projection preserves influence estimates. Establishes that sketch size should be at least rank(F) in the unregularized case; regularization shifts guarantees to depend on the effective dimension; Kronecker-factored curvature allows decoupled sketches to retain guarantees; introduces a leakage term to handle out-of-range test gradients and generalizes guarantees to arbitrary test points. The work yields practical guidance for choosing sketch size and underscores how regularization and structure affect sketching performance.
AI.conclusion: The paper offers a cohesive framework identifying when projection preserves influence scores under sketching, linking sketch size to rank or effective dimension and showing robustness under Kronecker-structured curvature with decoupled sketches. It also extends the theory to non-range test gradients via a leakage term, providing actionable guidance for applying sketching in influence computations in modern models.

title: AMAP-APP: Efficient Segmentation and Morphometry Quantification of Fluorescent Microscopy Images of Podocytes
summary:
Background: Automated podocyte foot process quantification is vital for kidney research, but the established "Automatic Morphological Analysis of Podocytes" (AMAP) method is hindered by high computational demands, a lack of a user interface, and Linux dependency. We developed AMAP-APP, a cross-platform desktop application designed to overcome these barriers.
  Methods: AMAP-APP optimizes efficiency by replacing intensive instance segmentation with classic image processing while retaining the original semantic segmentation model. It introduces a refined Region of Interest (ROI) algorithm to improve precision. Validation involved 365 mouse and human images (STED and confocal), benchmarking performance against the original AMAP via Pearson correlation and Two One-Sided T-tests (TOST).
  Results: AMAP-APP achieved a 147-fold increase in processing speed on consumer hardware. Morphometric outputs (area, perimeter, circularity, and slit diaphragm density) showed high correlation (r>0.90) and statistical equivalence (TOST P<0.05) to the original method. Additionally, the new ROI algorithm demonstrated superior accuracy compared to the original, showing reduced deviation from manual delineations.
  Conclusion: AMAP-APP democratizes deep learning-based podocyte morphometry. By eliminating the need for high-performance computing clusters and providing a user-friendly interface for Windows, macOS, and Linux, it enables widespread adoption in nephrology research and potential clinical diagnostics.

AI.tldr: AMAP-APP is a cross-platform desktop tool that speeds podocyte morphometry by replacing heavy instance segmentation with classic image processing while retaining the semantic segmentation backbone, plus a refined ROI; validated on 365 STED/confocal images, showing 147x speedup and high agreement (r>0.90) with the original and improved ROI accuracy.
AI.motivation: Overcome AMAP's high computational demands, lack of a user interface, and Linux dependency to democratize podocyte morphometry for nephrology research and potential clinical diagnostics.
AI.method: Maintain the original semantic segmentation model but substitute intensive instance segmentation with classic image processing. Introduce a refined ROI algorithm to improve precision. Validate on 365 mouse and human images (STED and confocal), comparing performance to the original AMAP using Pearson correlation and TOST for equivalence.
AI.result: AMAP-APP achieves a 147-fold speedup on consumer hardware. Morphometric outputs (area, perimeter, circularity, slit diaphragm density) show high correlation (r>0.90) and statistical equivalence (TOST P<0.05) to AMAP. The refined ROI algorithm yields superior accuracy, with reduced deviation from manual delineations.
AI.conclusion: AMAP-APP democratizes deep learning-based podocyte morphometry by eliminating the need for HPC clusters and providing a cross-platform, user-friendly interface, enabling broader adoption in nephrology research and potential clinical diagnostics.

title: Constructing Industrial-Scale Optimization Modeling Benchmark
summary: Optimization modeling underpins decision-making in logistics, manufacturing, energy, and finance, yet translating natural-language requirements into correct optimization formulations and solver-executable code remains labor-intensive. Although large language models (LLMs) have been explored for this task, evaluation is still dominated by toy-sized or synthetic benchmarks, masking the difficulty of industrial problems with $10^{3}$--$10^{6}$ (or more) variables and constraints. A key bottleneck is the lack of benchmarks that align natural-language specifications with reference formulations/solver code grounded in real optimization models. To fill in this gap, we introduce MIPLIB-NL, built via a structure-aware reverse construction methodology from real mixed-integer linear programs in MIPLIB~2017. Our pipeline (i) recovers compact, reusable model structure from flat solver formulations, (ii) reverse-generates natural-language specifications explicitly tied to this recovered structure under a unified model--data separation format, and (iii) performs iterative semantic validation through expert review and human--LLM interaction with independent reconstruction checks. This yields 223 one-to-one reconstructions that preserve the mathematical content of the original instances while enabling realistic natural-language-to-optimization evaluation. Experiments show substantial performance degradation on MIPLIB-NL for systems that perform strongly on existing benchmarks, exposing failure modes invisible at toy scale.

AI.tldr: Introduces MIPLIB-NL, a structure-aware reverse-construction benchmark that maps real MILP instances from MIPLIB 2017 to natural-language specifications linked to their recovered model structure; 223 reconstructions; exposes LLM weaknesses on industrial-scale optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: There is a critical gap in benchmarks that connect natural-language requirements with correct optimization formulations and solver code, especially for large-scale industrial problems. Existing benchmarks rely on toy sizes and fail to reveal difficulties present in 10^3–10^6+ variable/constraint MILPs.
AI.method: A three-step pipeline: (i) recover compact, reusable model structure from flat solver formulations, (ii) reverse-generate natural-language specifications explicitly tied to the recovered structure under a unified model–data separation format, and (iii) perform iterative semantic validation via expert review and human–LLM interaction with independent reconstruction checks.
AI.result: Produced 223 one-to-one reconstructions that preserve the mathematical content of the original MILP instances while enabling realistic NL-to-optimization evaluation. Experiments show substantial performance degradation for systems that perform well on existing benchmarks, revealing failure modes invisible at toy scale.
AI.conclusion: MIPLIB-NL provides a realistic, structure-aware benchmark linking natural language to optimization models and solver code. It highlights gaps in current LLM-based NL-to-optimization approaches and underscores the need for structure-guided evaluation and expert–LLM collaboration to expose weaknesses on large-scale problems.

title: TwiFF (Think With Future Frames): A Large-Scale Dataset for Dynamic Visual Reasoning
summary: Visual Chain-of-Thought (VCoT) has emerged as a promising paradigm for enhancing multimodal reasoning by integrating visual perception into intermediate reasoning steps. However, existing VCoT approaches are largely confined to static scenarios and struggle to capture the temporal dynamics essential for tasks such as instruction, prediction, and camera motion. To bridge this gap, we propose TwiFF-2.7M, the first large-scale, temporally grounded VCoT dataset derived from $2.7$ million video clips, explicitly designed for dynamic visual question and answer. Accompanying this, we introduce TwiFF-Bench, a high-quality evaluation benchmark of $1,078$ samples that assesses both the plausibility of reasoning trajectories and the correctness of final answers in open-ended dynamic settings. Building on these foundations, we propose the TwiFF model, a unified modal that synergistically leverages pre-trained video generation and image comprehension capabilities to produce temporally coherent visual reasoning cues-iteratively generating future action frames and textual reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TwiFF significantly outperforms existing VCoT methods and Textual Chain-of-Thought baselines on dynamic reasoning tasks, which fully validates the effectiveness for visual question answering in dynamic scenarios. Our code and data is available at https://github.com/LiuJunhua02/TwiFF.

AI.tldr: Proposes TwiFF-2.7M: a temporally grounded Visual Chain-of-Thought (VCoT) dataset from 2.7M video clips, plus TwiFF-Bench for dynamic reasoning evaluation and the TwiFF model that fuses video generation with image comprehension to produce temporally coherent reasoning cues and future-frame predictions for dynamic VQA.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap in VCoT for dynamic, temporally evolving visual tasks by capturing how reasoning unfolds over time, not just static frames; enable open-ended dynamic visual question answering (VQA).
AI.method: Construct TwiFF-2.7M (2.7M video clips) for dynamic VCoT; develop TwiFF-Bench with 1,078 samples to evaluate both reasoning trajectory plausibility and final answer correctness; design TwiFF, a unified model that leverages pre-trained video generation and image comprehension to iteratively generate future action frames and textual reasoning, ensuring temporal coherence.
AI.result: TwiFF significantly outperforms existing VCoT methods and textual CoT baselines on dynamic reasoning tasks, validating effectiveness for dynamic VQA.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a scalable dynamic VCoT ecosystem—dataset, benchmark, and model—that enables robust dynamic visual reasoning; code and data are released for reproducibility.

title: Driving Reaction Trajectories via Latent Flow Matching
summary: Recent advances in reaction prediction have achieved near-saturated accuracy on standard benchmarks (e.g., USPTO), yet most state-of-the-art models formulate the task as a one-shot mapping from reactants to products, offering limited insight into the underlying reaction process. Procedural alternatives introduce stepwise generation but often rely on mechanism-specific supervision, discrete symbolic edits, and computationally expensive inference. In this work, we propose LatentRxnFlow, a new reaction prediction paradigm that models reactions as continuous latent trajectories anchored at the thermodynamic product state. Built on Conditional Flow Matching, our approach learns time-dependent latent dynamics directly from standard reactant-product pairs, without requiring mechanistic annotations or curated intermediate labels. While LatentRxnFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance on USPTO benchmarks, more importantly, the continuous formulation exposes the full generative trajectory, enabling trajectory-level diagnostics that are difficult to realize with discrete or one-shot models. We show that latent trajectory analysis allows us to localize and characterize failure modes and to mitigate certain errors via gated inference. Furthermore, geometric properties of the learned trajectories provide an intrinsic signal of epistemic uncertainty, helping prioritize reliably predictable reaction outcomes and flag ambiguous cases for additional validation. Overall, LatentRxnFlow combines strong predictive accuracy with improved transparency, diagnosability, and uncertainty awareness, moving reaction prediction toward more trustworthy deployment in high-throughput discovery workflows.

AI.tldr: LatentRxnFlow models reaction prediction as continuous latent trajectories using Conditional Flow Matching anchored at the product state, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy while enabling trajectory-level diagnostics and uncertainty estimates without mechanistic labels.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of interpretability in one-shot reactant-to-product models and the reliance on mechanism-specific supervision in stepwise methods; provide a generative, transparent framework for reaction prediction.
AI.method: Learn time-dependent latent dynamics directly from reactant–product pairs using Conditional Flow Matching, with trajectories anchored to the thermodynamic product state; no intermediate annotations or curated labels required; continuous latent representation.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on USPTO benchmarks; enables localization of failure modes via trajectory analysis; gated inference can mitigate some errors; geometric properties of trajectories yield an intrinsic epistemic-uncertainty signal; improves transparency and diagnosability.
AI.conclusion: LatencyRxnFlow combines high predictive accuracy with interpretable trajectory generation and uncertainty awareness, advancing reaction prediction toward more trustworthy deployment in high-throughput discovery workflows.

title: Binary Flow Matching: Prediction-Loss Space Alignment for Robust Learning
summary: Flow matching has emerged as a powerful framework for generative modeling, with recent empirical successes highlighting the effectiveness of signal-space prediction ($x$-prediction). In this work, we investigate the transfer of this paradigm to binary manifolds, a fundamental setting for generative modeling of discrete data. While $x$-prediction remains effective, we identify a latent structural mismatch that arises when it is coupled with velocity-based objectives ($v$-loss), leading to a time-dependent singular weighting that amplifies gradient sensitivity to approximation errors. Motivated by this observation, we formalize prediction-loss alignment as a necessary condition for flow matching training. We prove that re-aligning the objective to the signal space ($x$-loss) eliminates the singular weighting, yielding uniformly bounded gradients and enabling robust training under uniform timestep sampling without reliance on heuristic schedules. Finally, with alignment secured, we examine design choices specific to binary data, revealing a topology-dependent distinction between probabilistic objectives (e.g., cross-entropy) and geometric losses (e.g., mean squared error). Together, these results provide theoretical foundations and practical guidelines for robust flow matching on binary -- and related discrete -- domains, positioning signal-space alignment as a key principle for robust diffusion learning.

AI.tldr: Signal-space (x) prediction alignment in flow matching removes a time-dependent singular weighting from velocity-based losses on binary manifolds, yielding uniformly bounded gradients and robust training; topology dictates when probabilistic losses (cross-entropy) vs geometric losses (MSE) are preferable, establishing signal-space alignment as a core principle for discrete diffusion learning.
AI.motivation: Binary/discrete data create a structural mismatch for velocity-based objectives in flow matching. There is a need to formalize prediction-loss alignment and demonstrate its impact on gradient behavior and training robustness.
AI.method: Theoretically formalize prediction-loss alignment as a necessary condition for flow matching training; prove that switching from v-loss to x-loss eliminates the singular weighting and yields uniformly bounded gradients, enabling robust training under uniform timestep sampling; analyze design choices for binary data and contrast probabilistic versus geometric losses in a topology-dependent manner.
AI.result: With x-loss alignment, gradients are uniformly bounded, enabling stable training with uniform timestep sampling without ad hoc schedules. The paper provides practical guidelines for robust flow matching on binary/discrete domains and clarifies when probabilistic vs geometric losses are advantageous depending on data topology.
AI.conclusion: Prediction-loss alignment to the signal space (x-loss) is a key principle for robust flow matching on binary and related discrete domains, removing problematic time-dependent weighting and enabling principled loss choices that reflect the underlying topology.

title: OmniVL-Guard: Towards Unified Vision-Language Forgery Detection and Grounding via Balanced RL
summary: Existing forgery detection methods are often limited to uni-modal or bi-modal settings, failing to handle the interleaved text, images, and videos prevalent in real-world misinformation. To bridge this gap, this paper targets to develop a unified framework for omnibus vision-language forgery detection and grounding. In this unified setting, the {interplay} between diverse modalities and the dual requirements of simultaneous detection and localization pose a critical ``difficulty bias`` problem: the simpler veracity classification task tends to dominate the gradients, leading to suboptimal performance in fine-grained grounding during multi-task optimization. To address this challenge, we propose \textbf{OmniVL-Guard}, a balanced reinforcement learning framework for omnibus vision-language forgery detection and grounding. Particularly, OmniVL-Guard comprises two core designs: Self-Evolving CoT Generatio and Adaptive Reward Scaling Policy Optimization (ARSPO). {Self-Evolving CoT Generation} synthesizes high-quality reasoning paths, effectively overcoming the cold-start challenge. Building upon this, {Adaptive Reward Scaling Policy Optimization (ARSPO)} dynamically modulates reward scales and task weights, ensuring a balanced joint optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OmniVL-Guard significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods and exhibits zero-shot robust generalization across out-of-domain scenarios.

AI.tldr: OmniVL-Guard presents a unified RL-based framework for omnibus vision-language forgery detection and grounding, addressing the difficulty bias where veracity classification dominates multi-task optimization, via Self-Evolving CoT Generation and Adaptive Reward Scaling Policy Optimization to balance tasks and improve zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Current forgery detectors are typically uni-/bi-modal and struggle with interleaved text, images, and videos in real-world misinformation. A unified framework is needed to jointly detect and localize forgeries across modalities while handling the interplay and competing objectives.
AI.method: Introduce OmniVL-Guard, a balanced reinforcement learning framework with two core components: (1) Self-Evolving Chain-of-Thought Generation to synthesize high-quality reasoning paths and mitigate cold-start in reasoning; (2) Adaptive Reward Scaling Policy Optimization (ARSPO) to dynamically adjust reward scales and task weights for balanced joint optimization of detection (veracity) and grounding tasks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show OmniVL-Guard outperforms state-of-the-art methods and achieves zero-shot robustness in out-of-domain scenarios.
AI.conclusion: A balanced RL approach with self-evolved reasoning paths and adaptive reward scaling effectively handles the multi-task challenges of omnibus vision-language forgery detection and grounding, enabling improved accuracy and generalization.

title: Learning Adaptive Distribution Alignment with Neural Characteristic Function for Graph Domain Adaptation
summary: Graph Domain Adaptation (GDA) transfers knowledge from labeled source graphs to unlabeled target graphs but is challenged by complex, multi-faceted distributional shifts. Existing methods attempt to reduce distributional shifts by aligning manually selected graph elements (e.g., node attributes or structural statistics), which typically require manually designed graph filters to extract relevant features before alignment. However, such approaches are inflexible: they rely on scenario-specific heuristics, and struggle when dominant discrepancies vary across transfer scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{ADAlign}, an Adaptive Distribution Alignment framework for GDA. Unlike heuristic methods, ADAlign requires no manual specification of alignment criteria. It automatically identifies the most relevant discrepancies in each transfer and aligns them jointly, capturing the interplay between attributes, structures, and their dependencies. This makes ADAlign flexible, scenario-aware, and robust to diverse and dynamically evolving shifts. To enable this adaptivity, we introduce the Neural Spectral Discrepancy (NSD), a theoretically principled parametric distance that provides a unified view of cross-graph shifts. NSD leverages neural characteristic function in the spectral domain to encode feature-structure dependencies of all orders, while a learnable frequency sampler adaptively emphasizes the most informative spectral components for each task via minimax paradigm. Extensive experiments on 10 datasets and 16 transfer tasks show that ADAlign not only outperforms state-of-the-art baselines but also achieves efficiency gains with lower memory usage and faster training.

AI.tldr: ADAlign introduces NSD and an adaptive distribution-alignment framework for Graph Domain Adaptation, enabling automatic, joint alignment of attribute-structure discrepancies without manual criteria, achieving superior transfer performance and efficiency across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: Current GDA methods rely on manually designed graph filters to align limited elements, making them inflexible to scenario-specific and evolving distribution shifts; there is a need for a unified, automatic alignment criterion that captures complex attribute-structure interactions.
AI.method: Propose Neural Spectral Discrepancy (NSD), a parametric distance in the spectral domain using neural characteristic functions to encode cross-graph shifts across all orders; a learnable frequency sampler optimized via a minimax objective emphasizes informative spectral components; integrate NSD into ADAlign to automatically identify and jointly align the most relevant discrepancies between source and target graphs.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 10 datasets and 16 transfer tasks shows ADAlign outperforms state-of-the-art baselines and delivers efficiency gains with lower memory usage and faster training.
AI.conclusion: ADAlign provides a flexible, scenario-aware, robust approach to GDA by automatically discovering and aligning attribute-structure dependencies; the NSD-based distance supports diverse, evolving shifts and improves transfer performance while reducing computational costs.

title: AugVLA-3D: Depth-Driven Feature Augmentation for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently achieved remarkable progress in robotic perception and control, yet most existing approaches primarily rely on VLM trained using 2D images, which limits their spatial understanding and action grounding in complex 3D environments. To address this limitation, we propose a novel framework that integrates depth estimation into VLA models to enrich 3D feature representations. Specifically, we employ a depth estimation baseline called VGGT to extract geometry-aware 3D cues from standard RGB inputs, enabling efficient utilization of existing large-scale 2D datasets while implicitly recovering 3D structural information. To further enhance the reliability of these depth-derived features, we introduce a new module called action assistant, which constrains the learned 3D representations with action priors and ensures their consistency with downstream control tasks. By fusing the enhanced 3D features with conventional 2D visual tokens, our approach significantly improves the generalization ability and robustness of VLA models. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method not only strengthens perception in geometrically ambiguous scenarios but also leads to superior action prediction accuracy. This work highlights the potential of depth-driven data augmentation and auxiliary expert supervision for bridging the gap between 2D observations and 3D-aware decision-making in robotic systems.

AI.tldr: A depth-augmented VLA framework for robotics that uses VGGT-based depth estimation to derive 3D cues from RGB inputs, adds an action assistant to align 3D representations with action priors, and fuses 3D features with 2D tokens to boost perception and action prediction.
AI.motivation: 2D-only vision-language-action models struggle with 3D understanding and precise action grounding in complex environments. Depth cues can enrich 3D representations while leveraging existing 2D datasets, but require mechanisms to ensure depth-derived features align with control tasks.
AI.method: Extract geometry-aware 3D cues from RGB using VGGT depth estimation. Introduce an action assistant module that imposes action priors and enforces consistency of 3D representations with downstream control tasks. Fuse enhanced 3D features with standard 2D visual tokens to train a unified VLA model.
AI.result: Improved generalization and robustness of VLA models; higher action prediction accuracy, especially in geometrically ambiguous scenarios, demonstrated by experiments.
AI.conclusion: Depth-driven data augmentation and auxiliary supervision can bridge 2D observations and 3D-aware decision-making in robotics, enabling better perception and control by leveraging depth information without requiring full 3D supervision.

title: Low-Dimensional Execution Manifolds in Transformer Learning Dynamics: Evidence from Modular Arithmetic Tasks
summary: We investigate the geometric structure of learning dynamics in overparameterized transformer models through carefully controlled modular arithmetic tasks. Our primary finding is that despite operating in high-dimensional parameter spaces ($d=128$), transformer training trajectories rapidly collapse onto low-dimensional execution manifolds of dimension $3$--$4$. This dimensional collapse is robust across random seeds and moderate task difficulties, though the orientation of the manifold in parameter space varies between runs. We demonstrate that this geometric structure underlies several empirically observed phenomena: (1) sharp attention concentration emerges as saturation along routing coordinates within the execution manifold, (2) stochastic gradient descent (SGD) exhibits approximately integrable dynamics when projected onto the execution subspace, with non-integrability confined to orthogonal staging directions, and (3) sparse autoencoders capture auxiliary routing structure but fail to isolate execution itself, which remains distributed across the low-dimensional manifold. Our results suggest a unifying geometric framework for understanding transformer learning, where the vast majority of parameters serve to absorb optimization interference while core computation occurs in a dramatically reduced subspace. These findings have implications for interpretability, training curriculum design, and understanding the role of overparameterization in neural network learning.

AI.tldr: Overparameterized transformers trained on modular arithmetic tasks rapidly collapse from a high-dimensional parameter space (d=128) to a low-dimensional execution manifold of dimension 3–4, with the core computation residing on this subspace and optimization noise absorbed elsewhere.
AI.motivation: Understand how overparameterization shapes learning dynamics in transformer models and whether a low-dimensional geometric structure underlies observed phenomena in training and routing.
AI.method: Controlled experiments with transformer models on modular arithmetic tasks; track training trajectories in parameter space, identify the execution manifold, project SGD dynamics onto the subspace, use sparse autoencoders to probe routing structure; test robustness across random seeds and task difficulty.
AI.result: Trajectories rapidly collapse to a 3–4D manifold in parameter space despite d=128; manifold orientation varies across runs but dimension stays constant; sharp attention concentration arises from saturation along routing coordinates on the manifold; SGD behaves approximately integrably when projected onto the execution subspace, with non-integrable behavior confined to orthogonal directions; sparse autoencoders reveal routing structure but fail to isolate core execution, which remains distributed on the low-dimensional manifold.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a unifying geometric framework for transformer learning where most parameters absorb optimization interference while core computation is confined to a dramatically reduced subspace; has implications for interpretability, curriculum design, and understanding the role of overparameterization in learning.

title: QTALE: Quantization-Robust Token-Adaptive Layer Execution for LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) demand substantial computational and memory resources, posing challenges for efficient deployment. Two complementary approaches have emerged to address these issues: token-adaptive layer execution, which reduces floating-point operations (FLOPs) by selectively bypassing layers, and quantization, which lowers memory footprint by reducing weight precision. However, naively integrating these techniques leads to additional accuracy degradation due to reduced redundancy in token-adaptive models. We propose QTALE (Quantization-Robust Token-Adaptive Layer Execution for LLMs), a novel framework that enables seamless integration of token-adaptive execution with quantization while preserving accuracy. Conventional token-adaptive methods reduce redundancy in two ways: (1) by limiting the diversity of training paths explored during fine-tuning, and (2) by lowering the number of parameters actively involved in inference. To overcome these limitations, QTALE introduces two key components: (1) a training strategy that ensures diverse execution paths are actively explored during fine-tuning, and (2) a post-training mechanism that allows flexible adjustment of the execution ratio at inference to reintroduce redundancy when needed. Experimental results show that QTALE enables seamless integration of token-adaptive layer execution with quantization, showing no noticeable accuracy difference, with the gap to quantization-only models kept below 0.5% on CommonsenseQA benchmarks. By combining token-adaptive execution for FLOPs reduction and quantization for memory savings, QTALE provides an effective solution for efficient LLM deployment.

AI.tldr: QTALE combines token-adaptive layer execution with quantization to maintain accuracy while reducing FLOPs and memory. It uses a diverse-path fine-tuning strategy and a post-training mechanism to flexibly adjust execution ratio, achieving <0.5% gap to quantization-only baselines on CommonsenseQA, enabling efficient LLM deployment.
AI.motivation: Efficient deployment of large language models requires reducing both compute (FLOPs) and memory. Token-adaptive execution reduces FLOPs but can hurt accuracy when combined with quantization due to reduced redundancy. A method that preserves accuracy while enabling joint FLOPs and memory savings is needed.
AI.method: QTALE integrates token-adaptive layer execution with quantization through two components: (1) a training strategy that enforces diverse execution paths during fine-tuning to preserve redundancy, and (2) a post-training mechanism that allows flexible adjustment of execution ratio at inference, reintroducing redundancy when needed.
AI.result: Empirical results show seamless integration of token-adaptive execution with quantization with negligible accuracy loss; the gap to quantization-only models on CommonsenseQA is below 0.5%. This demonstrates effective joint deployment benefits for FLOPs and memory.
AI.conclusion: QTALE provides a practical framework for efficient LLM deployment by enabling robust token-adaptive execution alongside quantization, achieving significant resource savings with minimal accuracy degradation.

title: Learning Structure-Semantic Evolution Trajectories for Graph Domain Adaptation
summary: Graph Domain Adaptation (GDA) aims to bridge distribution shifts between domains by transferring knowledge from well-labeled source graphs to given unlabeled target graphs. One promising recent approach addresses graph transfer by discretizing the adaptation process, typically through the construction of intermediate graphs or stepwise alignment procedures. However, such discrete strategies often fail in real-world scenarios, where graph structures evolve continuously and nonlinearly, making it difficult for fixed-step alignment to approximate the actual transformation process. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{DiffGDA}, a \textbf{Diff}usion-based \textbf{GDA} method that models the domain adaptation process as a continuous-time generative process. We formulate the evolution from source to target graphs using stochastic differential equations (SDEs), enabling the joint modeling of structural and semantic transitions. To guide this evolution, a domain-aware network is introduced to steer the generative process toward the target domain, encouraging the diffusion trajectory to follow an optimal adaptation path. We theoretically show that the diffusion process converges to the optimal solution bridging the source and target domains in the latent space. Extensive experiments on 14 graph transfer tasks across 8 real-world datasets demonstrate DiffGDA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: Presents DiffGDA, a diffusion-based, continuous-time graph domain adaptation method that treats source-to-target evolution as a stochastic process, guided by a domain-aware network, with theoretical convergence guarantees and strong empirical results across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Discrete, stepwise graph transfer methods struggle to capture continuous, nonlinear structural changes in graphs. A continuous-time diffusion framework can model gradual domain shifts and joint structural-semantic evolution.
AI.method: Model graph domain adaptation as a continuous-time generative process via stochastic differential equations to evolve source graphs toward target graphs. A domain-aware network guides the diffusion, steering trajectories toward the target domain. The authors prove convergence to an optimal latent-space solution bridging source and target.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 14 graph transfer tasks across 8 real-world datasets shows that DiffGDA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: DiffGDA provides a principled continuous-time framework for graph domain adaptation with theoretical guarantees and empirically superior performance, addressing the limitations of discrete, fixed-step alignment.

title: FGAA-FPN: Foreground-Guided Angle-Aware Feature Pyramid Network for Oriented Object Detection
summary: With the increasing availability of high-resolution remote sensing and aerial imagery, oriented object detection has become a key capability for geographic information updating, maritime surveillance, and disaster response. However, it remains challenging due to cluttered backgrounds, severe scale variation, and large orientation changes. Existing approaches largely improve performance through multi-scale feature fusion with feature pyramid networks or contextual modeling with attention, but they often lack explicit foreground modeling and do not leverage geometric orientation priors, which limits feature discriminability. To overcome these limitations, we propose FGAA-FPN, a Foreground-Guided Angle-Aware Feature Pyramid Network for oriented object detection. FGAA-FPN is built on a hierarchical functional decomposition that accounts for the distinct spatial resolution and semantic abstraction across pyramid levels, thereby strengthening multi-scale representations. Concretely, a Foreground-Guided Feature Modulation module learns foreground saliency under weak supervision to enhance object regions and suppress background interference in low-level features. In parallel, an Angle-Aware Multi-Head Attention module encodes relative orientation relationships to guide global interactions among high-level semantic features. Extensive experiments on DOTA v1.0 and DOTA v1.5 demonstrate that FGAA-FPN achieves state-of-the-art results, reaching 75.5% and 68.3% mAP, respectively.

AI.tldr: FGAA-FPN: Foreground-Guided Angle-Aware Feature Pyramid Network for oriented object detection; introduces foreground-guided modulation and angle-aware attention; achieves SOTA on DOTA v1.0/1.5 (mAP 75.5% / 68.3%).
AI.motivation: Oriented object detection in remote sensing is challenged by cluttered backgrounds, extreme scale variation, and large orientation changes. Existing multi-scale fusion and context attention often lack explicit foreground modeling and geometry priors, reducing discriminability.
AI.method: Hierarchical functional decomposition across pyramid levels. Foreground-Guided Feature Modulation module learns foreground saliency under weak supervision to enhance object regions and suppress background in low-level features. Angle-Aware Multi-Head Attention encodes relative orientation relationships to guide global interactions among high-level semantic features.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on DOTA v1.0 and v1.5 demonstrate state-of-the-art results, achieving 75.5% and 68.3% mAP, respectively.
AI.conclusion: FGAA-FPN strengthens multi-scale representations by integrating foreground cues and orientation priors, improving discriminability for oriented object detection and achieving SOTA on major remote sensing benchmarks.

title: Ecological mapping with geospatial foundation models
summary: Geospatial foundation models (GFMs) are a fast-emerging paradigm for various geospatial tasks, such as ecological mapping. However, the utility of GFMs has not been fully explored for high-value use cases. This study aims to explore the utility, challenges and opportunities associated with the application of GFMs for ecological uses. In this regard, we fine-tune several pretrained AI models, namely, Prithvi-E0-2.0 and TerraMind, across three use cases, and compare this with a baseline ResNet-101 model. Firstly, we demonstrate TerraMind's LULC generation capabilities. Lastly, we explore the utility of the GFMs in forest functional trait mapping and peatlands detection. In all experiments, the GFMs outperform the baseline ResNet models. In general TerraMind marginally outperforms Prithvi. However, with additional modalities TerraMind significantly outperforms the baseline ResNet and Prithvi models. Nonetheless, consideration should be given to the divergence of input data from pretrained modalities. We note that these models would benefit from higher resolution and more accurate labels, especially for use cases where pixel-level dynamics need to be mapped.

AI.tldr: GFMs show promise for ecological geospatial tasks: TerraMind and Prithvi-E0-2.0 outperform a ResNet-101 baseline across LULC generation, forest functional trait mapping, and peatland detection; TerraMind is generally stronger, especially when additional modalities are used; data divergence and labeling quality limit pixel-level accuracy.
AI.motivation: Geospatial foundation models (GFMs) are emerging and may enhance high-value ecological applications. The study investigates their utility, challenges, and opportunities in ecological uses by fine-tuning pretrained GFMs and comparing them to a ResNet-101 baseline across multiple tasks.
AI.method: Fine-tune pretrained GFMs (TerraMind and Prithvi-E0-2.0) on three ecological use cases (LULC generation, forest functional trait mapping, peatland detection) and compare performance against a ResNet-101 baseline. Analyze the impact of additional modalities on performance.
AI.result: GFMs outperform the baseline ResNet-101 across all experiments. TerraMind marginally outperforms Prithvi-E0-2.0, and with additional modalities TerraMind significantly outperforms both the baseline and Prithvi. However, input data divergence from pretrained modalities can affect performance; improvements require higher-resolution data and more accurate pixel-level labels.
AI.conclusion: GFMs demonstrate utility for ecological geospatial tasks and can surpass traditional CNN baselines, especially when leveraging multiple modalities. Caution is needed regarding modality divergence and data quality for precision mapping at the pixel level; future work should focus on higher-resolution data and better labeling to improve pixel-level dynamics.

title: A Diffusion-Based Generative Prior Approach to Sparse-view Computed Tomography
summary: The reconstruction of X-rays CT images from sparse or limited-angle geometries is a highly challenging task. The lack of data typically results in artifacts in the reconstructed image and may even lead to object distortions. For this reason, the use of deep generative models in this context has great interest and potential success. In the Deep Generative Prior (DGP) framework, the use of diffusion-based generative models is combined with an iterative optimization algorithm for the reconstruction of CT images from sinograms acquired under sparse geometries, to maintain the explainability of a model-based approach while introducing the generative power of a neural network. There are therefore several aspects that can be further investigated within these frameworks to improve reconstruction quality, such as image generation, the model, and the iterative algorithm used to solve the minimization problem, for which we propose modifications with respect to existing approaches. The results obtained even under highly sparse geometries are very promising, although further research is clearly needed in this direction.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based deep generative priors are integrated with iterative optimization to reconstruct CT images from sparse/sparse-angle sinograms, achieving promising results and highlighting areas for further improvement in image generation, model design, and optimization strategies.
AI.motivation: CT reconstruction from sparse or limited-angle data inevitably produces artifacts and distortions. Deep generative priors, especially diffusion models, offer a powerful prior to regularize reconstructions while preserving explainability through a model-based framework.
AI.method: Incorporate diffusion-based generative models as a prior in an iterative optimization algorithm (DGP framework) to solve the CT reconstruction from sparse sinograms. The abstract also mentions exploring modifications to image generation, the model, and the iterative solver to improve performance.
AI.result: The approach yields very promising reconstructions even under highly sparse geometries, suggesting the combination of diffusion priors with iterative data fidelity terms can mitigate artifacts and distortions.
AI.conclusion: The DGP framework with diffusion-based priors shows strong potential for sparse-data CT reconstruction, but further research is needed to optimize generation, modeling choices, and optimization strategies to maximize reconstruction quality and robustness.

title: A Swap-Adversarial Framework for Improving Domain Generalization in Electroencephalography-Based Parkinson's Disease Prediction
summary: Electroencephalography (ECoG) offers a promising alternative to conventional electrocorticography (EEG) for the early prediction of Parkinson's disease (PD), providing higher spatial resolution and a broader frequency range. However, reproducible comparisons has been limited by ethical constraints in human studies and the lack of open benchmark datasets. To address this gap, we introduce a new dataset, the first reproducible benchmark for PD prediction. It is constructed from long-term ECoG recordings of 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA)-induced rat models and annotated with neural responses measured before and after electrical stimulation. In addition, we propose a Swap-Adversarial Framework (SAF) that mitigates high inter-subject variability and the high-dimensional low-sample-size (HDLSS) problem in ECoG data, while achieving robust domain generalization across ECoG and EEG-based Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) datasets. The framework integrates (1) robust preprocessing, (2) Inter-Subject Balanced Channel Swap (ISBCS) for cross-subject augmentation, and (3) domain-adversarial training to suppress subject-specific bias. ISBCS randomly swaps channels between subjects to reduce inter-subject variability, and domain-adversarial training jointly encourages the model to learn task-relevant shared features. We validated the effectiveness of the proposed method through extensive experiments under cross-subject, cross-session, and cross-dataset settings. Our method consistently outperformed all baselines across all settings, showing the most significant improvements in highly variable environments. Furthermore, the proposed method achieved superior cross-dataset performance between public EEG benchmarks, demonstrating strong generalization capability not only within ECoG but to EEG data. The new dataset and source code will be made publicly available upon publication.

AI.tldr: Introduces a reproducible PD prediction benchmark dataset from long-term rat ECoG (6-OHDA model) and a Swap-Adversarial Framework (SAF) with Inter-Subject Balanced Channel Swap and domain-adversarial training to improve cross-subject and cross-dataset generalization, achieving superior results versus baselines and enabling cross-dataset transfer to EEG benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address the reproducibility gap in PD prediction studies due to ethical constraints limiting human data and the lack of open benchmarks, compounded by high inter-subject variability and HDLSS in ECoG/EEG data.
AI.method: Constructs a new open-access dataset from 6-OHDA-induced rat ECoG recordings with neural responses annotated before and after electrical stimulation. Proposes Swap-Adversarial Framework (SAF) comprising: (1) robust preprocessing, (2) Inter-Subject Balanced Channel Swap (ISBCS) for cross-subject augmentation, and (3) domain-adversarial training to suppress subject-specific bias. SAF is evaluated under cross-subject, cross-session, and cross-dataset settings, demonstrating cross-domain generalization from ECoG to EEG.
AI.result: SAF consistently outperforms all baselines across all evaluation settings, with the largest gains in highly variable environments. It achieves superior cross-dataset performance between public EEG benchmarks, indicating strong generalization from ECoG to EEG datasets.
AI.conclusion: The Swap-Adversarial Framework enhances cross-subject and cross-dataset generalization for PD prediction using ECoG and EEG, supporting reproducible benchmarks and potential cross-domain BCI applications; the authors will release the dataset and source code publicly.

title: Chamfer-Linkage for Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering
summary:
Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (HAC) is a widely-used clustering method based on repeatedly merging the closest pair of clusters, where inter-cluster distances are determined by a linkage function. Unlike many clustering methods, HAC does not optimize a single explicit global objective; clustering quality is therefore primarily evaluated empirically, and the choice of linkage function plays a crucial role in practice. However, popular classical linkages, such as single-linkage, average-linkage and Ward's method show high variability across real-world datasets and do not consistently produce high-quality clusterings in practice.
  In this paper, we propose \emph{Chamfer-linkage}, a novel linkage function that measures the distance between clusters using the Chamfer distance, a popular notion of distance between point-clouds in machine learning and computer vision. We argue that Chamfer-linkage satisfies desirable concept representation properties that other popular measures struggle to satisfy. Theoretically, we show that Chamfer-linkage HAC can be implemented in $O(n^2)$ time, matching the efficiency of classical linkage functions. Experimentally, we find that Chamfer-linkage consistently yields higher-quality clusterings than classical linkages such as average-linkage and Ward's method across a diverse collection of datasets. Our results establish Chamfer-linkage as a practical drop-in replacement for classical linkage functions, broadening the toolkit for hierarchical clustering in both theory and practice.

AI.tldr: Proposes Chamfer-linkage for hierarchical agglomerative clustering (HAC), using Chamfer distance between clusters. The approach achieves O(n^2) time, reports higher-quality clusterings than average-linkage and Ward across diverse datasets, and positions Chamfer-linkage as a practical drop-in replacement for classical HAC linkages.
AI.motivation: Address the variability and suboptimal clustering quality of classical HAC linkages by introducing a linkage that better preserves cluster structure and representation; provide a linkage with desirable representation properties and practical efficiency.
AI.method: Define the Chamfer distance between two clusters (as point-sets) and use it as the linkage criterion in HAC. Develop an O(n^2) HAC algorithm leveraging Chamfer distances. Validate theoretically and empirically against classical linkages (single/average/Ward).
AI.result: Chamfer-linkage HAC yields higher-quality clusterings on a diverse dataset collection, with computational efficiency on par with traditional linkages. It demonstrates practical effectiveness and consistency.
AI.conclusion: Chamfer-linkage is a viable drop-in replacement for classical HAC linkages, expanding the hierarchical clustering toolkit with theoretically and empirically supported benefits.

title: OccFace: Unified Occlusion-Aware Facial Landmark Detection with Per-Point Visibility
summary: Accurate facial landmark detection under occlusion remains challenging, especially for human-like faces with large appearance variation and rotation-driven self-occlusion. Existing detectors typically localize landmarks while handling occlusion implicitly, without predicting per-point visibility that downstream applications can benefits. We present OccFace, an occlusion-aware framework for universal human-like faces, including humans, stylized characters, and other non-human designs. OccFace adopts a unified dense 100-point layout and a heatmap-based backbone, and adds an occlusion module that jointly predicts landmark coordinates and per-point visibility by combining local evidence with cross-landmark context. Visibility supervision mixes manual labels with landmark-aware masking that derives pseudo visibility from mask-heatmap overlap. We also create an occlusion-aware evaluation suite reporting NME on visible vs. occluded landmarks and benchmarking visibility with Occ AP, F1@0.5, and ROC-AUC, together with a dataset annotated with 100-point landmarks and per-point visibility. Experiments show improved robustness under external occlusion and large head rotations, especially on occluded regions, while preserving accuracy on visible landmarks.

AI.tldr: OccFace is an occlusion-aware, universal 100-point facial landmark framework that predicts per-point visibility in addition to coordinates, using a heatmap backbone and a cross-landmark context module. It introduces mixed supervision for visibility, and an evaluation suite separating visible vs. occluded landmarks with new metrics and a 100-point annotated dataset.
AI.motivation: Current facial landmark detectors under occlusion lack explicit per-point visibility predictions and struggle to generalize across human-like and non-human faces with large appearance variation and rotation-driven self-occlusion. A unified, occlusion-aware framework with per-point visibility can improve robustness and enable downstream tasks.
AI.method: A unified dense 100-point layout with a heatmap-based backbone. An occlusion module jointly predicts landmark coordinates and per-point visibility by fusing local evidence with cross-landmark context. Visibility supervision combines manual labels with landmark-aware masking that derives pseudo visibility from mask-heatmap overlap. An evaluation suite reports NME on visible vs. occluded landmarks and benchmarks visibility via Occ AP, F1@0.5, and ROC-AUC. A dataset annotated with 100-point landmarks and per-point visibility.
AI.result: Experiments show improved robustness under external occlusion and large head rotations, especially for occluded regions, while preserving accuracy on visible landmarks.
AI.conclusion: OccFace enables occlusion-aware landmark detection across diverse face types with a unified 100-point framework. It provides a dedicated visibility-aware evaluation protocol, a dataset with per-point visibility, and demonstrates better occlusion handling without sacrificing precision on visible landmarks.

title: $μ$pscaling small models: Principled warm starts and hyperparameter transfer
summary: Modern large-scale neural networks are often trained and released in multiple sizes to accommodate diverse inference budgets. To improve efficiency, recent work has explored model upscaling: initializing larger models from trained smaller ones in order to transfer knowledge and accelerate convergence. However, this method can be sensitive to hyperparameters that need to be tuned at the target upscaled model size, which is prohibitively costly to do directly. It remains unclear whether the most common workaround -- tuning on smaller models and extrapolating via hyperparameter scaling laws -- is still sound when using upscaling. We address this with principled approaches to upscaling with respect to model widths and efficiently tuning hyperparameters in this setting. First, motivated by $μ$P and any-dimensional architectures, we introduce a general upscaling method applicable to a broad range of architectures and optimizers, backed by theory guaranteeing that models are equivalent to their widened versions and allowing for rigorous analysis of infinite-width limits. Second, we extend the theory of $μ$Transfer to a hyperparameter transfer technique for models upscaled using our method and empirically demonstrate that this method is effective on realistic datasets and architectures.

AI.tldr: A principled upscaling framework to widen neural networks from small to large sizes, with theory ensuring equivalence to widened models and a hyperparameter transfer method (μTransfer) validated empirically.
AI.motivation: Address the cost and fragility of hyperparameter tuning when scaling models; assess whether hyperparameter scaling laws remain sound under upscaling.
AI.method: Introduce a general upscaling method compatible with μP and arbitrary architectures; provide theoretical guarantees of equivalence to widened versions and infinite-width limits; extend μTransfer to transfer hyperparameters for upscaled models and validate empirically on realistic datasets and architectures.
AI.result: The framework offers theoretical guarantees of model equivalence and infinite-width behavior; empirical results demonstrate effective hyperparameter transfer for upscaled models across diverse datasets and architectures.
AI.conclusion: Provides a robust, general upscaling framework with a hyperparameter transfer technique enabling efficient, reliable scaling of neural networks across different widths.

title: Self-Supervised Image Super-Resolution Quality Assessment based on Content-Free Multi-Model Oriented Representation Learning
summary: Super-resolution (SR) applied to real-world low-resolution (LR) images often results in complex, irregular degradations that stem from the inherent complexity of natural scene acquisition. In contrast to SR artifacts arising from synthetic LR images created under well-defined scenarios, those distortions are highly unpredictable and vary significantly across different real-life contexts. Consequently, assessing the quality of SR images (SR-IQA) obtained from realistic LR, remains a challenging and underexplored problem. In this work, we introduce a no-reference SR-IQA approach tailored for such highly ill-posed realistic settings. The proposed method enables domain-adaptive IQA for real-world SR applications, particularly in data-scarce domains. We hypothesize that degradations in super-resolved images are strongly dependent on the underlying SR algorithms, rather than being solely determined by image content. To this end, we introduce a self-supervised learning (SSL) strategy that first pretrains multiple SR model oriented representations in a pretext stage. Our contrastive learning framework forms positive pairs from images produced by the same SR model and negative pairs from those generated by different methods, independent of image content. The proposed approach S3 RIQA, further incorporates targeted preprocessing to extract complementary quality information and an auxiliary task to better handle the various degradation profiles associated with different SR scaling factors. To this end, we constructed a new dataset, SRMORSS, to support unsupervised pretext training; it includes a wide range of SR algorithms applied to numerous real LR images, which addresses a gap in existing datasets. Experiments on real SR-IQA benchmarks demonstrate that S3 RIQA consistently outperforms most state-of-the-art relevant metrics.

AI.tldr: Introduces S3 RIQA, a no-reference SR-IQA method using self-supervised, model-oriented contrastive learning and a new dataset SRMORSS to handle real-world degradations in SR.
AI.motivation: Assessing SR quality in real-world LR images is challenging due to unpredictable, content-independent degradations; existing synthetic-distortion-based SR-IQA methods fail to generalize to real acquisitions; need domain-adaptive solutions for data-scarce contexts.
AI.method: Pretrain SR-model oriented representations via self-supervised contrastive learning: positive pairs from the same SR model outputs, negative pairs from different SR methods; incorporate targeted preprocessing and an auxiliary task to address varying degradation with scaling; build SRMORSS dataset for unsupervised pretext training.
AI.result: Empirical results on real SR-IQA benchmarks show S3 RIQA beating most state-of-the-art SR-IQA metrics.
AI.conclusion: S3 RIQA provides a domain-adaptive, no-reference SR-IQA framework for realistic SR scenarios and supports data-scarce domains via SSL pretraining on SR-model-centric representations; SRMORSS enables effective pretext training.

title: Spectral-Spatial Contrastive Learning Framework for Regression on Hyperspectral Data
summary: Contrastive learning has demonstrated great success in representation learning, especially for image classification tasks. However, there is still a shortage in studies targeting regression tasks, and more specifically applications on hyperspectral data. In this paper, we propose a spectral-spatial contrastive learning framework for regression tasks for hyperspectral data, in a model-agnostic design allowing to enhance backbones such as 3D convolutional and transformer-based networks. Moreover, we provide a collection of transformations relevant for augmenting hyperspectral data. Experiments on synthetic and real datasets show that the proposed framework and transformations significantly improve the performance of all studied backbone models.

AI.tldr: Proposes a spectral-spatial contrastive learning framework for regression on hyperspectral data, model-agnostic to backbones (3D CNNs, transformers), with hyperspectral-specific augmentations; demonstrates improved performance on synthetic and real datasets.
AI.motivation: Contrastive learning excels in representation learning for classification; regression and hyperspectral data are underexplored. There is a need for regression-oriented contrastive methods and augmentation strategies tailored to hyperspectral data.
AI.method: A model-agnostic spectral-spatial contrastive learning framework for regression on hyperspectral data, compatible with 3D CNNs and transformer backbones, incorporating hyperspectral-specific transformations for augmentation to build robust representations.
AI.result: Experimental results on synthetic and real hyperspectral datasets show significant performance gains across all studied backbone models when using the proposed framework and augmentations.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables effective regression using contrastive learning on hyperspectral data, with model-agnostic applicability and augmentation strategies that substantially improve backbone performance.

title: A Multimodal Conditional Mixture Model with Distribution-Level Physics Priors
summary: Many scientific and engineering systems exhibit intrinsically multimodal behavior arising from latent regime switching and non-unique physical mechanisms. In such settings, learning the full conditional distribution of admissible outcomes in a physically consistent and interpretable manner remains a challenge. While recent advances in machine learning have enabled powerful multimodal generative modeling, their integration with physics-constrained scientific modeling remains nontrivial, particularly when physical structure must be preserved or data are limited. This work develops a physics-informed multimodal conditional modeling framework based on mixture density representations. Mixture density networks (MDNs) provide an explicit and interpretable parameterization of multimodal conditional distributions. Physical knowledge is embedded through component-specific regularization terms that penalize violations of governing equations or physical laws. This formulation naturally accommodates non-uniqueness and stochasticity while remaining computationally efficient and amenable to conditioning on contextual inputs. The proposed framework is evaluated across a range of scientific problems in which multimodality arises from intrinsic physical mechanisms rather than observational noise, including bifurcation phenomena in nonlinear dynamical systems, stochastic partial differential equations, and atomistic-scale shock dynamics. In addition, the proposed method is compared with a conditional flow matching (CFM) model, a representative state-of-the-art generative modeling approach, demonstrating that MDNs can achieve competitive performance while offering a simpler and more interpretable formulation.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed MDN framework for multimodal conditional modeling that enforces physics via component-specific regularization; competitive with CFM across bifurcation, SPDEs, and atomistic shock dynamics.
AI.motivation: Many scientific systems exhibit multimodal outcomes due to latent regime switching and non-unique mechanisms. There is a need for interpretable, physics-consistent models that can capture multimodal conditional distributions under limited data.
AI.method: Use mixture density networks to parameterize multimodal conditionals. Embed physics through per-component regularization that penalizes violations of governing equations or physical laws. Enable conditioning on contextual inputs and compare with conditional flow matching (CFM).
AI.result: MDN-based framework naturally handles non-uniqueness and stochasticity, is computationally efficient, and achieves competitive performance with CFM while offering simpler, more interpretable modeling. Validated on bifurcation phenomena, stochastic PDEs, and atomistic-scale shock dynamics.
AI.conclusion: MDNs offer a transparent, physics-informed approach for multimodal conditional modeling in science, providing a viable alternative to flow-based methods, particularly when data are scarce or physical interpretability is required.

title: Text-to-Vector Conversion for Residential Plan Design
summary: Computer graphics, comprising both raster and vector components, is a fundamental part of modern science, industry, and digital communication. While raster graphics offer ease of use, its pixel-based structure limits scalability. Vector graphics, defined by mathematical primitives, provides scalability without quality loss, however, it is more complex to produce. For design and architecture, the versatility of vector graphics is paramount, despite its computational demands. This paper introduces a novel method for generating vector residential plans from textual descriptions. Our approach surpasses existing solutions by approximately 5% in CLIPScore-based visual quality, benefiting from its inherent handling of right angles and flexible settings. Additionally, we present a new algorithm for vectorizing raster plans into structured vector images. Such images have a better CLIPscore compared to others by about 4%.

AI.tldr: Proposes a two-part pipeline: (1) generate vector residential plans from text descriptions with ~5% CLIPScore gain; (2) convert raster plans to structured vector images with ~4% CLIPScore gain, leveraging vector graphics' scalability and right-angle handling.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between raster ease and vector scalability in design/architecture by enabling automatic, high-quality vector planning outputs from natural language and improving raster-to-vector conversion.
AI.method: Part 1: a text-to-vector method for residential plans that emphasizes right-angle geometry and adjustable design settings. Part 2: a raster-to-vector vectorization algorithm to produce structured vector images compatible with architectural workflows. Evaluation relies on CLIPScore as a proxy for visual quality.
AI.result: Text-to-vector generation shows ~5% improvement in CLIPScore over existing solutions; vectorization from raster to vector yields ~4% higher CLIPScore compared with competing methods.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates practical gains in vector-based design workflows, highlighting CLIPScore improvements as evidence of better visual quality and suggesting a robust approach for scalable, high-quality vector representation in architecture. Further validation with human studies and analysis of generalization and computational cost would strengthen the claims.

title: Analyzing Fairness of Neural Network Prediction via Counterfactual Dataset Generation
summary: Interpreting the inference-time behavior of deep neural networks remains a challenging problem. Existing approaches to counterfactual explanation typically ask: What is the closest alternative input that would alter the model's prediction in a desired way? In contrast, we explore counterfactual datasets. Rather than perturbing the input, our method efficiently finds the closest alternative training dataset, one that differs from the original dataset by changing a few labels. Training a new model on this altered dataset can then lead to a different prediction of a given test instance. This perspective provides a new way to assess fairness by directly analyzing the influence of label bias on training and inference. Our approach can be characterized as probing whether a given prediction depends on biased labels. Since exhaustively enumerating all possible alternate datasets is infeasible, we develop analysis techniques that trace how bias in the training data may propagate through the learning algorithm to the trained network. Our method heuristically ranks and modifies the labels of a bounded number of training examples to construct a counterfactual dataset, retrains the model, and checks whether its prediction on a chosen test case changes. We evaluate our approach on feedforward neural networks across over 1100 test cases from 7 widely-used fairness datasets. Results show that it modifies only a small subset of training labels, highlighting its ability to pinpoint the critical training examples that drive prediction changes. Finally, we demonstrate how our counterfactual datasets reveal connections between training examples and test cases, offering an interpretable way to probe dataset bias.

AI.tldr: Counterfactual datasets: modify a few labels in training data to alter a test instance's prediction; retrain; analyze label bias's influence on fairness; finds small set of critical examples.
AI.motivation: Interpret inference-time behavior and fairness by examining effect of label bias on training. Unlike perturbing inputs, study how biased labels in training propagate to predictions.
AI.method: Heuristically identify a bounded set of training labels to flip, construct a counterfactual dataset, retrain, and see if test prediction changes; develop analysis to trace bias propagation; evaluate on feedforward nets across 1100 test cases on 7 fairness datasets.
AI.result: Only a small subset of labels need modification; can pinpoint critical training examples driving predictions; reveals connections between training examples and test cases; provides interpretable probes of dataset bias.
AI.conclusion: Counterfactual datasets offer a practical, interpretable approach to assess fairness and bias in training data by directly evaluating how label changes influence inference.

title: Dual-End Consistency Model
summary: The slow iterative sampling nature remains a major bottleneck for the practical deployment of diffusion and flow-based generative models. While consistency models (CMs) represent a state-of-the-art distillation-based approach for efficient generation, their large-scale application is still limited by two key issues: training instability and inflexible sampling. Existing methods seek to mitigate these problems through architectural adjustments or regularized objectives, yet overlook the critical reliance on trajectory selection. In this work, we first conduct an analysis on these two limitations: training instability originates from loss divergence induced by unstable self-supervised term, whereas sampling inflexibility arises from error accumulation. Based on these insights and analysis, we propose the Dual-End Consistency Model (DE-CM) that selects vital sub-trajectory clusters to achieve stable and effective training. DE-CM decomposes the PF-ODE trajectory and selects three critical sub-trajectories as optimization targets. Specifically, our approach leverages continuous-time CMs objectives to achieve few-step distillation and utilizes flow matching as a boundary regularizer to stabilize the training process. Furthermore, we propose a novel noise-to-noisy (N2N) mapping that can map noise to any point, thereby alleviating the error accumulation in the first step. Extensive experimental results show the effectiveness of our method: it achieves a state-of-the-art FID score of 1.70 in one-step generation on the ImageNet 256x256 dataset, outperforming existing CM-based one-step approaches.

AI.tldr: Introduces Dual-End Consistency Model (DE-CM) to improve training stability and sampling flexibility in consistency-model-based generation by selecting key sub-trajectory clusters and introducing a noise-to-noise mapping, achieving state-of-the-art one-step FID on ImageNet-256.
AI.motivation: Address training instability caused by divergence from unstable self-supervised terms and sampling inflexibility due to error accumulation in trajectory-based distillation.
AI.method: Decompose the PF-ODE trajectory and optimize three critical sub-trajectories. Use continuous-time CM objectives for few-step distillation, apply flow matching as a boundary regularizer, and introduce a noise-to-noise (N2N) mapping to map noise to any point to mitigate first-step error accumulation.
AI.result: Achieves a state-of-the-art one-step FID of 1.70 on ImageNet 256×256, outperforming existing CM-based one-step methods.
AI.conclusion: DE-CM demonstrates that selective optimization of trajectory sub-clusters plus N2N mapping yields stable training and effective few-step distillation, enabling highly efficient one-step generation.

title: RSHallu: Dual-Mode Hallucination Evaluation for Remote-Sensing Multimodal Large Language Models with Domain-Tailored Mitigation
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly adopted in remote sensing (RS) and have shown strong performance on tasks such as RS visual grounding (RSVG), RS visual question answering (RSVQA), and multimodal dialogue. However, hallucinations, which are responses inconsistent with the input RS images, severely hinder their deployment in high-stakes scenarios (e.g., emergency management and agricultural monitoring) and remain under-explored in RS. In this work, we present RSHallu, a systematic study with three deliverables: (1) we formalize RS hallucinations with an RS-oriented taxonomy and introduce image-level hallucination to capture RS-specific inconsistencies beyond object-centric errors (e.g., modality, resolution, and scene-level semantics); (2) we build a hallucination benchmark RSHalluEval (2,023 QA pairs) and enable dual-mode checking, supporting high-precision cloud auditing and low-cost reproducible local checking via a compact checker fine-tuned on RSHalluCheck dataset (15,396 QA pairs); and (3) we introduce a domain-tailored dataset RSHalluShield (30k QA pairs) for training-friendly mitigation and further propose training-free plug-and-play strategies, including decoding-time logit correction and RS-aware prompting. Across representative RS-MLLMs, our mitigation improves the hallucination-free rate by up to 21.63 percentage points under a unified protocol, while maintaining competitive performance on downstream RS tasks (RSVQA/RSVG). Code and datasets will be released.

AI.tldr: RSHallu studies hallucinations in multimodal RS-LMs, formalizes an RS-specific taxonomy including image-level errors, provides benchmarks for both high-precision cloud auditing and low-cost local checking, and proposes training-free mitigation datasets and plug-and-play strategies that yield up to 21.63 percentage points reduction in hallucinations while preserving RSVQA/RSVG performance.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in RS multimodal LLMs undermine reliability in high-stakes remote sensing tasks (e.g., disaster response, agriculture). An RS-tailored understanding, benchmarks, and mitigation strategies are needed to ensure trustworthy deployment.
