AI.method: 1) Taxonomy development with image-level RS hallucinations (beyond object-level errors). 2) RSHalluEval benchmark (2,023 QA pairs) with dual-mode checking; creation of RSHalluCheck (15,396 pairs) for compact onboard checking. 3) RSHalluShield dataset (30k pairs) for mitigation; propose training-free strategies: decoding-time logit correction and RS-aware prompting.
AI.result: Mitigation improves hallucination-free rate by up to 21.63 percentage points under a unified protocol across representative RS-MLLMs, with minimal impact on RSVQA/RSVG performance.
AI.conclusion: RS-specific hallucination taxonomy and dual-mode benchmarks enable effective, training-free mitigation; these resources facilitate safer deployment of RS-MLLMs and can be extended to other RS tasks, with code/datasets to be released.

title: DMP-3DAD: Cross-Category 3D Anomaly Detection via Realistic Depth Map Projection with Few Normal Samples
summary: Cross-category anomaly detection for 3D point clouds aims to determine whether an unseen object belongs to a target category using only a few normal examples. Most existing methods rely on category-specific training, which limits their flexibility in few-shot scenarios. In this paper, we propose DMP-3DAD, a training-free framework for cross-category 3D anomaly detection based on multi-view realistic depth map projection. Specifically, by converting point clouds into a fixed set of realistic depth images, our method leverages a frozen CLIP visual encoder to extract multi-view representations and performs anomaly detection via weighted feature similarity, which does not require any fine-tuning or category-dependent adaptation. Extensive experiments on the ShapeNetPart dataset demonstrate that DMP-3DAD achieves state-of-the-art performance under few-shot setting. The results show that the proposed approach provides a simple yet effective solution for practical cross-category 3D anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: Training-free cross-category 3D anomaly detection (DMP-3DAD) converts point clouds to multi-view depth maps, uses a frozen CLIP encoder for multi-view features, and detects anomalies via weighted feature similarity, achieving state-of-the-art few-shot results on ShapeNetPart.
AI.motivation: Category-specific, fine-tuning-heavy anomaly detectors struggle with few-shot cross-category scenarios; a versatile, training-free approach is desirable.
AI.method: Transform point clouds into a fixed set of realistic depth images from multiple views; extract features with a frozen CLIP visual encoder; compute a weighted similarity score across views to detect anomalies without any fine-tuning or category adaptation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ShapeNetPart demonstrate state-of-the-art performance under few-shot settings, indicating effective cross-category 3D anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: A simple, effective training-free framework that leverages 2D pretraining (CLIP) for 3D anomaly detection via depth-map projections, offering practical cross-category applicability without category-dependent training.

title: LLM-Based Scientific Equation Discovery via Physics-Informed Token-Regularized Policy Optimization
summary: Symbolic regression aims to distill mathematical equations from observational data. Recent approaches have successfully leveraged Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate equation hypotheses, capitalizing on their vast pre-trained scientific priors. However, existing frameworks predominantly treat the LLM as a static generator, relying on prompt-level guidance to steer exploration. This paradigm fails to update the model's internal representations based on search feedback, often yielding physically inconsistent or mathematically redundant expressions. In this work, we propose PiT-PO (Physics-informed Token-regularized Policy Optimization), a unified framework that evolves the LLM into an adaptive generator via reinforcement learning. Central to PiT-PO is a dual-constraint mechanism that rigorously enforces hierarchical physical validity while simultaneously applying fine-grained, token-level penalties to suppress redundant structures. Consequently, PiT-PO aligns LLM to produce equations that are both scientifically consistent and structurally parsimonious. Empirically, PiT-PO achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks and successfully discovers novel turbulence models for challenging fluid dynamics problems. We also demonstrate that PiT-PO empowers small-scale models to outperform closed-source giants, democratizing access to high-performance scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: RL-based physics-informed token-regularized policy optimization (PiT-PO) for symbolic regression using LLMs; enforces hierarchical physical validity and token-level sparsity penalties, achieving state-of-the-art results and discovering turbulence models; enables small models to outperform large ones.
AI.motivation: Address the static nature of LLMs in symbolic regression that rely on prompt guidance. Introduce an adaptive RL-based generator that enforces physical validity and parsimonious expressions to improve scientific discovery.
AI.method: Treat the LLM as a policy network optimized via reinforcement learning with two constraints: (1) hierarchical physical validity constraints (structure-level) and (2) token-level penalties that penalize redundant or non-parsimonious tokens. The framework evolves the model to generate equations that are both physically consistent and structurally compact.
AI.result: PiT-PO achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard symbolic regression benchmarks, discovers novel turbulence models for challenging fluid dynamics problems, and enables smaller, open models to outperform large closed-source models.
AI.conclusion: A unified, physics-informed RL framework that transforms LLMs from static generators into adaptive equation generators, yielding valid, parsimonious equations and democratizing high-performance scientific discovery.

title: Enhancing Ride-Hailing Forecasting at DiDi with Multi-View Geospatial Representation Learning from the Web
summary: The proliferation of ride-hailing services has fundamentally transformed urban mobility patterns, making accurate ride-hailing forecasting crucial for optimizing passenger experience and urban transportation efficiency. However, ride-hailing forecasting faces significant challenges due to geospatial heterogeneity and high susceptibility to external events. This paper proposes MVGR-Net(Multi-View Geospatial Representation Learning), a novel framework that addresses these challenges through a two-stage approach. In the pretraining stage, we learn comprehensive geospatial representations by integrating Points-of-Interest and temporal mobility patterns to capture regional characteristics from both semantic attribute and temporal mobility pattern views. The forecasting stage leverages these representations through a prompt-empowered framework that fine-tunes Large Language Models while incorporating external events. Extensive experiments on DiDi's real-world datasets demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: MVGR-Net uses a two-stage approach to forecast ride-hailing demand by learning multi-view geospatial representations from POIs and temporal mobility, then uses a prompt-enabled LLM fine-tuning framework with external events for forecasting. It achieves state-of-the-art results on DiDi datasets.
AI.motivation: Geospatial heterogeneity and sensitivity to external events cause significant variability in ride-hailing demand, necessitating models that can capture regional characteristics and exogenous factors.
AI.method: Stage 1 (pretraining): learn geospatial representations by integrating Points-of-Interest and temporal mobility patterns from semantic and temporal views. Stage 2 (forecasting): employ a prompt-empowered framework to fine-tune Large Language Models (LLMs) using these representations and incorporate external event data for forecasting.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world DiDi datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, validating the proposed framework.
AI.conclusion: MVGR-Net effectively addresses geospatial heterogeneity and external-event sensitivity in ride-hailing forecasting, achieving superior predictive accuracy through multi-view geospatial representations and LLM-based forecasting.

title: DeepImageSearch: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Context-Aware Image Retrieval in Visual Histories
summary: Existing multimodal retrieval systems excel at semantic matching but implicitly assume that query-image relevance can be measured in isolation. This paradigm overlooks the rich dependencies inherent in realistic visual streams, where information is distributed across temporal sequences rather than confined to single snapshots. To bridge this gap, we introduce DeepImageSearch, a novel agentic paradigm that reformulates image retrieval as an autonomous exploration task. Models must plan and perform multi-step reasoning over raw visual histories to locate targets based on implicit contextual cues. We construct DISBench, a challenging benchmark built on interconnected visual data. To address the scalability challenge of creating context-dependent queries, we propose a human-model collaborative pipeline that employs vision-language models to mine latent spatiotemporal associations, effectively offloading intensive context discovery before human verification. Furthermore, we build a robust baseline using a modular agent framework equipped with fine-grained tools and a dual-memory system for long-horizon navigation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DISBench poses significant challenges to state-of-the-art models, highlighting the necessity of incorporating agentic reasoning into next-generation retrieval systems.

AI.tldr: Reframes image retrieval as agentic, multi-step reasoning over visual histories; introduces DISBench benchmark; proposes a vision-language assisted, human-in-the-loop pipeline for context-dependent query mining; provides a modular agent baseline with dual-memory; demonstrates DISBench challenges current models and motivates agentic retrieval.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal retrieval assumes relevance can be assessed from single images, ignoring temporal dependencies and context-rich signals in visual streams. There is a need for context-aware, long-horizon reasoning to locate targets within sequences of visuals.
AI.method: Proposes DeepImageSearch, an autonomous agent that plans and executes multi-step reasoning over raw visual histories to find targets based on implicit contextual cues. Builds DISBench, a benchmark of interconnected visual data. Introduces a human-model collaboration pipeline using vision-language models to mine latent spatiotemporal associations before human verification. Develops a modular agent baseline with fine-grained tools and a dual-memory system for long-horizon navigation.
AI.result: DISBench proves challenging for state-of-the-art models, underscoring the need for agentic reasoning in retrieval. The proposed modular agent baseline demonstrates feasibility and provides a starting point for developing context-aware retrieval systems.
AI.conclusion: Agentic, multi-step reasoning over visual histories is a promising direction for next-generation retrieval systems. The DISBench benchmark and the human-model collaboration pipeline show that context-dependent queries can be effectively mined and utilized to improve retrieval performance.

title: Neural Additive Experts: Context-Gated Experts for Controllable Model Additivity
summary: The trade-off between interpretability and accuracy remains a core challenge in machine learning. Standard Generalized Additive Models (GAMs) offer clear feature attributions but are often constrained by their strictly additive nature, which can limit predictive performance. Introducing feature interactions can boost accuracy yet may obscure individual feature contributions. To address these issues, we propose Neural Additive Experts (NAEs), a novel framework that seamlessly balances interpretability and accuracy. NAEs employ a mixture of experts framework, learning multiple specialized networks per feature, while a dynamic gating mechanism integrates information across features, thereby relaxing rigid additive constraints. Furthermore, we propose targeted regularization techniques to mitigate variance among expert predictions, facilitating a smooth transition from an exclusively additive model to one that captures intricate feature interactions while maintaining clarity in feature attributions. Our theoretical analysis and experiments on synthetic data illustrate the model's flexibility, and extensive evaluations on real-world datasets confirm that NAEs achieve an optimal balance between predictive accuracy and transparent, feature-level explanations. The code is available at https://github.com/Teddy-XiongGZ/NAE.

AI.tldr: Neural Additive Experts (NAEs) balance interpretability and accuracy by using a mixture-of-experts per feature with a dynamic gating mechanism that aggregates information across features, enabling controlled feature interactions while preserving clear feature attributions.
AI.motivation: To overcome the interpretability-accuracy trade-off in ML: GAMs are interpretable but limited in interactions; adding interactions improves accuracy but harms explanations. NAEs aim to offer flexible modeling that preserves explanations while enhancing predictive performance.
AI.method: NAEs implement a mixture-of-experts framework where multiple specialized networks are learned per feature. A dynamic gating mechanism integrates across features, relaxing rigid additive constraints. Targeted regularization reduces variance among expert predictions to ensure a smooth shift from additive to interactive modeling, maintaining interpretable feature attributions.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis and experiments on synthetic data demonstrate model flexibility. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show NAEs achieve a favorable balance between predictive accuracy and transparent, feature-level explanations.Code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: NAEs provide a principled approach to trading interpretability for predictive power by controlling the degree of feature interactions via a gating mechanism and regularization, achieving an optimal balance between accuracy and interpretability.

title: Why Does RL Generalize Better Than SFT? A Data-Centric Perspective on VLM Post-Training
summary: The adaptation of large-scale Vision-Language Models (VLMs) through post-training reveals a pronounced generalization gap: models fine-tuned with Reinforcement Learning (RL) consistently achieve superior out-of-distribution (OOD) performance compared to those trained with Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). This paper posits a data-centric explanation for this phenomenon, contending that RL's generalization advantage arises from an implicit data filtering mechanism that inherently prioritizes medium-difficulty training samples. To test this hypothesis, we systematically evaluate the OOD generalization of SFT models across training datasets of varying difficulty levels. Our results confirm that data difficulty is a critical factor, revealing that training on hard samples significantly degrades OOD performance. Motivated by this finding, we introduce Difficulty-Curated SFT (DC-SFT), a straightforward method that explicitly filters the training set based on sample difficulty. Experiments show that DC-SFT not only substantially enhances OOD generalization over standard SFT, but also surpasses the performance of RL-based training, all while providing greater stability and computational efficiency. This work offers a data-centric account of the OOD generalization gap in VLMs and establishes a more efficient pathway to achieving robust generalization. Code is available at https://github.com/byyx666/DC-SFT.

AI.tldr: Difficulty-curated supervised fine-tuning (DC-SFT) filters training samples by difficulty to close the OOD generalization gap in vision-language models, outperforming RL-based methods with greater stability and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Explain the OOD generalization gap between RL-tuned and SFT-tuned VLMs with a data-centric hypothesis; investigate how training data difficulty affects OOD performance; seek efficient, robust generalization without RL complexity.
AI.method: Evaluate SFT models on datasets of varying difficulty; quantify impact of data difficulty on OOD performance; propose DC-SFT by explicitly filtering training data by sample difficulty; compare DC-SFT to RL-based training.
AI.result: Data difficulty is a critical factor; training on hard samples degrades OOD; DC-SFT significantly improves OOD over standard SFT and can surpass RL-based training, with increased stability and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A data-centric explanation for the OOD generalization gap; DC-SFT provides a practical, efficient pathway to robust generalization in VLMs; release code for replication.

title: Hierarchical Zero-Order Optimization for Deep Neural Networks
summary: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization has long been favored for its biological plausibility and its capacity to handle non-differentiable objectives, yet its computational complexity has historically limited its application in deep neural networks. Challenging the conventional paradigm that gradients propagate layer-by-layer, we propose Hierarchical Zeroth-Order (HZO) optimization, a novel divide-and-conquer strategy that decomposes the depth dimension of the network. We prove that HZO reduces the query complexity from $O(ML^2)$ to $O(ML \log L)$ for a network of width $M$ and depth $L$, representing a significant leap over existing ZO methodologies. Furthermore, we provide a detailed error analysis showing that HZO maintains numerical stability by operating near the unitary limit ($L_{lip} \approx 1$). Extensive evaluations on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet demonstrate that HZO achieves competitive accuracy compared to backpropagation.

AI.tldr: Introduces Hierarchical Zeroth-Order (HZO) optimization that divides the network depth to reduce query complexity from O(ML^2) to O(ML log L); provides stability-aware error analysis near a unitary Lipschitz regime and shows competitive accuracy on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet against backpropagation.
AI.motivation: ZO optimization is attractive for non-differentiable objectives and biological plausibility but scales poorly with depth. A depth-wise hierarchical divide-and-conquer strategy could dramatically reduce query complexity while preserving accuracy.
AI.method: Propose a hierarchical/depth-wise decomposition of the network for ZO optimization (divide-and-conquer along depth). Provide theoretical proof that query complexity scales as O(ML log L) instead of O(ML^2). Conduct error analysis showing numerical stability near the unitary limit (L_lip ≈ 1).
AI.result: The approach reduces query complexity from O(ML^2) to O(ML log L). The paper provides error analysis ensuring stability near L_lip ≈ 1. Empirical evaluations on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet report competitive accuracy relative to backpropagation.
AI.conclusion: HZO offers substantial computational efficiency gains for zeroth-order optimization in deep nets while maintaining competitive accuracy; the method is validated both theoretically and empirically, though practical deployment may depend on how depth-wise partitioning affects bias-variance and scalability to very large architectures.

title: Don't Eliminate Cut: Exponential Separations in LLM-Based Theorem Proving
summary: We develop a theoretical analysis of LLM-guided formal theorem proving in interactive proof assistants (e.g., Lean) by modeling tactic proposal as a stochastic policy in a finite-horizon deterministic MDP. To capture modern representation learning, we treat the state and action spaces as general compact metric spaces and assume Lipschitz policies. To explain the gap between worst-case hardness and empirical success, we introduce problem distributions generated by a reference policy $q$, including a latent-variable model in which proofs exhibit reusable cut/lemma/sketch structure represented by a proof DAG. Under a top-$k$ search protocol and Tsybakov-type margin conditions, we derive lower bounds on finite-horizon success probability that decompose into search and learning terms, with learning controlled by sequential Rademacher/covering complexity. Our main separation result shows that when cut elimination expands a DAG of depth $D$ into a cut-free tree of size $Ω(Λ^D)$ while the cut-aware hierarchical process has size $O(λ^D)$ with $λ\llΛ$, a flat (cut-free) learner provably requires exponentially more data than a cut-aware hierarchical learner. This provides a principled justification for subgoal decomposition in recent agentic theorem provers.

AI.tldr: The work presents a theoretical framework for LLM-guided formal proof in interactive provers by modeling tactic proposal as a stochastic policy in a finite-horizon MDP, with state/action spaces as compact metric spaces and Lipschitz policies. It shows an exponential data-efficiency gap between cut-aware hierarchical learners and flat cut-free learners under certain DAG-depth growth, justifying subgoal decomposition.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between worst-case hardness and empirical success in LLM-guided theorem proving; incorporate modern representation learning; quantify how subgoal decomposition affects sample complexity and performance in proof search.
AI.method: Model the proof state as a compact metric space and tactic proposals as Lipschitz policies within a finite-horizon deterministic MDP. Introduce problem distributions generated by a reference policy q, including a latent-variable DAG structure for reusable proof segments. Analyze under a top-k search and Tsybakov margin conditions. Derive lower bounds on finite-horizon success probability decomposed into search and learning components; bound learning via sequential Rademacher/covering complexity. Establish a separation where expanding a DAG of depth D into a cut-free tree of size Ω(Λ^D) with cut-aware hierarchical policy having size O(λ^D) (λ≪Λ) yields exponential data requirements for flat learners but not for hierarchical ones.
AI.result: Lower bounds show an exponential data-need gap: a cut-free learner requires exponentially more data than a cut-aware hierarchical learner under the stated growth rates. This separation provides theoretical justification for subgoal decomposition used in modern agentic theorem provers.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a principled explanation for the empirical success of subgoal-decomposition in LLM-guided theorem proving, linking representation-learning assumptions to sample-efficient policy learning. It also outlines assumptions (Lipschitz policies, top-k search, margin conditions) and suggests directions for extending to broader search protocols and DAG-to-tree transformations.

title: Resource-Efficient RGB-Only Action Recognition for Edge Deployment
summary: Action recognition on edge devices poses stringent constraints on latency, memory, storage, and power consumption. While auxiliary modalities such as skeleton and depth information can enhance recognition performance, they often require additional sensors or computationally expensive pose-estimation pipelines, limiting practicality for edge use. In this work, we propose a compact RGB-only network tailored for efficient on-device inference. Our approach builds upon an X3D-style backbone augmented with Temporal Shift, and further introduces selective temporal adaptation and parameter-free attention. Extensive experiments on the NTU RGB+D 60 and 120 benchmarks demonstrate a strong accuracy-efficiency balance. Moreover, deployment-level profiling on the Jetson Orin Nano verifies a smaller on-device footprint and practical resource utilization compared to existing RGB-based action recognition techniques.

AI.tldr: A compact RGB-only action-recognition network for edge devices using an X3D-style backbone with Temporal Shift, selective temporal adaptation, and parameter-free attention, achieving favorable accuracy-efficiency on NTU RGB+D 60/120 and a reduced on-device footprint on Jetson Orin Nano.
AI.motivation: Edge-processing constraints (latency, memory, storage, power) and the desire to avoid reliance on skeleton/depth modalities that require extra sensors or heavy pose estimation.
AI.method: X3D-style backbone augmented with Temporal Shift. Adds selective temporal adaptation and parameter-free attention for efficiency on-device.
AI.result: Strong accuracy-efficiency balance on NTU RGB+D 60/120. Deployment-level profiling on Jetson Orin Nano shows smaller on-device footprint and practical resource utilization compared to existing RGB-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RGB-only approach is viable for efficient on-device action recognition, balancing accuracy with resource constraints.

title: Prioritize the Process, Not Just the Outcome: Rewarding Latent Thought Trajectories Improves Reasoning in Looped Language Models
summary: Looped Language Models (LoopLMs) perform multi-step latent reasoning prior to token generation and outperform conventional LLMs on reasoning benchmarks at smaller parameter budgets. However, attempts to further improve LoopLM reasoning with reinforcement learning have failed - standard objectives such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) only assign credit to the final latent state, creating a fundamental mismatch with the model's internal computation. To resolve this, we introduce RLTT (Reward Latent Thought Trajectories), a reinforcement learning framework which distributes reward across the full latent reasoning trajectory. RLTT provides dense, trajectory-level credit assignment without relying on external verifiers and can directly replace GRPO with negligible overhead. Across extensive experiments with Ouro-2.6B-Thinking under identical training and inference conditions, RLTT yields substantial improvements over GRPO on challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, improving accuracy by +14.4% on MATH-500, +16.6% on AIME24, and +10.0% on BeyondAIME. Despite being trained exclusively on mathematics, RLTT also transfers effectively to non-mathematical reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness of trajectory-level credit assignment for reinforcement learning in LoopLMs.

AI.tldr: RLTT distributes reward across full latent thought trajectories in LoopLMs, replacing GRPO, yielding notable gains on math benchmarks and transfer to non-math tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard reinforcement learning objectives like GRPO credit only the final latent state, creating a mismatch with the model's internal, multi-step reasoning process. Dense, trajectory-level credit assignment is needed to align learning with internal computation and improve reasoning performance.
AI.method: Introduce RLTT (Reward Latent Thought Trajectories), a reinforcement learning framework that assigns rewards along the entire latent reasoning trajectory. It replaces GRPO with negligible overhead, using the full latent thought sequence for credit assignment. Trained on Ouro-2.6B-Thinking under identical conditions; evaluates on mathematics benchmarks (MATH-500, AIME24, BeyondAIME) and transfers to non-mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.result: RLTT yields substantial improvements over GRPO: +14.4% on MATH-500, +16.6% on AIME24, and +10.0% on BeyondAIME. It also demonstrates effective transfer to non-mathematical reasoning benchmarks despite being trained exclusively on mathematics.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-level credit assignment is effective for reinforcement learning in LoopLMs. RLTT can replace GRPO with negligible overhead and leads to improved multi-step reasoning performance, with demonstrated transfer to non-mathematical domains.

title: Flow caching for autoregressive video generation
summary: Autoregressive models, often built on Transformer architectures, represent a powerful paradigm for generating ultra-long videos by synthesizing content in sequential chunks. However, this sequential generation process is notoriously slow. While caching strategies have proven effective for accelerating traditional video diffusion models, existing methods assume uniform denoising across all frames-an assumption that breaks down in autoregressive models where different video chunks exhibit varying similarity patterns at identical timesteps. In this paper, we present FlowCache, the first caching framework specifically designed for autoregressive video generation. Our key insight is that each video chunk should maintain independent caching policies, allowing fine-grained control over which chunks require recomputation at each timestep. We introduce a chunkwise caching strategy that dynamically adapts to the unique denoising characteristics of each chunk, complemented by a joint importance-redundancy optimized KV cache compression mechanism that maintains fixed memory bounds while preserving generation quality. Our method achieves remarkable speedups of 2.38 times on MAGI-1 and 6.7 times on SkyReels-V2, with negligible quality degradation (VBench: 0.87 increase and 0.79 decrease respectively). These results demonstrate that FlowCache successfully unlocks the potential of autoregressive models for real-time, ultra-long video generation-establishing a new benchmark for efficient video synthesis at scale. The code is available at https://github.com/mikeallen39/FlowCache.

AI.tldr: FlowCache introduces chunkwise caching for autoregressive video generation, enabling independent per-chunk caching policies and a joint importance-redundancy optimized KV cache compression to speed up ultra-long video synthesis with minimal quality loss.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive video generation is slow due to sequential dependency. Existing caching methods assume uniform denoising across frames, which is invalid for autoregressive generation where different chunks exhibit distinct similarity patterns over timesteps. A per-chunk caching strategy is needed to balance speed and quality.
AI.method: Propose a chunkwise caching framework with independent caching policies per video chunk. Dynamically decide which chunks require recomputation at each timestep. Use a joint importance-redundancy optimized KV cache compression to maintain fixed memory bounds while preserving generation quality, integrating into Transformer-based autoregressive video models.
AI.result: Achieves 2.38x speedup on MAGI-1 and 6.7x on SkyReels-V2. Quality changes reported by VBench: +0.87 (increase) on MAGI-1 and -0.79 (decrease) on SkyReels-V2, indicating negligible degradation overall. Code available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: FlowCache demonstrates that chunkwise caching enables real-time, ultra-long autoregressive video generation and sets a new efficiency benchmark for large-scale video synthesis, with publicly available code.

title: Hyperspectral Smoke Segmentation via Mixture of Prototypes
summary: Smoke segmentation is critical for wildfire management and industrial safety applications. Traditional visible-light-based methods face limitations due to insufficient spectral information, particularly struggling with cloud interference and semi-transparent smoke regions. To address these challenges, we introduce hyperspectral imaging for smoke segmentation and present the first hyperspectral smoke segmentation dataset (HSSDataset) with carefully annotated samples collected from over 18,000 frames across 20 real-world scenarios using a Many-to-One annotations protocol. However, different spectral bands exhibit varying discriminative capabilities across spatial regions, necessitating adaptive band weighting strategies. We decompose this into three technical challenges: spectral interaction contamination, limited spectral pattern modeling, and complex weighting router problems. We propose a mixture of prototypes (MoP) network with: (1) Band split for spectral isolation, (2) Prototype-based spectral representation for diverse patterns, and (3) Dual-level router for adaptive spatial-aware band weighting. We further construct a multispectral dataset (MSSDataset) with RGB-infrared images. Extensive experiments validate superior performance across both hyperspectral and multispectral modalities, establishing a new paradigm for spectral-based smoke segmentation.

AI.tldr: Introduces a hyperspectral smoke segmentation framework using a Mixture of Prototypes (MoP) network, along with two new datasets (HSSDataset and MSSDataset) and demonstrates state-of-the-art performance for spectral-based smoke segmentation.
AI.motivation: Visible-light smoke segmentation is hindered by limited spectral information, cloud interference, and semi-transparent regions; hyperspectral imaging and adaptive spectral weighting can improve discrimination of smoke in complex scenes. A dataset with real-world scenarios is needed to drive progress.
AI.method: MoP network with band split for spectral isolation, prototype-based spectral representation to capture diverse patterns, and a dual-level router for adaptive spatial-aware band weighting. Datasets: HSSDataset (hyperspectral) with 18k frames across 20 real-world scenarios using Many-to-One annotations; MSSDataset (RGB-infrared).
AI.result: Extensive experiments show superior performance on both hyperspectral and multispectral modalities, establishing a new paradigm for spectral-based smoke segmentation.
AI.conclusion: The MoP framework effectively leverages spectral diversity for improved smoke segmentation and enables new capabilities for wildfire management and industrial safety; the work also provides valuable datasets for the community. Future work may address scalability, real-time performance, and generalization to broader conditions.

title: What Makes Value Learning Efficient in Residual Reinforcement Learning?
summary: Residual reinforcement learning (RL) enables stable online refinement of expressive pretrained policies by freezing the base and learning only bounded corrections. However, value learning in residual RL poses unique challenges that remain poorly understood. In this work, we identify two key bottlenecks: cold start pathology, where the critic lacks knowledge of the value landscape around the base policy, and structural scale mismatch, where the residual contribution is dwarfed by the base action. Through systematic investigation, we uncover the mechanisms underlying these bottlenecks, revealing that simple yet principled solutions suffice: base-policy transitions serve as an essential value anchor for implicit warmup, and critic normalization effectively restores representation sensitivity for discerning value differences. Based on these insights, we propose DAWN (Data-Anchored Warmup and Normalization), a minimal approach targeting efficient value learning in residual RL. By addressing these bottlenecks, DAWN demonstrates substantial efficiency gains across diverse benchmarks, policy architectures, and observation modalities.

AI.tldr: DAWN: Data-Anchored Warmup and Normalization for residual RL. It targets value-learning bottlenecks (cold-start and scale mismatch) via base-policy transitions as a value anchor and critic normalization, achieving faster, more data-efficient value learning across architectures and modalities.
AI.motivation: Residual RL freezes the base policy and learns corrections, but the critic faces a cold start (lacking context around the base policy) and a scale mismatch (residual can be overwhelmed by the base action). A minimal, principled fix is sought to improve value learning efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce two components: (1) use base-policy transitions as an implicit warmup anchor for value estimates; (2) apply critic normalization to restore representation sensitivity and discern value differences. Combine into the DAWN framework.
AI.result: DAWN yields substantial efficiency gains in value learning across diverse benchmarks, policy architectures, and observation modalities, with improved stability and data efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A minimal, practical solution to residual RL value-learning bottlenecks; DAWN generalizes across settings and simplifies integration.

title: Stride-Net: Fairness-Aware Disentangled Representation Learning for Chest X-Ray Diagnosis
comment: 6 pages, 2 Tables, 3 Figures. Our code is available https://github.com/Daraksh/Fairness_StrideNet
summary: Deep neural networks for chest X-ray classification achieve strong average performance, yet often underperform for specific demographic subgroups, raising critical concerns about clinical safety and equity. Existing debiasing methods frequently yield inconsistent improvements across datasets or attain fairness by degrading overall diagnostic utility, treating fairness as a post hoc constraint rather than a property of the learned representation. In this work, we propose Stride-Net (Sensitive Attribute Resilient Learning via Disentanglement and Learnable Masking with Embedding Alignment), a fairness-aware framework that learns disease-discriminative yet demographically invariant representations for chest X-ray analysis. Stride-Net operates at the patch level, using a learnable stride-based mask to select label-aligned image regions while suppressing sensitive attribute information through adversarial confusion loss. To anchor representations in clinical semantics and discourage shortcut learning, we further enforce semantic alignment between image features and BioBERT-based disease label embeddings via Group Optimal Transport. We evaluate Stride-Net on the MIMIC-CXR and CheXpert benchmarks across race and intersectional race-gender subgroups. Across architectures including ResNet and Vision Transformers, Stride-Net consistently improves fairness metrics while matching or exceeding baseline accuracy, achieving a more favorable accuracy-fairness trade-off than prior debiasing approaches. Our code is available at https://github.com/Daraksh/Fairness_StrideNet.

AI.tldr: Stride-Net is a fairness-aware chest X-ray model that learns disease-discriminative yet demographically invariant representations by masking image patches, using adversarial confusion to suppress sensitive attributes, and aligning image features with disease label embeddings via Group Optimal Transport; it improves fairness with preserved or improved accuracy across datasets and subgroups.
AI.motivation: Address systematic underperformance of chest X-ray classifiers on demographic subgroups and limitations of post hoc debiasing methods that degrade diagnostic utility; aim to learn representations that are both clinically meaningful and demographically unbiased.
AI.method: 1) Patch-level, learnable stride-based mask selects label-aligned regions while suppressing sensitive attribute information via adversarial confusion loss. 2) Semantic alignment of image features with BioBERT-based disease label embeddings using Group Optimal Transport to anchor clinical semantics and discourage shortcut learning. 3) Evaluation across ResNet and Vision Transformers on MIMIC-CXR and CheXpert for race and race-gender subgroups.
AI.result: Stride-Net consistently improves fairness metrics across architectures while maintaining or surpassing baseline diagnostic accuracy, yielding a more favorable accuracy-fairness trade-off compared to prior debiasing approaches on MIMIC-CXR and CheXpert.
AI.conclusion: A unified framework combining patch-based feature selection, adversarial debiasing, and semantic embedding alignment can produce robust, demographically invariant representations for chest X-ray classification, with strong cross-dataset and cross-architecture generalization and improved fairness without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Bridging the Compression-Precision Paradox: A Hybrid Architecture for Clinical EEG Report Generation with Guaranteed Measurement Accuracy
summary:
Automated EEG monitoring requires clinician-level precision for seizure detection and reporting. Clinical EEG recordings exceed LLM context windows, requiring extreme compression (400:1+ ratios) that destroys fine-grained temporal precision. A 0.5 Hz error distinguishes absence epilepsy from Lennox-Gastaut syndrome. LLMs lack inherent time-series comprehension and rely on statistical associations from compressed representations. This dual limitation causes systems to hallucinate clinically incorrect measurement values.
  We separate measurement extraction from text generation. Our hybrid architecture computes exact clinical values via signal processing before compression, employs a cross-modal bridge for EEG-to-language translation, and uses parameter-efficient fine-tuning with constrained decoding around frozen slots. Multirate sampling maintains long-range context while preserving event-level precision. Evaluation on TUH and CHB-MIT datasets achieves 60% fewer false alarms, 50% faster detection, and sub-clinical measurement precision. This is the first system guaranteeing clinical measurement accuracy in automated EEG reports.

AI.tldr: A hybrid EEG-to-language system preserves clinical measurement accuracy by decoupling signal extraction from text generation, enabling exact measurements before compression and cross-modal translation to language, yielding fewer false alarms and faster detection.
AI.motivation: Clinical EEG reporting requires precise temporal measurements; conventional LLM-based pipelines compress data and rely on statistical associations, risking clinically meaningful errors and hallucinations. A system that preserves raw measurements while translating to language is needed.
AI.method: Compute exact clinical values via signal processing prior to compression; use a cross-modal bridge to translate EEG measurements into language; apply parameter-efficient fine-tuning with constrained decoding around fixed slots; employ multirate sampling to maintain long-range context without sacrificing event-level precision.
AI.result: On TUH and CHB-MIT datasets, the approach achieved ~60% reduction in false alarms, ~50% faster detection, and sub-clinical measurement precision, claiming to be the first system to guarantee clinical measurement accuracy in automated EEG reports.
AI.conclusion: Separating measurement extraction from text generation and preserving time-series precision through multirate, cross-modal translation yields clinically reliable automated EEG reporting, addressing a key bottleneck in LLM-based medical automation.

title: Chart Specification: Structural Representations for Incentivizing VLM Reasoning in Chart-to-Code Generation
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown promise in generating plotting code from chart images, yet achieving structural fidelity remains challenging. Existing approaches largely rely on supervised fine-tuning, encouraging surface-level token imitation rather than faithful modeling of underlying chart structure, which often leads to hallucinated or semantically inconsistent outputs. We propose Chart Specification, a structured intermediate representation that shifts training from text imitation to semantically grounded supervision. Chart Specification filters syntactic noise to construct a structurally balanced training set and supports a Spec-Align Reward that provides fine-grained, verifiable feedback on structural correctness, enabling reinforcement learning to enforce consistent plotting logic. Experiments on three public benchmarks show that our method consistently outperforms prior approaches. With only 3K training samples, we achieve strong data efficiency, surpassing leading baselines by up to 61.7% on complex benchmarks, and scaling to 4K samples establishes new state-of-the-art results across all evaluated metrics. Overall, our results demonstrate that precise structural supervision offers an efficient pathway to high-fidelity chart-to-code generation. Code and dataset are available at: https://github.com/Mighten/chart-specification-paper

AI.tldr: A structured intermediate representation (Chart Specification) with semantically grounded supervision and Spec-Align rewards improves chart-to-code fidelity and data efficiency, outperforming baselines on three benchmarks with as few as 3K training samples.
AI.motivation: Current VLM-to-chart code generation relies on surface-level token imitation, leading to hallucinations and semantically inconsistent outputs. A semantically grounded training signal is needed to enforce accurate chart structure.
AI.method: Introduce Chart Specification as a structured intermediate representation that filters syntactic noise to create a structurally balanced training set. Introduce Spec-Align Reward to provide fine-grained, verifiable feedback on structural correctness. Use reinforcement learning to enforce consistent plotting logic based on this supervision.
AI.result: Across three public benchmarks, the approach consistently outperforms prior methods. With only 3K training samples, it surpasses leading baselines by up to 61.7% on complex benchmarks; scaling to 4K samples establishes new state-of-the-art results across all evaluated metrics.
AI.conclusion: Precise structural supervision enables high-fidelity chart-to-code generation and data-efficient learning; the method demonstrates the viability of semantically grounded supervision for VLM-to-chart tasks. Code and dataset are publicly available.

title: VESPO: Variational Sequence-Level Soft Policy Optimization for Stable Off-Policy LLM Training
summary: Training stability remains a central challenge in reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models (LLMs). Policy staleness, asynchronous training, and mismatches between training and inference engines all cause the behavior policy to diverge from the current policy, risking training collapse. Importance sampling provides a principled correction for this distribution shift but suffers from high variance; existing remedies such as token-level clipping and sequence-level normalization lack a unified theoretical foundation. We propose Variational sEquence-level Soft Policy Optimization (VESPO). By incorporating variance reduction into a variational formulation over proposal distributions, VESPO derives a closed-form reshaping kernel that operates directly on sequence-level importance weights without length normalization. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that VESPO maintains stable training under staleness ratios up to 64x and fully asynchronous execution, and delivers consistent gains across both dense and Mixture-of-Experts models. Code is available at https://github.com/FloyedShen/VESPO

AI.tldr: VESPO introduces a variational sequence-level soft policy optimization to stabilize RL fine-tuning of LLMs by reducing variance in importance-weighted updates without length normalization, enabling robust training under high staleness and asynchronous execution and improving math-reasoning benchmarks for dense and Mixture-of-Experts models.
AI.motivation: Policy staleness, asynchronous distributed training, and train–inference mismatch cause the behavior policy to drift from the current policy, risking training collapse. Importance sampling has high variance, and existing token- or sequence-level fixes lack a unified theoretical foundation.
AI.method: Propose Variational sEquence-level Soft Policy Optimization (VESPO) by formulating a variational objective over proposal distributions to achieve variance reduction; derive a closed-form reshaping kernel that acts on sequence-level importance weights without length normalization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on mathematical reasoning benchmarks shows stable training under staleness ratios up to 64x and fully asynchronous execution; consistent performance gains across both dense and Mixture-of-Experts models; code released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: VESPO offers a principled variance-reduction mechanism for sequence-level importance sampling in RL for LLM fine-tuning, enabling stable, scalable training and reliable gains, with potential for broader applicability and future theoretical refinement.

title: ResWorld: Temporal Residual World Model for End-to-End Autonomous Driving
summary: The comprehensive understanding capabilities of world models for driving scenarios have significantly improved the planning accuracy of end-to-end autonomous driving frameworks. However, the redundant modeling of static regions and the lack of deep interaction with trajectories hinder world models from exerting their full effectiveness. In this paper, we propose Temporal Residual World Model (TR-World), which focuses on dynamic object modeling. By calculating the temporal residuals of scene representations, the information of dynamic objects can be extracted without relying on detection and tracking. TR-World takes only temporal residuals as input, thus predicting the future spatial distribution of dynamic objects more precisely. By combining the prediction with the static object information contained in the current BEV features, accurate future BEV features can be obtained. Furthermore, we propose Future-Guided Trajectory Refinement (FGTR) module, which conducts interaction between prior trajectories (predicted from the current scene representation) and the future BEV features. This module can not only utilize future road conditions to refine trajectories, but also provides sparse spatial-temporal supervision on future BEV features to prevent world model collapse. Comprehensive experiments conducted on the nuScenes and NAVSIM datasets demonstrate that our method, namely ResWorld, achieves state-of-the-art planning performance. The code is available at https://github.com/mengtan00/ResWorld.git.

AI.tldr: Introduces TR-World and FGTR (collectively ResWorld) for driving world models: a temporal residual-based dynamic object modeling approach that eschews detection/tracking, paired with a Future-Guided Trajectory Refinement module to leverage future BEV features for improved planning; achieves state-of-the-art results on nuScenes and NAVSIM.
AI.motivation: Current world models suffer from redundant static-region modeling and weak interaction with predicted trajectories. By focusing on dynamic objects via temporal residuals and integrating with static BEV information, the method aims to improve future spatial-object distribution predictions and planning accuracy.
AI.method: Temporal Residual World Model (TR-World) computes temporal residuals of scene representations to isolate dynamic object information, using only these residuals as input to predict future dynamic object distributions; static BEV features from the current frame are merged to obtain accurate future BEV. The Future-Guided Trajectory Refinement (FGTR) modules enables interaction between prior trajectories and future BEV features, leveraging future road conditions for refinement and providing sparse spatio-temporal supervision to prevent world model collapse.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on nuScenes and NAVSIM show state-of-the-art planning performance for the ResWorld approach; code released at https://github.com/mengtan00/ResWorld.git.
AI.conclusion: Temporal residual-based dynamic modeling without explicit detection/tracking improves planning accuracy, while FGTR utilizes future BEV information to refine trajectories and stabilize training. ResWorld demonstrates strong empirical gains and strong potential for end-to-end driving systems.

title: FastUSP: A Multi-Level Collaborative Acceleration Framework for Distributed Diffusion Model Inference
summary: Large-scale diffusion models such as FLUX (12B parameters) and Stable Diffusion 3 (8B parameters) require multi-GPU parallelism for efficient inference. Unified Sequence Parallelism (USP), which combines Ulysses and Ring attention mechanisms, has emerged as the state-of-the-art approach for distributed attention computation. However, existing USP implementations suffer from significant inefficiencies including excessive kernel launch overhead and suboptimal computation-communication scheduling. In this paper, we propose \textbf{FastUSP}, a multi-level optimization framework that integrates compile-level optimization (graph compilation with CUDA Graphs and computation-communication reordering), communication-level optimization (FP8 quantized collective communication), and operator-level optimization (pipelined Ring attention with double buffering). We evaluate FastUSP on FLUX (12B) and Qwen-Image models across 2, 4, and 8 NVIDIA RTX 5090 GPUs. On FLUX, FastUSP achieves consistent \textbf{1.12$\times$--1.16$\times$} end-to-end speedup over baseline USP, with compile-level optimization contributing the dominant improvement. On Qwen-Image, FastUSP achieves \textbf{1.09$\times$} speedup on 2 GPUs; on 4--8 GPUs, we identify a PyTorch Inductor compatibility limitation with Ring attention that prevents compile optimization, while baseline USP scales to 1.30$\times$--1.46$\times$ of 2-GPU performance. We further provide a detailed analysis of the performance characteristics of distributed diffusion inference, revealing that kernel launch overhead -- rather than communication latency -- is the primary bottleneck on modern high-bandwidth GPU interconnects.

AI.tldr: FastUSP delivers end-to-end speedups for USP-based multi-GPU diffusion inference via a multi-level optimization framework; gains ~1.12x-1.16x on FLUX 12B, ~1.09x on Qwen-Image 2 GPUs; 4–8 GPUs show constraints due to PyTorch Inductor; kernel launch overhead is the main bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiencies in Unified Sequence Parallelism (USP) for large diffusion models, notably kernel launch overhead and suboptimal scheduling, by applying structured multi-level optimizations.
AI.method: Integrate compile-level optimization (graph compilation with CUDA Graphs and computation-communication reordering), communication-level optimization (FP8 quantized collective communication), and operator-level optimization (pipelined Ring attention with double buffering) into a framework named FastUSP; evaluate on FLUX 12B and Qwen-Image across 2, 4, and 8 GPUs; analyze performance bottlenecks.
AI.result: On FLUX (12B), FastUSP achieves 1.12x–1.16x end-to-end speedup over baseline USP, with compile-level optimization contributing the majority of gains. On Qwen-Image, FastUSP achieves 1.09x speedup on 2 GPUs; on 4–8 GPUs, a PyTorch Inductor compatibility limitation prevents compile optimization, while baseline USP scales to 1.30x–1.46x of 2-GPU performance. Kernel launch overhead, not communication latency, is identified as the primary bottleneck on modern high-bandwidth interconnects.
AI.conclusion: Compile-level optimization is the dominant contributor to performance gains; FastUSP reduces kernel launch overhead in USP-based diffusion inference. Compatibility with PyTorch Inductor at larger scales remains a barrier; future work should enhance Inductor integration and further reduce kernel overhead and optimize scheduling.

title: Interpretable Graph-Level Anomaly Detection via Contrast with Normal Prototypes
summary: The task of graph-level anomaly detection (GLAD) is to identify anomalous graphs that deviate significantly from the majority of graphs in a dataset. While deep GLAD methods have shown promising performance, their black-box nature limits their reliability and deployment in real-world applications. Although some recent methods have made attempts to provide explanations for anomaly detection results, they either provide explanations without referencing normal graphs, or rely on abstract latent vectors as prototypes rather than concrete graphs from the dataset. To address these limitations, we propose Prototype-based Graph-Level Anomaly Detection (ProtoGLAD), an interpretable unsupervised framework that provides explanation for each detected anomaly by explicitly contrasting with its nearest normal prototype graph. It employs a point-set kernel to iteratively discover multiple normal prototype graphs and their associated clusters from the dataset, then identifying graphs distant from all discovered normal clusters as anomalies. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate that ProtoGLAD achieves competitive anomaly detection performance compared to state-of-the-art GLAD methods while providing better human-interpretable prototype-based explanations.

AI.tldr: ProtoGLAD is an unsupervised, interpretable GLAD method that discovers multiple normal prototype graphs using a point-set kernel and explains anomalies by contrasting them with nearest prototypes, achieving competitive performance with improved interpretability.
AI.motivation: To address the black-box nature and lack of concrete, dataset-based explanations in graph-level anomaly detection (GLAD); prior explanations either reference abstract latent vectors or omit normal graphs.
AI.method: Iteratively discovers normal prototype graphs and their clusters via a point-set kernel; represents graphs in a way that allows distance to prototypes; flags graphs distant from all normal clusters as anomalies; provides explanations by contrasting anomalies with their nearest prototypes.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world datasets shows ProtoGLAD achieves competitive anomaly detection performance while offering more human-friendly, prototype-based explanations.
AI.conclusion: ProtoGLAD delivers an interpretable, unsupervised GLAD framework grounded in concrete prototype graphs, balancing detection performance with interpretability and aiding practical deployment.

title: Online Min-Max Optimization: From Individual Regrets to Cumulative Saddle Points
summary: We propose and study an online version of min-max optimization based on cumulative saddle points under a variety of performance measures beyond convex-concave settings. After first observing the incompatibility of (static) Nash equilibrium (SNE-Reg$_T$) with individual regrets even for strongly convex-strongly concave functions, we propose an alternate \emph{static} duality gap (SDual-Gap$_T$) inspired by the online convex optimization (OCO) framework. We provide algorithms that, using a reduction to classic OCO problems, achieve bounds for SDual-Gap$_T$~and a novel \emph{dynamic} saddle point regret (DSP-Reg$_T$), which we suggest naturally represents a min-max version of the dynamic regret in OCO. We derive our bounds for SDual-Gap$_T$~and DSP-Reg$_T$~under strong convexity-strong concavity and a min-max notion of exponential concavity (min-max EC), and in addition we establish a class of functions satisfying min-max EC~that captures a two-player variant of the classic portfolio selection problem. Finally, for a dynamic notion of regret compatible with individual regrets, we derive bounds under a two-sided Polyak-Łojasiewicz (PL) condition.

AI.tldr: Online min-max optimization via cumulative saddle points; introduces static duality gap (SDual-Gap_T) and dynamic saddle point regret (DSP-Reg_T); reduces to classic OCO; provides bounds under strong convexity-strong concavity and min-max exponential concavity (min-max EC); identifies a function class including a two-player portfolio variant; also derives regret bounds under a two-sided Polyak-Łojasiewicz (PL) condition.
AI.motivation: Static Nash equilibrium (SNE-Reg_T) is incompatible with individual regret notions in online settings for min-max problems. A static duality gap and a dynamic regret notion better capture online performance and adaptivity.
AI.method: Introduce SDual-Gap_T and DSP-Reg_T; reduce the online min-max problem to classic Online Convex Optimization (OCO) problems to derive performance guarantees; analyze under strong convexity-strong concavity (SC/SC) and min-max exponential concavity (min-max EC); characterize a function class satisfying min-max EC (includes a two-player portfolio variant); derive PL-based bounds for individual regret compatibility.
AI.result: Establish bounds for SDual-Gap_T and DSP-Reg_T under SC/SC and min-max EC; identify a function class satisfying min-max EC that includes a two-player portfolio variant; obtain dynamic regret bounds under a two-sided PL condition compatible with individual regrets.
AI.conclusion: Proposes and analyzes an online framework for min-max optimization with new performance metrics (SDual-Gap_T, DSP-Reg_T); provides theoretical guarantees, broad function classes, and potential applications in online portfolio selection and two-player settings.

title: Gauss-Newton Unlearning for the LLM Era
summary: Standard large language model training can create models that produce outputs their trainer deems unacceptable in deployment. The probability of these outputs can be reduced using methods such as LLM unlearning. However, unlearning a set of data (called the forget set) can degrade model performance on other distributions where the trainer wants to retain the model's behavior. To improve this trade-off, we demonstrate that using the forget set to compute only a few uphill Gauss-Newton steps provides a conceptually simple, state-of-the-art unlearning approach for LLMs. While Gauss-Newton steps adapt Newton's method to non-linear models, it is non-trivial to efficiently and accurately compute such steps for LLMs. Hence, our approach crucially relies on parametric Hessian approximations such as Kronecker-Factored Approximate Curvature (K-FAC). We call this combined approach K-FADE (K-FAC for Distribution Erasure). Our evaluation on the WMDP and ToFU benchmarks demonstrates that K-FADE suppresses outputs from the forget set and approximates, in output space, the results of retraining without the forget set. Critically, our method does this while altering the outputs on the retain set less than previous methods. This is because K-FADE transforms a constraint on the model's outputs across the entire retain set into a constraint on the model's weights, allowing the algorithm to minimally change the model's behavior on the retain set at each step. Moreover, the unlearning updates computed by K-FADE can be reapplied later if the model undergoes further training, allowing unlearning to be cheaply maintained.

AI.tldr: K-FADE performs efficient unlearning for LLMs by taking a few uphill Gauss-Newton steps computed with K-FAC Hessian approximations, using the forget set to constrain updates while preserving performance on the retain set. The updates can be reapplied after further training, enabling cheap maintenance.
AI.motivation: Standard unlearning methods can degrade model performance on retained distributions; there is a need for a simple, effective, and reusable approach that minimizes unintended changes to retain-set behavior.
AI.method: Compute uphill Gauss-Newton steps constrained by the forget set, approximating the Hessian with Kronecker-Factored Approximate Curvature (K-FAC); transform a retain-set output constraint into a weight-space constraint, apply a few steps, and allow updates to be re-applied if further training occurs.
AI.result: K-FADE suppresses forget-set outputs and closely matches retraining-without-forget-set in output space, while causing less disruption to the retain set than previous methods on WMDP and ToFU benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: K-FADE provides a simple, state-of-the-art unlearning approach for LLMs that preserves retain-set behavior, is computationally efficient due to Hessian approximation, and is maintainable through subsequent training.

title: Healthy Harvests: A Comparative Look at Guava Disease Classification Using InceptionV3
comment: 6 pages, 13 figures, his is the author's accepted manuscript of a paper accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 16th International IEEE Conference on Computing, Communication and Networking Technologies (ICCCNT 2025). The final published version will be available via IEEE Xplore
summary: Guava fruits often suffer from many diseases. This can harm fruit quality and fruit crop yield. Early identification is important for minimizing damage and ensuring fruit health. This study focuses on 3 different categories for classifying diseases. These are Anthracnose, Fruit flies, and Healthy fruit. The data set used in this study is collected from Mendeley Data. This dataset contains 473 original images of Guava. These images vary in size and format. The original dataset was resized to 256x256 pixels with RGB color mode for better consistency. After this, the Data augmentation process is applied to improve the dataset by generating variations of the original images. The augmented dataset consists of 3784 images using advanced preprocessing techniques. Two deep learning models were implemented to classify the images. The InceptionV3 model is well known for its advanced framework. These apply multiple convolutional filters for obtaining different features effectively. On the other hand, the ResNet50 model helps to train deeper networks by using residual learning. The InceptionV3 model achieved the impressive accuracy of 98.15%, and ResNet50got 94.46% accuracy. Data mixing methods such as CutMix and MixUp were applied to enhance the model's robustness. The confusion matrix was used to evaluate the overall model performance of both InceptionV3 and Resnet50. Additionally, SHAP analysis is used to improve interpretability, which helps to find the significant parts of the image for the model prediction. This study purposes to highlight how advanced models enhan

AI.tldr: CNN-based classification of guava fruit diseases using InceptionV3 and ResNet50 with data augmentation and SHAP interpretability; InceptionV3 achieves 98.15% accuracy, ResNet50 94.46%.
AI.motivation: Early, reliable identification of guava diseases to minimize yield loss; addresses small original dataset (473 images) by augmentation and robust models; compares two architectures and adds interpretability.
AI.method: Dataset: 473 original guava images from Mendeley Data; resized to 256x256 RGB; augmented to 3784 images. Models: InceptionV3 and ResNet50. Data augmentation via MixUp and CutMix. Evaluation via confusion matrix; interpretability via SHAP. Terminology: transfer learning with CNN backbones; image-level classification.
AI.result: InceptionV3 achieved 98.15% accuracy; ResNet50 achieved 94.46% accuracy. Confusion matrices indicate performance across three classes: Anthracnose, Fruit flies, Healthy. SHAP analysis identifies image regions driving predictions, enhancing interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Advanced CNNs effectively classify guava diseases; InceptionV3 outperforms ResNet50. Data augmentation (MixUp, CutMix) improves robustness; SHAP adds interpretability. Limitations include small original dataset and potential generalization gaps; future work could expand data, explore more architectures, ablation studies on augmentation methods, and real-world deployment considerations.

title: VFGS-Net: Frequency-Guided State-Space Learning for Topology-Preserving Retinal Vessel Segmentation
summary: Accurate retinal vessel segmentation is a critical prerequisite for quantitative analysis of retinal images and computer-aided diagnosis of vascular diseases such as diabetic retinopathy. However, the elongated morphology, wide scale variation, and low contrast of retinal vessels pose significant challenges for existing methods, making it difficult to simultaneously preserve fine capillaries and maintain global topological continuity. To address these challenges, we propose the Vessel-aware Frequency-domain and Global Spatial modeling Network (VFGS-Net), an end-to-end segmentation framework that seamlessly integrates frequency-aware feature enhancement, dual-path convolutional representation learning, and bidirectional asymmetric spatial state-space modeling within a unified architecture. Specifically, VFGS-Net employs a dual-path feature convolution module to jointly capture fine-grained local textures and multi-scale contextual semantics. A novel vessel-aware frequency-domain channel attention mechanism is introduced to adaptively reweight spectral components, thereby enhancing vessel-relevant responses in high-level features. Furthermore, at the network bottleneck, we propose a bidirectional asymmetric Mamba2-based spatial modeling block to efficiently capture long-range spatial dependencies and strengthen the global continuity of vascular structures. Extensive experiments on four publicly available retinal vessel datasets demonstrate that VFGS-Net achieves competitive or superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our model consistently improves segmentation accuracy for fine vessels, complex branching patterns, and low-contrast regions, highlighting its robustness and clinical potential.

AI.tldr: VFGS-Net introduces a specialized retina vessel segmentation network that combines frequency-domain enhancement, dual-path convolution, and bidirectional asymmetric spatial modeling to improve segmentation of fine and low-contrast vessels, achieving competitive-to-superior results on four public datasets.
AI.motivation: Retinal vessels exhibit elongated morphology, wide scale variation, and low contrast, making it hard to preserve thin capillaries and maintain continuous topology with existing methods. A unified framework that jointly enhances spectral features, captures local and multi-scale context, and models long-range spatial dependencies is needed.
AI.method: Propose VFGS-Net with: (1) dual-path feature convolution for fine and multi-scale context; (2) vessel-aware frequency-domain channel attention to emphasize vessel-relevant spectral components; (3) bidirectional asymmetric Mamba2-based spatial modeling block at the bottleneck to capture long-range dependencies and enforce global vessel continuity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four publicly available retinal vessel datasets show competitive or superior performance to state-of-the-art methods, with consistent improvements for fine vessels, complex branching, and low-contrast regions, indicating robustness and clinical potential.
AI.conclusion: VFGS-Net effectively tackles the challenges of retinal vessel segmentation by integrating frequency-domain enhancement, dual-path representation, and global spatial modeling, achieving robust, accurate segmentation with potential clinical impact.

title: Exploring the impact of adaptive rewiring in Graph Neural Networks
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: This paper explores sparsification methods as a form of regularization in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to address high memory usage and computational costs in large-scale graph applications. Using techniques from Network Science and Machine Learning, including Erdős-Rényi for model sparsification, we enhance the efficiency of GNNs for real-world applications. We demonstrate our approach on N-1 contingency assessment in electrical grids, a critical task for ensuring grid reliability. We apply our methods to three datasets of varying sizes, exploring Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) and Graph Isomorphism Networks (GIN) with different degrees of sparsification and rewiring. Comparison across sparsification levels shows the potential of combining insights from both research fields to improve GNN performance and scalability. Our experiments highlight the importance of tuning sparsity parameters: while sparsity can improve generalization, excessive sparsity may hinder learning of complex patterns. Our adaptive rewiring approach, particularly when combined with early stopping, proves promising by allowing the model to adapt its connectivity structure during training. This research contributes to understanding how sparsity can be effectively leveraged in GNNs for critical applications like power grid reliability analysis.

AI.tldr: Sparsification-based regularization for Graph Neural Networks (GCN/GIN) enhances memory efficiency and scalability in large graphs, with adaptive rewiring and early stopping; evaluated on power-grid N-1 contingency data across three datasets, showing sparsity improves generalization up to a point but excessive sparsity harms learning.
AI.motivation: Reduce memory usage and computation cost of GNNs in large-scale graph applications while maintaining performance for critical tasks such as power-grid reliability analysis.
AI.method: Apply sparsification inspired by Network Science (Erdős-Rényi) to GCN and GIN; explore varying sparsity levels and adaptive rewiring; incorporate early stopping; evaluate on three datasets with N-1 contingency assessment in electrical grids.
AI.result: Sparsification can improve generalization up to an optimal sparsity level; excessive sparsity degrades performance; adaptive rewiring with training-time connectivity adjustments yields promising improvements; demonstrates viability of sparsity-based regularization for efficient, scalable GNNs in critical infrastructure tasks.
AI.conclusion: Sparsity is a viable axis to improve GNN efficiency and performance in critical applications; combining Erdős-Rényi sparsification with adaptive rewiring and early stopping can enhance learning and scalability, provided sparsity is carefully tuned.

title: When Gradient Clipping Becomes a Control Mechanism for Differential Privacy in Deep Learning
comment: This manuscript is under review in the Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence journal
summary: Privacy-preserving training on sensitive data commonly relies on differentially private stochastic optimization with gradient clipping and Gaussian noise. The clipping threshold is a critical control knob: if set too small, systematic over-clipping induces optimization bias; if too large, injected noise dominates updates and degrades accuracy. Existing adaptive clipping methods often depend on per-example gradient norm statistics, adding computational overhead and introducing sensitivity to datasets and architectures. We propose a control-driven clipping strategy that adapts the threshold using a lightweight, weight-only spectral diagnostic computed from model parameters. At periodic probe steps, the method analyzes a designated weight matrix via spectral decomposition and estimates a heavy-tailed spectral indicator associated with training stability. This indicator is smoothed over time and fed into a bounded feedback controller that updates the clipping threshold multiplicatively in the log domain. Because the controller uses only parameters produced during privacy-preserving training, the resulting threshold updates are post-processing and do not increase privacy loss beyond that of the underlying DP optimizer under standard composition accounting.

AI.tldr: A DP-SGD clipping method adapts the clipping threshold via a lightweight, weight-driven spectral diagnostic and a bounded controller, enabling post-processing updates that do not add privacy loss.
AI.motivation: Fix the sensitivity of DP training to the clipping threshold, which is hard to set: too small causes bias from over-clipping, too large injects noise; aim to reduce overhead and dataset/architecture sensitivity of adaptive clipping.
AI.method: Compute a heavy-tailed spectral indicator from a designated weight matrix at periodic probe steps using spectral decomposition. Smooth the indicator over time and feed it into a bounded feedback controller that updates the clipping threshold multiplicatively in the log domain. Updates depend only on model parameters produced during training, ensuring post-processing compatibility with DP accounting.
AI.result: Introduces a control-driven clipping strategy that uses weight-space spectral diagnostics to adapt the clipping threshold via a log-domain multiplicative controller; updates are post-processing and do not increase privacy loss beyond the underlying DP optimizer under standard composition.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a principled, privacy-preserving mechanism to adapt clipping thresholds during DP training, potentially improving stability and accuracy with reduced overhead, by leveraging a spectral diagnostic of weights and a feedback control loop.

title: DFIC: Towards a balanced facial image dataset for automatic ICAO compliance verification
summary:
Ensuring compliance with ISO/IEC and ICAO standards for facial images in machine-readable travel documents (MRTDs) is essential for reliable identity verification, but current manual inspection methods are inefficient in high-demand environments. This paper introduces the DFIC dataset, a novel comprehensive facial image dataset comprising around 58,000 annotated images and 2706 videos of more than 1000 subjects, that cover a broad range of non-compliant conditions, in addition to compliant portraits. Our dataset provides a more balanced demographic distribution than the existing public datasets, with one partition that is nearly uniformly distributed, facilitating the development of automated ICAO compliance verification methods.
  Using DFIC, we fine-tuned a novel method that heavily relies on spatial attention mechanisms for the automatic validation of ICAO compliance requirements, and we have compared it with the state-of-the-art aimed at ICAO compliance verification, demonstrating improved results. DFIC dataset is now made public (https://github.com/visteam-isr-uc/DFIC) for the training and validation of new models, offering an unprecedented diversity of faces, that will improve both robustness and adaptability to the intrinsically diverse combinations of faces and props that can be presented to the validation system. These results emphasize the potential of DFIC to enhance automated ICAO compliance methods but it can also be used in many other applications that aim to improve the security, privacy, and fairness of facial recognition systems.

AI.tldr: Introduces the DFIC dataset and a spatial-attention model for automated ICAO/MRTD facial image compliance verification; outperforms prior methods and is publicly released to advance robust, fair facial verification under ICAO standards.
AI.motivation: Manual inspection of ICAO/MRTD facial images is inefficient in high-demand environments; existing datasets lack comprehensive non-compliant conditions and balanced demographics. A large, diverse, and annotated dataset (DFIC) is needed to train robust automated compliance verification systems that generalize across varied faces and props.
AI.method: Fine-tuning a novel spatial-attention-based method to automatically validate ICAO compliance requirements using the DFIC dataset; comparative evaluation against state-of-the-art ICAO compliance verification methods.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves improved performance over state-of-the-art methods for ICAO compliance verification. Public release of the DFIC dataset (≈58k images, 2706 videos, >1000 subjects) with broad demographic balance and non-compliant conditions enables robust training and validation, enhancing robustness and adaptability to diverse facial presentations.
AI.conclusion: DFIC can enhance automated ICAO compliance methods and has broader potential applications in security, privacy, and fairness of facial recognition systems; the public dataset will facilitate future research and improved automated validation of MRTD imagery.

title: LOREN: Low Rank-Based Code-Rate Adaptation in Neural Receivers
comment: Accepted to / To appear IEEE Wireless Communications and Networking Conference Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia 13 - 16 April 2026
summary: Neural network based receivers have recently demonstrated superior system-level performance compared to traditional receivers.
