title: Domain Knowledge Guided Bayesian Optimization For Autonomous Alignment Of Complex Scientific Instruments
summary: Bayesian Optimization (BO) is a powerful tool for optimizing complex non-linear systems. However, its performance degrades in high-dimensional problems with tightly coupled parameters and highly asymmetric objective landscapes, where rewards are sparse. In such needle-in-a-haystack scenarios, even advanced methods like trust-region BO (TurBO) often lead to unsatisfactory results. We propose a domain knowledge guided Bayesian Optimization approach, which leverages physical insight to fundamentally simplify the search problem by transforming coordinates to decouple input features and align the active subspaces with the primary search axes. We demonstrate this approach's efficacy on a challenging 12-dimensional, 6-crystal Split-and-Delay optical system, where conventional approaches, including standard BO, TuRBO and multi-objective BO, consistently led to unsatisfactory results. When combined with an reverse annealing exploration strategy, this approach reliably converges to the global optimum. The coordinate transformation itself is the key to this success, significantly accelerating the search by aligning input co-ordinate axes with the problem's active subspaces. As increasingly complex scientific instruments, from large telescopes to new spectrometers at X-ray Free Electron Lasers are deployed, the demand for robust high-dimensional optimization grows. Our results demonstrate a generalizable paradigm: leveraging physical insight to transform high-dimensional, coupled optimization problems into simpler representations can enable rapid and robust automated tuning for consistent high performance while still retaining current optimization algorithms.

AI.tldr: Domain-knowledge guided Bayesian optimization via a coordinate transformation that decouples inputs and aligns active subspaces with search axes, enabling robust high-dimensional tuning. Demonstrated on a 12D, 6-crystal Split-and-Delay optical system with reverse annealing, outperforming standard BO and TurBO; suggests a generalizable paradigm for complex scientific instruments.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional BO struggles in tightly coupled, asymmetric landscapes with sparse rewards. A physically informed coordinate transform can simplify the search by decoupling features and aligning with active subspaces, potentially transforming needle-in-a-haystack problems into tractable ones.
AI.method: Apply a domain-specific coordinate transformation to decouple input features and align active subspaces with the primary search axes. Integrate this transform with Bayesian optimization and a reverse annealing exploration strategy. Evaluate on a 12-dimensional, 6-crystal Split-and-Delay optical system and compare against standard BO, TuRBO, and multi-objective BO.
AI.result: The coordinate transformation significantly accelerates the search and, when combined with reverse annealing, reliably converges to the global optimum. Conventional approaches fail on this problem.
AI.conclusion: Leverage physical insight to transform high-dimensional, coupled optimization problems into simpler representations. This enables rapid, robust automated tuning for complex instruments while remaining compatible with existing optimization algorithms; the approach is generalizable to other high-dimensional, physics-guided optimization tasks.

title: SurfPhase: 3D Interfacial Dynamics in Two-Phase Flows from Sparse Videos
comment: The first two authors contributed equally. Project website: https://yuegao.me/SurfPhase
summary: Interfacial dynamics in two-phase flows govern momentum, heat, and mass transfer, yet remain difficult to measure experimentally. Classical techniques face intrinsic limitations near moving interfaces, while existing neural rendering methods target single-phase flows with diffuse boundaries and cannot handle sharp, deformable liquid-vapor interfaces. We propose SurfPhase, a novel model for reconstructing 3D interfacial dynamics from sparse camera views. Our approach integrates dynamic Gaussian surfels with a signed distance function formulation for geometric consistency, and leverages a video diffusion model to synthesize novel-view videos to refine reconstruction from sparse observations. We evaluate on a new dataset of high-speed pool boiling videos, demonstrating high-quality view synthesis and velocity estimation from only two camera views. Project website: https://yuegao.me/SurfPhase.

AI.tldr: SurfPhase reconstructs 3D interfacial dynamics from sparse camera views by merging dynamic Gaussian surfels with an SDF and using a diffusion-based synthesis for novel-view refinement, enabling accurate view synthesis and velocity estimation from two cameras on sharp liquid-vapor interfaces.
AI.motivation: Interfacial dynamics in two-phase flows critically affect momentum, heat, and mass transfer but are difficult to measure experimentally; existing neural-rendering methods struggle with sharp, deformable liquid-vapor interfaces and sparse observations.
AI.method: Combine dynamic Gaussian surfels with a signed distance function for geometric consistency; incorporate a video diffusion model to synthesize novel-view videos and refine reconstructions from sparse observations; evaluate on a high-speed pool-boiling dataset with only two camera views.
AI.result: Demonstrates high-quality view synthesis and velocity estimation from two views on high-speed pool boiling; provides a new dataset and a project website for SurfPhase.
AI.conclusion: SurfPhase offers a viable framework for 3D reconstruction of sharp interfacial dynamics from sparse views, outperforming methods tuned for diffuse, single-phase scenes by leveraging explicit surface representations and diffusion-based refinement.

title: SceneSmith: Agentic Generation of Simulation-Ready Indoor Scenes
summary: Simulation has become a key tool for training and evaluating home robots at scale, yet existing environments fail to capture the diversity and physical complexity of real indoor spaces. Current scene synthesis methods produce sparsely furnished rooms that lack the dense clutter, articulated furniture, and physical properties essential for robotic manipulation. We introduce SceneSmith, a hierarchical agentic framework that generates simulation-ready indoor environments from natural language prompts. SceneSmith constructs scenes through successive stages$\unicode{x2013}$from architectural layout to furniture placement to small object population$\unicode{x2013}$each implemented as an interaction among VLM agents: designer, critic, and orchestrator. The framework tightly integrates asset generation through text-to-3D synthesis for static objects, dataset retrieval for articulated objects, and physical property estimation. SceneSmith generates 3-6x more objects than prior methods, with <2% inter-object collisions and 96% of objects remaining stable under physics simulation. In a user study with 205 participants, it achieves 92% average realism and 91% average prompt faithfulness win rates against baselines. We further demonstrate that these environments can be used in an end-to-end pipeline for automatic robot policy evaluation.

AI.tldr: SceneSmith is a hierarchical agentic framework that generates dense, physics-ready indoor environments from natural language prompts, improving object density and realism for home robot simulation and enabling end-to-end robot policy evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing scene synthesis methods yield sparsely furnished rooms lacking clutter, articulated furniture, and physical properties necessary for robotic manipulation, limiting scalable, realistic home-robot simulation.
AI.method: A staged pipeline (layout design, furniture placement, small-object population) managed by interacting VLM agents—designer, critic, and orchestrator. It integrates asset generation through text-to-3D for static objects, dataset retrieval for articulated objects, and physical-property estimation to ensure consistent physics behavior. The result is 3–6× more objects with <2% inter-object collisions and 96% object stability under physics.
AI.result: In a user study with 205 participants, the approach achieved 92% average realism and 91% average prompt faithfulness compared with baselines. The environments support an end-to-end pipeline for automatic robot policy evaluation.
AI.conclusion: SceneSmith delivers scalable, realism-enhanced, physics-grounded indoor environments from natural language, enabling reliable automatic evaluation of robot policies and facilitating broader use in learned or optimized robot control pipelines.

title: ROCKET: Rapid Optimization via Calibration-guided Knapsack Enhanced Truncation for Efficient Model Compression
summary: We present ROCKET, a training-free model compression method that achieves state-of-the-art performance in comparison with factorization, structured-sparsification and dynamic compression baselines. Operating under a global compression budget, ROCKET comprises two key innovations: First, it formulates layer-wise compression allocation as a multi-choice knapsack problem, selecting the optimal compression level for each layer to minimize total reconstruction error while adhering to a target model size. Second, it introduces a single-step sparse matrix factorization inspired by dictionary learning: using only a small calibration set, it sparsifies weight coefficients based on activation-weights sensitivity and then updates the dictionary in closed form via least squares bypassing iterative optimization, sparse coding, or backpropagation entirely. ROCKET consistently outperforms existing compression approaches across different model architectures at 20-50\% compression rates. Notably, it retains over 90\% of the original model's performance at 30\% compression without any fine-tuning. Moreover, when applying a light fine-tuning phase, recovery is substantially enhanced: for instance, compressing Qwen3-14B to an 8B-parameter model and healing it with just 30 million tokens yields performance nearly on par with the original Qwen3-8B. The code for ROCKET is at github.com/mts-ai/ROCKET/tree/main.

AI.tldr: ROCKET is a training-free model compression method combining (i) per-layer compression allocation via a multi-choice knapsack under a global budget, and (ii) a one-shot sparse matrix factorization inspired by dictionary learning, with closed-form dictionary updates and no backpropagation. It achieves state-of-the-art results at 20-50% compression, preserves >90% performance at 30% compression without fine-tuning, and gains additional gains with light fine-tuning; code available.
AI.motivation: To enable high-quality neural network compression without retraining, providing strong performance under a global compression budget using training-free techniques suitable for limited data and compute. It addresses limitations of iterative optimization, backpropagation, and extensive fine-tuning in existing methods.
AI.method: 1) Layer-wise compression levels are chosen by solving a multi-choice knapsack problem to minimize reconstruction error under a target model size. 2) A single-step sparse matrix factorization inspired by dictionary learning uses a small calibration set to sparsify weight coefficients based on activation-weights sensitivity, then updates the dictionary in closed form via least squares, bypassing iterative optimization, sparse coding, or backpropagation.
AI.result: ROCKET outperforms existing compression baselines (factorization, structured sparsification, dynamic compression) across architectures at 20-50% compression. It preserves over 90% of the original performance at 30% compression without any fine-tuning. With light fine-tuning (e.g., compress Qwen3-14B to 8B and fine-tune with ~30M tokens), performance approaches the original 8B model. Code is available at github.com/mts-ai/ROCKET/tree/main.
AI.conclusion: ROCKET provides a training-free, efficient, and competitive model compression solution that attains state-of-the-art performance under a global budget, with minimal calibration and optional light fine-tuning, suitable for practical deployment across diverse architectures.

title: Reducing Estimation Uncertainty Using Normalizing Flows and Stratification
comment: This is the extended version of a paper accepted for publication at ACIIDS 2026
summary: Estimating the expectation of a real-valued function of a random variable from sample data is a critical aspect of statistical analysis, with far-reaching implications in various applications. Current methodologies typically assume (semi-)parametric distributions such as Gaussian or mixed Gaussian, leading to significant estimation uncertainty if these assumptions do not hold. We propose a flow-based model, integrated with stratified sampling, that leverages a parametrized neural network to offer greater flexibility in modeling unknown data distributions, thereby mitigating this limitation. Our model shows a marked reduction in estimation uncertainty across multiple datasets, including high-dimensional (30 and 128) ones, outperforming crude Monte Carlo estimators and Gaussian mixture models. Reproducible code is available at https://github.com/rnoxy/flowstrat.

AI.tldr: Flow-based model with stratified sampling to estimate E[f(X)] under unknown distributions, reducing uncertainty and outperforming MC and Gaussian mixtures, including high-dimensional data.
AI.motivation: Standard estimators rely on (semi-)parametric assumptions (e.g., Gaussian) which can lead to large estimation uncertainty when these assumptions are violated; flexible distribution modeling via neural flows can mitigate this.
AI.method: Employs a parametrized neural network within a normalizing-flow framework, integrated with stratified sampling to estimate the expectation of a real-valued function of a random variable.
AI.result: Across multiple datasets, including high-dimensional cases (dimensions 30 and 128), the approach reduces estimation uncertainty and outperforms crude Monte Carlo and Gaussian mixtures.
AI.conclusion: Flow-based stratified estimation provides robust, scalable, and more accurate computation of E[f(X)] under unknown distributions; code is available at the cited GitHub repository.

title: OSIL: Learning Offline Safe Imitation Policies with Safety Inferred from Non-preferred Trajectories
summary: This work addresses the problem of offline safe imitation learning (IL), where the goal is to learn safe and reward-maximizing policies from demonstrations that do not have per-timestep safety cost or reward information. In many real-world domains, online learning in the environment can be risky, and specifying accurate safety costs can be difficult. However, it is often feasible to collect trajectories that reflect undesirable or unsafe behavior, implicitly conveying what the agent should avoid. We refer to these as non-preferred trajectories. We propose a novel offline safe IL algorithm, OSIL, that infers safety from non-preferred demonstrations. We formulate safe policy learning as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP). Instead of relying on explicit safety cost and reward annotations, OSIL reformulates the CMDP problem by deriving a lower bound on reward maximizing objective and learning a cost model that estimates the likelihood of non-preferred behavior. Our approach allows agents to learn safe and reward-maximizing behavior entirely from offline demonstrations. We empirically demonstrate that our approach can learn safer policies that satisfy cost constraints without degrading the reward performance, thus outperforming several baselines.

AI.tldr: OSIL enables offline safe imitation learning by inferring safety from non-preferred trajectories; learns policies that maximize reward subject to cost constraints in a CMDP, all from offline data.
AI.motivation: In many real-world domains, online exploration is risky and explicit safety costs are hard to annotate; non-preferred trajectories provide implicit safety signals; need to learn safe strategies offline.
AI.method: OSIL derives a lower bound on the reward objective and trains a cost model that estimates likelihood of non-preferred behavior; reformulates the CMDP to enforce safety without explicit costs, relying solely on offline demonstrations.
AI.result: Empirically, OSIL achieves safer policies under cost constraints without degrading reward performance and outperforms several baselines.
AI.conclusion: Offline safe imitation learning is feasible by leveraging non-preferred demonstrations to infer safety signals, enabling safe and rewarding policies without per-timestep costs or rewards.

title: SnapMLA: Efficient Long-Context MLA Decoding via Hardware-Aware FP8 Quantized Pipelining
summary: While FP8 attention has shown substantial promise in innovations like FlashAttention-3, its integration into the decoding phase of the DeepSeek Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) architecture presents notable challenges. These challenges include numerical heterogeneity arising from the decoupling of positional embeddings, misalignment of quantization scales in FP8 PV GEMM, and the need for optimized system-level support. In this paper, we introduce SnapMLA, an FP8 MLA decoding framework optimized to improve long-context efficiency through the following hardware-aware algorithm-kernel co-optimization techniques: (i) RoPE-Aware Per-Token KV Quantization, where the RoPE part is maintained in high precision, motivated by our comprehensive analysis of the heterogeneous quantization sensitivity inherent to the MLA KV cache. Furthermore, per-token granularity is employed to align with the autoregressive decoding process and maintain quantization accuracy. (ii) Quantized PV Computation Pipeline Reconstruction, which resolves the misalignment of quantization scale in FP8 PV computation stemming from the shared KV structure of the MLA KV cache. (iii) End-to-End Dataflow Optimization, where we establish an efficient data read-and-write workflow using specialized kernels, ensuring efficient data flow and performance gains. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art MLA LLMs show that SnapMLA achieves up to a 1.91x improvement in throughput, with negligible risk of performance degradation in challenging long-context tasks, including mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/meituan-longcat/SGLang-FluentLLM.

AI.tldr: SnapMLA introduces an FP8-based MLA decoding framework with hardware-aware optimizations to improve long-context throughput in decoder for multi-head latent attention on LLMs, achieving up to 1.91x throughput with robust performance in long-context tasks.
AI.motivation: Address numerical heterogeneity and FP8 PV GEMM scale misalignment when integrating FP8 attention into MLA decoding, and improve throughput for long-context, autoregressive decoding in modern LLMs.
AI.method: Per-token RoPE-aware KV quantization (keep RoPE in high precision; per-token granularity), PV computation pipeline reconstruction to fix quantization scale misalignment due to shared KV in MLA KV cache, and end-to-end dataflow optimization with specialized kernels.
AI.result: Experimental results on state-of-the-art MLA LLMs show up to 1.91x throughput improvement; negligible performance degradation on long-context tasks including math reasoning and code generation benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Hardware-aware FP8 MLA decoding with quantization-aware kernel/dataflow optimizations yields substantial throughput gains while preserving accuracy; code is available at the linked GitHub repository.

title: Rising Multi-Armed Bandits with Known Horizons
summary: The Rising Multi-Armed Bandit (RMAB) framework models environments where expected rewards of arms increase with plays, which models practical scenarios where performance of each option improves with the repeated usage, such as in robotics and hyperparameter tuning. For instance, in hyperparameter tuning, the validation accuracy of a model configuration (arm) typically increases with each training epoch. A defining characteristic of RMAB is em horizon-dependent optimality: unlike standard settings, the optimal strategy here shifts dramatically depending on the available budget $T$. This implies that knowledge of $T$ yields significantly greater utility in RMAB, empowering the learner to align its decision-making with this shifting optimality. However, the horizon-aware setting remains underexplored. To address this, we propose a novel CUmulative Reward Estimation UCB (CURE-UCB) that explicitly integrates the horizon. We provide a rigorous analysis establishing a new regret upper bound and prove that our method strictly outperforms horizon-agnostic strategies in structured environments like ``linear-then-flat'' instances. Extensive experiments demonstrate its significant superiority over baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes CURE-UCB for horizon-aware RMAB where arm rewards accumulate with plays; establishes a regret bound and shows superiority over horizon-agnostic baselines, with experiments on structured 'linear-then-flat' environments indicating practical gains.
AI.motivation: RMABs with horizon dependence exhibit shifting optimal strategies; knowledge of the horizon T is crucial to align decisions with the changing optimality and to improve cumulative reward, particularly in applications like hyperparameter tuning.
AI.method: Introduce CUmulative Reward Estimation UCB (CURE-UCB) that explicitly incorporates horizon information into the UCB framework. Provides a theoretical regret upper bound and analyzes performance in structured environments (e.g., linear-then-flat). Empirically compares against horizon-agnostic baselines and demonstrates performance gains.
AI.result: New regret upper bound for horizon-aware RMAB under CURE-UCB; empirical and theoretical results show that CURE-UCB outperforms horizon-agnostic strategies in linear-then-flat instances and other structured settings.
AI.conclusion: Horizon-aware design yields tangible benefits in RMAB tasks. CURE-UCB effectively leverages horizon information to achieve superior regret performance, with strong empirical validation; suggests broader applicability in settings where arm quality improves with usage.

title: From Circuits to Dynamics: Understanding and Stabilizing Failure in 3D Diffusion Transformers
summary: Reliable surface completion from sparse point clouds underpins many applications spanning content creation and robotics. While 3D diffusion transformers attain state-of-the-art results on this task, we uncover that they exhibit a catastrophic mode of failure: arbitrarily small on-surface perturbations to the input point cloud can fracture the output into multiple disconnected pieces -- a phenomenon we call Meltdown. Using activation-patching from mechanistic interpretability, we localize Meltdown to a single early denoising cross-attention activation. We find that the singular-value spectrum of this activation provides a scalar proxy: its spectral entropy rises when fragmentation occurs and returns to baseline when patched. Interpreted through diffusion dynamics, we show that this proxy tracks a symmetry-breaking bifurcation of the reverse process. Guided by this insight, we introduce PowerRemap, a test-time control that stabilizes sparse point-cloud conditioning. We demonstrate that Meltdown persists across state-of-the-art architectures (WaLa, Make-a-Shape), datasets (GSO, SimJEB) and denoising strategies (DDPM, DDIM), and that PowerRemap effectively counters this failure with stabilization rates of up to 98.3%. Overall, this work is a case study on how diffusion model behavior can be understood and guided based on mechanistic analysis, linking a circuit-level cross-attention mechanism to diffusion-dynamics accounts of trajectory bifurcations.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based 3D point-cloud completion models can catastrophically fragment outputs with tiny on-surface input perturbations (Meltdown). A single early cross-attention activation's singular-value spectrum acts as a robust indicator (spectral entropy rises during fragmentation and normalizes after patching). Interpreting the diffusion process as a symmetry-breaking bifurcation explains the phenomenon. A test-time control, PowerRemap, stabilizes conditioning and mitigates Meltdown across architectures and datasets, achieving up to 98.3% stabilization. The work links circuit-level attention mechanics to diffusion dynamics, contributing to mechanistic interpretability and robust generation.
AI.motivation: Address the fragility of state-of-the-art 3D diffusion transformers for sparse point-cloud completion, where small input perturbations can cause complete fragmentation. Provide mechanistic understanding via interpretability tools and practical stabilization.
AI.method: Use activation-patching to localize Meltdown to an early denoising cross-attention activation. Analyze the singular-value spectrum of this activation as a proxy (spectral entropy). Interpret diffusion dynamics to relate the proxy to a symmetry-breaking bifurcation in the reverse process. Propose PowerRemap as a test-time control to stabilize conditioning. Evaluate across architectures (WaLa, Make-a-Shape), datasets (GSO, SimJEB), and denoising strategies (DDPM, DDIM).
AI.result: Meltdown is a robust, cross-architecture phenomenon triggered by tiny input perturbations, indicating output fragmentation into multiple disconnected pieces. The spectral entropy of the early cross-attention activation correlates with fragmentation and returns to baseline when patched. PowerRemap at test-time effectively counteracts Meltdown, achieving stabilization rates up to 98.3%. These findings generalize across models, data, and denoising schemes.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that diffusion-model failures can be understood and mitigated through mechanistic interpretability, connecting a circuit-level cross-attention feature to diffusion-dynamics trajectory bifurcations. PowerRemap offers a practical path to robust sparse point-cloud completion, advancing reliability and interpretability in diffusion-based 3D generation.

title: GENIUS: Generative Fluid Intelligence Evaluation Suite
summary: Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) have shown remarkable progress in visual generation. Yet, existing benchmarks predominantly assess $\textit{Crystallized Intelligence}$, which relies on recalling accumulated knowledge and learned schemas. This focus overlooks $\textit{Generative Fluid Intelligence (GFI)}$: the capacity to induce patterns, reason through constraints, and adapt to novel scenarios on the fly. To rigorously assess this capability, we introduce $\textbf{GENIUS}$ ($\textbf{GEN}$ Fluid $\textbf{I}$ntelligence Eval$\textbf{U}$ation $\textbf{S}$uite). We formalize $\textit{GFI}$ as a synthesis of three primitives. These include $\textit{Inducing Implicit Patterns}$ (e.g., inferring personalized visual preferences), $\textit{Executing Ad-hoc Constraints}$ (e.g., visualizing abstract metaphors), and $\textit{Adapting to Contextual Knowledge}$ (e.g., simulating counter-intuitive physics). Collectively, these primitives challenge models to solve problems grounded entirely in the immediate context. Our systematic evaluation of 12 representative models reveals significant performance deficits in these tasks. Crucially, our diagnostic analysis disentangles these failure modes. It demonstrates that deficits stem from limited context comprehension rather than insufficient intrinsic generative capability. To bridge this gap, we propose a training-free attention intervention strategy. Ultimately, $\textbf{GENIUS}$ establishes a rigorous standard for $\textit{GFI}$, guiding the field beyond knowledge utilization toward dynamic, general-purpose reasoning. Our dataset and code will be released at: $\href{https://github.com/arctanxarc/GENIUS}{https://github.com/arctanxarc/GENIUS}$.

AI.tldr: GENIUS is a benchmark suite to evaluate Generative Fluid Intelligence in multimodal models, highlighting context-understanding bottlenecks and proposing a training-free attention intervention; dataset and code to be released.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks emphasize crystallized intelligence (recalling knowledge) and overlook the ability to induce patterns, reason under constraints, and adapt to novel contexts (GFI). GENIUS formalizes GFI and provides a rigorous evaluation framework.
AI.method: Define GFI as three primitives: Inducing Implicit Patterns, Executing Ad-hoc Constraints, Adapting to Contextual Knowledge. Create tasks grounded in the immediate context to test these primitives. Systematically evaluate 12 representative Unified Multimodal Models, complemented by diagnostic analyses to identify failure modes. Propose a training-free attention intervention to bridge gaps. Release dataset and code.
AI.result: Models exhibit significant deficits in GFI tasks. Diagnostic analyses reveal that failures stem from limited context comprehension rather than intrinsic generative capacity. A training-free attention intervention is proposed to mitigate these gaps.
AI.conclusion: GENIUS establishes a rigorous standard for Generative Fluid Intelligence, guiding the field toward dynamic, context-aware reasoning in multimodal generation and encouraging development beyond static knowledge utilization. Dataset and code will be released.

title: In-the-Wild Model Organisms: Mitigating Undesirable Emergent Behaviors in Production LLM Post-Training via Data Attribution
summary: We propose activation-based data attribution, a method that traces behavioral changes in post-trained language models to responsible training datapoints. By computing activation-difference vectors for both test prompts and preference pairs and ranking by cosine similarity, we identify datapoints that cause specific behaviors and validate these attributions causally by retraining with modified data. Clustering behavior-datapoint similarity matrices also enables unsupervised discovery of emergent behaviors. Applying this to OLMo 2's production DPO training, we surfaced distractor-triggered compliance: a harmful behavior where the model complies with dangerous requests when benign formatting instructions are appended. Filtering top-ranked datapoints reduces this behavior by 63% while switching their labels achieves 78%. Our method outperforms gradient-based attribution and LLM-judge baselines while being over 10 times cheaper than both. This in-the-wild model organism - emerging from contaminated preference data rather than deliberate injection - provides a realistic benchmark for safety techniques.

AI.tldr: Activation-based data attribution traces post-trained language-model behaviors to responsible training datapoints via activation-difference vectors and cosine similarity; validated causally by retraining with modified data; enables unsupervised discovery of emergent behaviors; achieves notable safety improvements with favorable efficiency.
AI.motivation: Addressing how contaminated preference data and post-training updates shape model behaviors, and providing scalable, causal attribution to training datapoints for safety.
AI.method: Compute activation-difference vectors for test prompts and preference pairs, rank training datapoints by cosine similarity to observed behaviors, identify and modify or relabel datapoints, retrain to validate causality; cluster behavior-datapoint similarity matrices to reveal emergent, unsupervised patterns.
AI.result: Top-ranked datapoints filtered or relabeled reduces distractor-triggered compliance by 63% and 78%, respectively; method outperforms gradient-based attribution and LLM-judge baselines; costs >10× cheaper than both baselines.
AI.conclusion: Activation-based data attribution offers a scalable, causal, and cost-efficient approach for post-training behavior debugging and safety assessment, with real-world applicability demonstrated on a production model and contaminated preference data.

title: Predicting integers from continuous parameters
summary: We study the problem of predicting numeric labels that are constrained to the integers or to a subrange of the integers. For example, the number of up-votes on social media posts, or the number of bicycles available at a public rental station. While it is possible to model these as continuous values, and to apply traditional regression, this approach changes the underlying distribution on the labels from discrete to continuous. Discrete distributions have certain benefits, which leads us to the question whether such integer labels can be modeled directly by a discrete distribution, whose parameters are predicted from the features of a given instance. Moreover, we focus on the use case of output distributions of neural networks, which adds the requirement that the parameters of the distribution be continuous so that backpropagation and gradient descent may be used to learn the weights of the network. We investigate several options for such distributions, some existing and some novel, and test them on a range of tasks, including tabular learning, sequential prediction and image generation. We find that overall the best performance comes from two distributions: Bitwise, which represents the target integer in bits and places a Bernoulli distribution on each, and a discrete analogue of the Laplace distribution, which uses a distribution with exponentially decaying tails around a continuous mean.

AI.tldr: Discrete-output modeling for integer targets; Bitwise Bernoulli-per-bit and discrete Laplace distributions outperform standard regression across tasks.
AI.motivation: Maintain discreteness of integer labels and enable end-to-end gradient-based learning in neural nets without smearing the target distribution into continuous space.
AI.method: Propose two discrete distributions parameterized by neural nets: (i) Bitwise representation of the integer with independent Bernoulli parameters for each bit; (ii) A discrete Laplace distribution with a continuous mean parameter and exponentially decaying tails; train via backpropagation; evaluate on tabular, sequential, and image tasks.
AI.result: Across tasks, Bitwise and discrete Laplace yield the best performance, outperforming continuous regression methods that ignore discreteness.
AI.conclusion: Discrete distributions are a promising approach for integer-valued prediction; Bitwise and discrete Laplace offer strong, broadly applicable options; future work could explore additional discrete families, calibration, efficiency, and broader domains.

title: GRASP: group-Shapley feature selection for patients
summary: Feature selection remains a major challenge in medical prediction, where existing approaches such as LASSO often lack robustness and interpretability. We introduce GRASP, a novel framework that couples Shapley value driven attribution with group $L_{21}$ regularization to extract compact and non-redundant feature sets. GRASP first distills group level importance scores from a pretrained tree model via SHAP, then enforces structured sparsity through group $L_{21}$ regularized logistic regression, yielding stable and interpretable selections. Extensive comparisons with LASSO, SHAP, and deep learning based methods show that GRASP consistently delivers comparable or superior predictive accuracy, while identifying fewer, less redundant, and more stable features.

AI.tldr: GRASP combines SHAP-based attribution from a pretrained tree model with group L21-regularized logistic regression to select compact, non-redundant features for medical prediction, achieving stable interpretability with competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Feature selection in medical prediction requires robustness and interpretability. LASSO-like methods may be brittle and struggle with redundancy. Integrating Shapley-value attribution with structured sparsity aims to produce stable, interpretable, and compact feature sets.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (1) derive group-level importance scores from a pretrained tree model using SHAP to distill Shapley-value driven attributions; (2) enforce structured sparsity via group L21-regularized logistic regression to obtain compact, non-redundant feature subsets.
AI.result: GRASP achieves predictive accuracy that is comparable to or superior to baselines (LASSO, SHAP, and deep learning methods) while selecting fewer features, reducing redundancy, and yielding more stable feature sets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GRASP framework successfully couples Shapley-based attribution with group-wise sparsity to deliver robust, interpretable, and compact feature selections for medical prediction without sacrificing accuracy.

title: General Flexible $f$-divergence for Challenging Offline RL Datasets with Low Stochasticity and Diverse Behavior Policies
comment: Extended version of the full paper with the appendix accepted at AAMAS 2026
summary: Offline RL algorithms aim to improve upon the behavior policy that produces the collected data while constraining the learned policy to be within the support of the dataset. However, practical offline datasets often contain examples with little diversity or limited exploration of the environment, and from multiple behavior policies with diverse expertise levels. Limited exploration can impair the offline RL algorithm's ability to estimate \textit{Q} or \textit{V} values, while constraining towards diverse behavior policies can be overly conservative. Such datasets call for a balance between the RL objective and behavior policy constraints. We first identify the connection between $f$-divergence and optimization constraint on the Bellman residual through a more general Linear Programming form for RL and the convex conjugate. Following this, we introduce the general flexible function formulation for the $f$-divergence to incorporate an adaptive constraint on algorithms' learning objectives based on the offline training dataset. Results from experiments on the MuJoCo, Fetch, and AdroitHand environments show the correctness of the proposed LP form and the potential of the flexible $f$-divergence in improving performance for learning from a challenging dataset when applied to a compatible constrained optimization algorithm.

AI.tldr: General LP formulation links f-divergence constraints to Bellman residual in offline RL; introduces adaptive, flexible f-divergence constraints to balance data-divergence with learning; empirical results on MuJoCo, Fetch, AdroitHand show potential improvements.
AI.motivation: Offline RL datasets often have limited diversity and multiple behavior policies, making it hard to estimate Q/V accurately while respecting dataset support. A balance between RL objectives and behavior constraint is needed.
AI.method: Derive a linear programming (LP) formulation of RL and use the convex conjugate to connect f-divergence-based constraints to the Bellman residual. Propose a flexible, adaptive f-divergence function to modulate the constraint based on dataset characteristics and integrate this into a constrained optimization algorithm.
AI.result: The LP formulation is validated as correct, and the flexible f-divergence approach shows potential to improve learning from challenging offline datasets when used with a compatible constrained optimizer; experiments on MuJoCo, Fetch, and AdroitHand support feasibility.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, flexible f-divergence constrained optimization is a promising direction for offline RL with diverse but limited datasets, enabling a tunable balance between leveraging dataset support and pursuing policy improvement; further work needed on deeper theoretical analysis and broader empirical validation.

title: Collaborative Threshold Watermarking
summary: In federated learning (FL), $K$ clients jointly train a model without sharing raw data. Because each participant invests data and compute, clients need mechanisms to later prove the provenance of a jointly trained model. Model watermarking embeds a hidden signal in the weights, but naive approaches either do not scale with many clients as per-client watermarks dilute as $K$ grows, or give any individual client the ability to verify and potentially remove the watermark. We introduce $(t,K)$-threshold watermarking: clients collaboratively embed a shared watermark during training, while only coalitions of at least $t$ clients can reconstruct the watermark key and verify a suspect model. We secret-share the watermark key $τ$ so that coalitions of fewer than $t$ clients cannot reconstruct it, and verification can be performed without revealing $τ$ in the clear. We instantiate our protocol in the white-box setting and evaluate on image classification. Our watermark remains detectable at scale ($K=128$) with minimal accuracy loss and stays above the detection threshold ($z\ge 4$) under attacks including adaptive fine-tuning using up to 20% of the training data.

AI.tldr: Proposes a (t,K)-threshold watermarking scheme for federated learning where the watermark key τ is secret-shared among clients and only coalitions of at least t clients can reconstruct and verify the watermark, enabling scalable, robust model provenance verification without revealing τ.
AI.motivation: Existing model watermarking in FL suffers from scalability (per-client watermarks dilute with K) and single-client verification/removal risks. A threshold, collaborative watermark addresses scalability and trust by enabling group-based verification while preserving privacy.
AI.method: Embed a watermark via a secret-shared key τ among clients using threshold secret sharing; coalitions of size ≥ t can reconstruct τ to verify a suspect model, while coalitions < t learn nothing about τ. Verification is done in a white-box setting without revealing τ. Evaluation is conducted on image classification tasks, scaling to K=128 clients with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.result: Watermark remains detectable at scale (K=128) with minimal accuracy loss; detection threshold maintained (z≥4) even under adaptive fine-tuning using up to 20% of training data.
AI.conclusion: The (t,K)-threshold watermarking framework provides scalable, robust model provenance in federated learning by distributing the watermark key across clients and enforcing coalition-based verification, mitigating risks of dilution and individual client manipulation while preserving model utility.

title: Direct Learning of Calibration-Aware Uncertainty for Neural PDE Surrogates
summary: Neural PDE surrogates are often deployed in data-limited or partially observed regimes where downstream decisions depend on calibrated uncertainty in addition to low prediction error. Existing approaches obtain uncertainty through ensemble replication, fixed stochastic noise such as dropout, or post hoc calibration. Cross-regularized uncertainty learns uncertainty parameters during training using gradients routed through a held-out regularization split. The predictor is optimized on the training split for fit, while low-dimensional uncertainty controls are optimized on the regularization split to reduce train-test mismatch, yielding regime-adaptive uncertainty without per-regime noise tuning. The framework can learn continuous noise levels at the output head, within hidden features, or within operator-specific components such as spectral modes. We instantiate the approach in Fourier Neural Operators and evaluate on APEBench sweeps over observed fraction and training-set size. Across these sweeps, the learned predictive distributions are better calibrated on held-out splits and the resulting uncertainty fields concentrate in high-error regions in one-step spatial diagnostics.

AI.tldr: Cross-regularized uncertainty learning for neural PDE surrogates enabling regime-adaptive uncertainty without per-regime tuning, applied to Fourier Neural Operators and calibrated on APEBench.
AI.motivation: In data-limited or partially observed PDE settings, calibrated uncertainty is crucial for downstream decisions; traditional uncertainty methods (ensembles, dropout, post hoc calibration) either are inefficient or fail to adapt across regimes.
AI.method: Introduce cross-regularized uncertainty: optimization splits into training (predictor fit) and held-out regularization split (uncertainty parameters). Gradients flow through regularization split to adjust low-dimensional uncertainty controls, allowing continuous noise levels at output, hidden features, or operator components; implemented in Fourier Neural Operators; evaluated on APEBench across observed fraction and training-set size.
AI.result: Predictive distributions are better calibrated on held-out splits; uncertainty fields focus in high-error regions in one-step spatial diagnostics.
AI.conclusion: Cross-regularized uncertainty enables regime-adaptive, calibrated uncertainty without per-regime noise tuning, offering efficient, flexible uncertainty modeling for neural PDE surrogates, validated on FNOs with APEBench.

title: Weight Decay Improves Language Model Plasticity
summary: The prevailing paradigm in large language model (LLM) development is to pretrain a base model, then perform further training to improve performance and model behavior. However, hyperparameter optimization and scaling laws have been studied primarily from the perspective of the base model's validation loss, ignoring downstream adaptability. In this work, we study pretraining from the perspective of model plasticity, that is, the ability of the base model to successfully adapt to downstream tasks through fine-tuning. We focus on the role of weight decay, a key regularization parameter during pretraining. Through systematic experiments, we show that models trained with larger weight decay values are more plastic, meaning they show larger performance gains when fine-tuned on downstream tasks. This phenomenon can lead to counterintuitive trade-offs where base models that perform worse after pretraining can perform better after fine-tuning. Further investigation of weight decay's mechanistic effects on model behavior reveals that it encourages linearly separable representations, regularizes attention matrices, and reduces overfitting on the training data. In conclusion, this work demonstrates the importance of using evaluation metrics beyond cross-entropy loss for hyperparameter optimization and casts light on the multifaceted role of that a single optimization hyperparameter plays in shaping model behavior.

AI.tldr: Pretraining with larger weight decay increases base-model plasticity, leading to larger gains on downstream fine-tuning, even if pretraining loss is worse; weight decay promotes linearly separable representations, regularizes attention, and reduces overfitting, highlighting the need for transfer-aware hyperparameter optimization.
AI.motivation: Current hyperparameter optimization emphasizes base-model validation loss, neglecting downstream adaptability (plasticity). Weight decay is a critical knob whose impact on transfer performance warrants investigation.
AI.method: Systematic experiments varying weight decay during pretraining, then evaluating downstream fine-tuning gains on tasks; analysis of representations (linearity), attention matrices, and overfitting to understand mechanistic effects.
AI.result: Models pretrained with larger weight decay exhibit greater plasticity, achieving larger performance gains after fine-tuning on downstream tasks. This can create counterintuitive trade-offs where a base-model that performs worse after pretraining can outperform others after fine-tuning. Mechanistically, weight decay encourages more linearly separable representations, regularizes attention, and reduces overfitting.
AI.conclusion: Hyperparameter optimization should incorporate metrics beyond cross-entropy loss of the base model. Weight decay plays a multifaceted role in shaping downstream adaptability, influencing transfer performance and the structure of learned representations and attention.

title: RePO: Bridging On-Policy Learning and Off-Policy Knowledge through Rephrasing Policy Optimization
summary: Aligning large language models (LLMs) on domain-specific data remains a fundamental challenge. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) offers a straightforward way to inject domain knowledge but often degrades the model's generality. In contrast, on-policy reinforcement learning (RL) preserves generality but fails to effectively assimilate hard samples that exceed the model's current reasoning level. Recent off-policy RL attempts improve hard sample utilization, yet they suffer from severe training instability due to the forced distribution shift toward off-policy knowledge. To reconcile effective off-policy knowledge absorption with the stability of on-policy RL, we propose Rephrasing Policy Optimization (RePO). In RePO, the policy model is prompted to first comprehend off-policy knowledge and then rephrase it into trajectories that conform to its own stylistic and parametric distribution. RePO dynamically replaces low-reward rollouts with these rephrased, high-quality trajectories. This strategy guides the model toward correct reasoning paths while strictly preserving on-policy training dynamics. Experiments on several benchmarks demonstrate that RePO improves hard-sample utilization and outperforms existing baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: Introduces RePO, a rephrasing-based off-policy-to-on-policy policy optimization to improve hard-sample learning while preserving on-policy stability, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Domain-specific LLM alignment is challenging: SFT harms generality; on-policy RL preserves generality but struggles with hard samples; off-policy RL helps hard samples but causes instability due to distribution shift.
AI.method: Prompt the policy to understand off-policy knowledge and rephrase it into trajectories that fit its own distribution; dynamically replace low-reward rollouts with rephrased high-quality trajectories; preserves on-policy dynamics.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple benchmarks show improved hard-sample utilization and better performance than baselines, achieving state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: RePO offers a stable mechanism to leverage off-policy knowledge without breaking on-policy training, boosting hard-sample performance and overall results.

title: Adaptive Sampling for Private Worst-Case Group Optimization
summary: Models trained by minimizing the average loss often fail to be accurate on small or hard-to-learn groups of the data. Various methods address this issue by optimizing a weighted objective that focuses on the worst-performing groups. However, this approach becomes problematic when learning with differential privacy, as unequal data weighting can result in inhomogeneous privacy guarantees, in particular weaker privacy for minority groups. In this work, we introduce a new algorithm for differentially private worst-case group optimization called ASC (Adaptively Sampled and Clipped Worst-case Group Optimization). It adaptively controls both the sampling rate and the clipping threshold of each group. Thereby, it allows for harder-to-learn groups to be sampled more often while ensuring consistent privacy guarantees across all groups. Comparing ASC to prior work, we show that it results in lower-variance gradients, tighter privacy guarantees, and substantially higher worst-case group accuracy without sacrificing overall average accuracy.

AI.tldr: ASC adaptively samples and clips group-wise losses to optimize the worst-case group under differential privacy, achieving higher worst-case accuracy with tighter privacy and no loss in average accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address the gap where standard loss minimization fails on small or hard groups and prior weighted worst-case objectives create inhomogeneous privacy guarantees in DP, especially harming minority groups.
AI.method: ASC assigns per-group sampling rates and clipping thresholds that adapt during training, enabling harder-to-learn groups to contribute more to updates while maintaining consistent DP guarantees across all groups.
AI.result: Lower gradient variance, tighter DP guarantees, substantially higher worst-case group accuracy, and preserved overall average accuracy compared to prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive sampling and clipping per group provide a robust DP framework for worst-case group optimization, improving minority-group performance without compromising overall performance.

title: SimuScene: Training and Benchmarking Code Generation to Simulate Physical Scenarios
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have been extensively studied for tasks like math competitions, complex coding, and scientific reasoning, yet their ability to accurately represent and simulate physical scenarios via code remains underexplored. We propose SimuScene, the first systematic study that trains and evaluates LLMs on simulating physical scenarios across five physics domains and 52 physical concepts. We build an automatic pipeline to collect data, with human verification to ensure quality. The final dataset contains 7,659 physical scenarios with 334 human-verified examples as the test set. We evaluated 10 contemporary LLMs and found that even the strongest model achieves only a 21.5% pass rate, demonstrating the difficulty of the task. Finally, we introduce a reinforcement learning pipeline with visual rewards that uses a vision-language model as a judge to train textual models. Experiments show that training with our data improves physical simulation via code while substantially enhancing general code generation performance.

AI.tldr: SimuScene studies LLMs for simulating physical scenarios via code across five physics domains and 52 concepts; builds a 7,659-scenario dataset with 334 verified test examples; evaluates 10 LLMs with a best pass rate of 21.5%; introduces a reinforcement learning pipeline using visual rewards and a vision-language judge to train text models, improving physical-simulation code and general code generation.
AI.motivation: Addresses the underexplored area of using large language models to explicitly represent and simulate physical systems via executable code, complementing existing math, coding, and reasoning benchmarks by focusing on physical realism and cross-domain generalization.
AI.method: Constructs an automatic data-collection pipeline with human verification to assemble a large, multi-domain physics dataset (5 domains, 52 concepts, 7,659 scenarios; 334 verified test instances). Evaluates 10 contemporary LLMs on physical-simulation tasks. Develops a reinforcement learning setup that uses visual rewards and a vision–language model as an external judge to align textual models with physical-simulation objectives.
AI.result: Even the strongest model achieves only a 21.5% pass rate on the test set, highlighting the difficulty of the task. The RL pipeline with visual rewards improves physical simulation via code and also enhances general code generation performance.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a challenging benchmark for LLM-based physical simulation via code, provides a high-quality dataset with verification, and demonstrates that guided RL with multimodal feedback can yield improvements in both targeted physical-simulation capabilities and broader coding performance.

title: Automated Model Design using Gated Neuron Selection in Telecom
summary: The telecommunications industry is experiencing rapid growth in adopting deep learning for critical tasks such as traffic prediction, signal strength prediction, and quality of service optimisation. However, designing neural network architectures for these applications remains challenging and time-consuming, particularly when targeting compact models suitable for resource-constrained network environments. Therefore, there is a need for automating the model design process to create high-performing models efficiently. This paper introduces TabGNS (Tabular Gated Neuron Selection), a novel gradient-based Neural Architecture Search (NAS) method specifically tailored for tabular data in telecommunications networks. We evaluate TabGNS across multiple telecommunications and generic tabular datasets, demonstrating improvements in prediction performance while reducing the architecture size by 51-82% and reducing the search time by up to 36x compared to state-of-the-art tabular NAS methods. Integrating TabGNS into the model lifecycle management enables automated design of neural networks throughout the lifecycle, accelerating deployment of ML solutions in telecommunications networks.

AI.tldr: TabGNS is a gradient-based NAS method for tabular data in telecom networks that reduces model size by 51-82% and speeds up search up to 36x, while improving prediction performance across telecom and generic tabular datasets; integrates into lifecycle management for automated neural network design.
AI.motivation: Automate and optimize neural architecture design to produce compact, high-performing models suitable for resource-constrained telecom environments and rapid deployment.
AI.method: Introduces TabGNS (Tabular Gated Neuron Selection), a gradient-based NAS approach specialized for tabular data in telecommunications. The method searches architectures efficiently to balance performance and compactness, leveraging gating mechanisms for neuron selection.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple telecom and generic tabular datasets shows improved prediction performance with substantial reductions in architectural size (51-82%) and search time (up to 36x) compared to state-of-the-art tabular NAS methods.
AI.conclusion: Integrating TabGNS into the model lifecycle enables automated neural network design throughout the lifecycle, accelerating deployment of ML solutions in telecommunications networks.

title: The Sample Complexity of Uniform Approximation for Multi-Dimensional CDFs and Fixed-Price Mechanisms
summary: We study the sample complexity of learning a uniform approximation of an $n$-dimensional cumulative distribution function (CDF) within an error $ε> 0$, when observations are restricted to a minimal one-bit feedback. This serves as a counterpart to the multivariate DKW inequality under ''full feedback'', extending it to the setting of ''bandit feedback''. Our main result shows a near-dimensional-invariance in the sample complexity: we get a uniform $ε$-approximation with a sample complexity $\frac{1}{ε^3}{\log\left(\frac 1 ε\right)^{\mathcal{O}(n)}}$ over a arbitrary fine grid, where the dimensionality $n$ only affects logarithmic terms. As direct corollaries, we provide tight sample complexity bounds and novel regret guarantees for learning fixed-price mechanisms in small markets, such as bilateral trade settings.

AI.tldr: Bandit-feedback uniform CDF learning: near-dimension-invariant sample complexity (1/ε^3) log(1/ε)^{O(n)}; applications to learning fixed-price mechanisms in small markets.
AI.motivation: Extend multivariate DKW-style uniform CDF learning to a bandit (one-bit feedback) setting and quantify sample complexity.
AI.method: Develop grid-based uniform approximation under one-bit feedback; derive upper bounds showing that dimensionality affects only logarithmic factors; connect results to learning fixed-price mechanisms.
AI.result: Proves a sample complexity of (1/ε^3) log(1/ε)^{O(n)} for ε-uniform CDF approximation on a fine grid; yields tight bounds and novel regret guarantees in small-market learning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Extends the DKW inequality to bandit feedback; demonstrates near dimension-invariant sample complexity and practical implications for mechanism design in small markets.

title: Natural Hypergradient Descent: Algorithm Design, Convergence Analysis, and Parallel Implementation
summary: In this work, we propose Natural Hypergradient Descent (NHGD), a new method for solving bilevel optimization problems. To address the computational bottleneck in hypergradient estimation--namely, the need to compute or approximate Hessian inverse--we exploit the statistical structure of the inner optimization problem and use the empirical Fisher information matrix as an asymptotically consistent surrogate for the Hessian. This design enables a parallel optimize-and-approximate framework in which the Hessian-inverse approximation is updated synchronously with the stochastic inner optimization, reusing gradient information at negligible additional cost. Our main theoretical contribution establishes high-probability error bounds and sample complexity guarantees for NHGD that match those of state-of-the-art optimize-then-approximate methods, while significantly reducing computational time overhead. Empirical evaluations on representative bilevel learning tasks further demonstrate the practical advantages of NHGD, highlighting its scalability and effectiveness in large-scale machine learning settings.

AI.tldr: NHGD offers a scalable bilevel optimization method by substituting Hessian inverse with the empirical Fisher, enabling parallelized optimize-and-approximate updates with theoretical guarantees and empirical scalability.
AI.motivation: Hypergradient computation bottlenecks due to Hessian inverse; need scalable, parallelizable methods for large-scale bilevel learning.
AI.method: Use empirical Fisher information as asymptotically consistent surrogate for Hessian; implement a parallel optimize-and-approximate framework updating the Hessian-inverse approximation synchronously with stochastic inner optimization, reusing gradient information at negligible cost.
AI.result: High-probability error bounds and sample complexity matching state-of-the-art optimize-then-approximate methods; reduced computational time overhead; scalability demonstrated empirically on representative bilevel tasks.
AI.conclusion: NHGD provides a practical, scalable approach for bilevel learning with strong theoretical guarantees and empirical performance in large-scale ML settings.

title: Tuning the burn-in phase in training recurrent neural networks improves their performance
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026, https://openreview.net/forum?id=jwkdKpioHJ
summary: Training recurrent neural networks (RNNs) with standard backpropagation through time (BPTT) can be challenging, especially in the presence of long input sequences. A practical alternative to reduce computational and memory overhead is to perform BPTT repeatedly over shorter segments of the training data set, corresponding to truncated BPTT. In this paper, we examine the training of RNNs when using such a truncated learning approach for time series tasks. Specifically, we establish theoretical bounds on the accuracy and performance loss when optimizing over subsequences instead of the full data sequence. This reveals that the burn-in phase of the RNN is an important tuning knob in its training, with significant impact on the performance guarantees. We validate our theoretical results through experiments on standard benchmarks from the fields of system identification and time series forecasting. In all experiments, we observe a strong influence of the burn-in phase on the training process, and proper tuning can lead to a reduction of the prediction error on the training and test data of more than 60% in some cases.

AI.tldr: Truncated BPTT with subsequences can be analyzed; burn-in phase is a key tuning knob, and proper tuning yields substantial performance gains.
AI.motivation: To reduce computational and memory overhead of backpropagation through time for long sequences and to understand the trade-offs when training on subsequences, providing theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Derive theoretical bounds on accuracy and performance loss when optimizing over subsequences in RNN training; analyze burn-in as a primary tuning parameter; validate with experiments on standard time-series benchmarks (system identification and forecasting).
AI.result: Established bounds on accuracy loss due to subsequence training; demonstrated that the burn-in phase strongly influences training dynamics and final performance; appropriate tuning can reduce training and test error by up to 60% in some cases.
AI.conclusion: The burn-in phase is a critical tuning knob in truncated BPTT; proper tuning enables reliable performance with reduced computation and memory overhead.

title: Near-Constant Strong Violation and Last-Iterate Convergence for Online CMDPs via Decaying Safety Margins
summary: We study safe online reinforcement learning in Constrained Markov Decision Processes (CMDPs) under strong regret and violation metrics, which forbid error cancellation over time. Existing primal-dual methods that achieve sublinear strong reward regret inevitably incur growing strong constraint violation or are restricted to average-iterate convergence due to inherent oscillations. To address these limitations, we propose the Flexible safety Domain Optimization via Margin-regularized Exploration (FlexDOME) algorithm, the first to provably achieve near-constant $\tilde{O}(1)$ strong constraint violation alongside sublinear strong regret and non-asymptotic last-iterate convergence. FlexDOME incorporates time-varying safety margins and regularization terms into the primal-dual framework. Our theoretical analysis relies on a novel term-wise asymptotic dominance strategy, where the safety margin is rigorously scheduled to asymptotically majorize the functional decay rates of the optimization and statistical errors, thereby clamping cumulative violations to a near-constant level. Furthermore, we establish non-asymptotic last-iterate convergence guarantees via a policy-dual Lyapunov argument. Experiments corroborate our theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: Introduces FlexDOME, a safe online RL algorithm for CMDPs achieving near-constant strong constraint violation with sublinear strong regret and non-asymptotic last-iterate convergence, by using time-varying safety margins and margin-regularized exploration within a primal-dual framework.
AI.motivation: Address the inability of existing primal-dual CMDP methods to achieve both sublinear strong regret and bounded strong constraint violation without error cancellation, and to guarantee non-asymptotic last-iterate convergence.
AI.method: FlexDOME algorithm; integrates time-varying safety margins and regularization into a primal-dual CMDP framework. Introduces a term-wise asymptotic dominance strategy to schedule safety margins so they dominate optimization/statistical error decay, clamping cumulative violations. Proves non-asymptotic last-iterate convergence via a policy-dual Lyapunov argument; supports theory with experiments.
AI.result: Provably achieves near-constant (tilde O(1)) strong constraint violation, sublinear strong regret, and non-asymptotic last-iterate convergence. Experimental results corroborate theoretical claims.
AI.conclusion: FlexDOME provides provable safety-regret guarantees in CMDPs with improved practical performance by dynamically balancing safety margins and exploration; the term-wise dominance and Lyapunov approach offer a blueprint for preventing drift while maintaining learning efficiency.

title: CMAD: Cooperative Multi-Agent Diffusion via Stochastic Optimal Control
summary: Continuous-time generative models have achieved remarkable success in image restoration and synthesis. However, controlling the composition of multiple pre-trained models remains an open challenge. Current approaches largely treat composition as an algebraic composition of probability densities, such as via products or mixtures of experts. This perspective assumes the target distribution is known explicitly, which is almost never the case. In this work, we propose a different paradigm that formulates compositional generation as a cooperative Stochastic Optimal Control problem. Rather than combining probability densities, we treat pre-trained diffusion models as interacting agents whose diffusion trajectories are jointly steered, via optimal control, toward a shared objective defined on their aggregated output. We validate our framework on conditional MNIST generation and compare it against a naive inference-time DPS-style baseline replacing learned cooperative control with per-step gradient guidance.

AI.tldr: A cooperative stochastic optimal control framework for composing pre-trained diffusion models, reframing composition as joint trajectory optimization instead of density algebra, validated on conditional MNIST against a naive DPS-style baseline.
AI.motivation: Density-based composition assumes a known target distribution, which is seldom available in practice. The authors seek a flexible framework that can coordinate multiple pre-trained generative agents without explicit target densities.
AI.method: Model diffusion models as interacting agents whose trajectories are jointly steered by an optimal control policy toward a shared objective defined on the aggregated outputs. Evaluate the approach on conditional MNIST and compare to an inference-time baseline that replaces the cooperative controller with per-step gradient guidance (DPS-style).
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of cooperative control-based composition and provides empirical comparison to a naive baseline on a conditional generation task (MNIST).
AI.conclusion: Introduces a paradigm shift from density-based composition to cooperative control for integrating multiple pre-trained diffusion models, with initial validation on MNIST suggesting potential advantages over naive gradient-guided baselines.

title: Stochastic Parroting in Temporal Attention -- Regulating the Diagonal Sink
summary: Spatio-temporal models analyze spatial structures and temporal dynamics, which makes them prone to information degeneration among space and time. Prior literature has demonstrated that over-squashing in causal attention or temporal convolutions creates a bias on the first tokens. To analyze whether such a bias is present in temporal attention mechanisms, we derive sensitivity bounds on the expected value of the Jacobian of a temporal attention layer. We theoretically show how off-diagonal attention scores depend on the sequence length, and that temporal attention matrices suffer a diagonal attention sink. We suggest regularization methods, and experimentally demonstrate their effectiveness.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes temporal attention in spatio-temporal models, derives Jacobian-sensitivity bounds, reveals sequence-length effects on off-diagonal attention and a diagonal sink, proposes regularization, and validates it experimentally.
AI.motivation: To understand and mitigate information degeneration and early-token bias (over-squashing) in temporal attention within spatio-temporal models by quantifying sensitivity and proposing remedies.
AI.method: The authors derive bounds on the expected Jacobian of a temporal-attention layer, analyze how off-diagonal attention scores depend on sequence length, identify a diagonal attention sink, propose regularization strategies, and test them experimentally.
AI.result: They obtain theoretical bounds showing sequence-length dependence of attention structure, observe the diagonal attention sink, and demonstrate that the proposed regularization improves empirical performance.
AI.conclusion: Temporal attention exhibits biases that worsen with longer sequences; targeted regularization can mitigate these biases and improve stability, suggesting avenues for robust temporal-attention design.

title: MoEEdit: Efficient and Routing-Stable Knowledge Editing for Mixture-of-Experts LLMs
summary: Knowledge editing (KE) enables precise modifications to factual content in large language models (LLMs). Existing KE methods are largely designed for dense architectures, limiting their applicability to the increasingly prevalent sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models that underpin modern scalable LLMs. Although MoEs offer strong efficiency and capacity scaling, naively adapting dense-model editors is both computationally costly and prone to routing distribution shifts that undermine stability and consistency. To address these challenges, we introduce MoEEdit, the first routing-stable framework for parameter-modifying knowledge editing in MoE LLMs. Our method reparameterizes expert updates via per-expert null-space projections that keep router inputs invariant and thereby suppress routing shifts. The resulting block-structured optimization is solved efficiently with a block coordinate descent (BCD) solver. Experiments show that MoEEdit attains state-of-the-art efficacy and generalization while preserving high specificity and routing stability, with superior compute and memory efficiency. These results establish a robust foundation for scalable, precise knowledge editing in sparse LLMs and underscore the importance of routing-stable interventions.

AI.tldr: MoEEdit introduces routing-stable knowledge editing for sparse Mixture-of-Experts LLMs by projecting updates into per-expert null spaces and solving with block-coordinate descent, achieving strong efficacy with preserved routing stability and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Knowledge editing methods for dense models do not translate well to sparse MoE architectures. Naively editing MoEs can cause routing distribution shifts, harming stability and scalability.
AI.method: Reparameterize expert updates via per-expert null-space projections to keep router inputs invariant, forming a block-structured optimization solved efficiently with a block coordinate descent (BCD) solver.
AI.result: State-of-the-art editing efficacy and generalization, with high specificity and routing stability, and improved compute/memory efficiency on MoE LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Presents a scalable, precise knowledge editing framework for sparse MoE models and emphasizes routing-stable interventions as critical for robust edits.

title: A Jointly Efficient and Optimal Algorithm for Heteroskedastic Generalized Linear Bandits with Adversarial Corruptions
summary: We consider the problem of heteroskedastic generalized linear bandits (GLBs) with adversarial corruptions, which subsumes various stochastic contextual bandit settings, including heteroskedastic linear bandits and logistic/Poisson bandits. We propose HCW-GLB-OMD, which consists of two components: an online mirror descent (OMD)-based estimator and Hessian-based confidence weights to achieve corruption robustness. This is computationally efficient in that it only requires ${O}(1)$ space and time complexity per iteration. Under the self-concordance assumption on the link function, we show a regret bound of $\tilde{O}\left( d \sqrt{\sum_t g(τ_t) \dotμ_{t,\star}} + d^2 g_{\max} κ+ d κC \right)$, where $\dotμ_{t,\star}$ is the slope of $μ$ around the optimal arm at time $t$, $g(τ_t)$'s are potentially exogenously time-varying dispersions (e.g., $g(τ_t) = σ_t^2$ for heteroskedastic linear bandits, $g(τ_t) = 1$ for Bernoulli and Poisson), $g_{\max} = \max_{t \in [T]} g(τ_t)$ is the maximum dispersion, and $C \geq 0$ is the total corruption budget of the adversary. We complement this with a lower bound of $\tildeΩ(d \sqrt{\sum_t g(τ_t) \dotμ_{t,\star}} + d C)$, unifying previous problem-specific lower bounds. Thus, our algorithm achieves, up to a $κ$-factor in the corruption term, instance-wise minimax optimality simultaneously across various instances of heteroskedastic GLBs with adversarial corruptions.

AI.tldr: HCW-GLB-OMD: a corruption-robust, space-efficient estimator for heteroskedastic generalized linear bandits using online mirror descent and Hessian-based confidence weights. It achieves near-optimal instance-wise regret bounds under self-concordant links and adversarial corruption, and provides a matching lower bound, unifying several stochastic contextual settings.
AI.motivation: Unify and robustify heteroskedastic generalized linear bandits (including linear, logistic, Poisson variants) under adversarial corruptions, while maintaining computational efficiency (O(1) per-iteration).
AI.method: Two components: (i) an online mirror descent (OMD) based estimator; (ii) Hessian-based confidence weights. Self-concordance of the link function enables regret analysis. Analyze regret with time-varying dispersions g(τ_t) and corruption budget C. Complexity is O(1) space/time per iteration.
AI.result: Upper regret bound: ~O(d sqrt(sum_t g(τ_t) μ̇_{t,*}) + d^2 g_max κ + d κ C). Lower bound: ~Ω(d sqrt(sum_t g(τ_t) μ̇_{t,*}) + d C). The bounds are instance-wise minimax optimal up to a κ factor in the corruption term, and hold across heterogeneous GLBs with adversarial corruptions.
AI.conclusion: HCW-GLB-OMD achieves corruption-robust, instance-wise minimax-optimal regret with efficient per-iteration computation, unifying several heteroskedastic GLB settings under adversarial noise; results rely on self-concordance and provide bounds that match prior problem-specific results up to κ. Potential extensions include relaxing assumptions and empirical validation.

title: TVCACHE: A Stateful Tool-Value Cache for Post-Training LLM Agents
comment: Abhishek Vijaya Kumar and Bhaskar Kataria have equal contribution
summary: In RL post-training of LLM agents, calls to external tools take several seconds or even minutes, leaving allocated GPUs idle and inflating post-training time and cost. While many tool invocations repeat across parallel rollouts and could in principle be cached, naively caching their outputs for reuse is incorrect since tool outputs depend on the environment state induced by prior agent interactions. We present TVCACHE, a stateful tool-value cache for LLM agent post-training. TVCACHE maintains a tree of observed tool-call sequences and performs longest-prefix matching for cache lookups: a hit occurs only when the agent's full tool history matches a previously executed sequence, guaranteeing identical environment state. On three diverse workloads-terminal-based tasks, SQL generation, and video understanding. TVCACHE achieves cache hit rates of up to 70% and reduces median tool call execution time by up to 6.9X, with no degradation in post-training reward accumulation.

AI.tldr: Introduces TVCACHE, a stateful tool-value cache for LLM agent post-training that uses a longest-prefix tree of observed tool-call sequences to enable safe caching. It yields high hit rates and large latency reductions without harming reward accumulation.
AI.motivation: Naive caching of tool outputs in RL-trained LLM agents is unsafe because tool outputs depend on the evolving environment state created by prior agent interactions, leading to incorrect reuse. There is a need for a state-aware caching mechanism that preserves identical environment states for correctness while reducing latency.
AI.method: TVCACHE builds a tree of observed tool-call sequences and performs longest-prefix matching for cache lookups. A cache hit requires that the agent's full tool history matches a previously executed sequence, guaranteeing identical environment state. Evaluated on three workloads (terminal-based tasks, SQL generation, video understanding).
AI.result: Achieves cache hit rates up to 70% and reduces median tool call execution time by up to 6.9×, with no degradation in post-training reward accumulation.
AI.conclusion: Stateful, prefix-based tool caching can substantially speed up post-training with safe reuse of tool outputs across diverse tasks without harming learning performance.

title: Learning Page Order in Shuffled WOO Releases
summary: We investigate document page ordering on 5,461 shuffled WOO documents (Dutch freedom of information releases) using page embeddings.
