These documents are heterogeneous collections such as emails, legal texts, and spreadsheets compiled into single PDFs, where semantic ordering signals are unreliable. We compare five methods, including pointer networks, seq2seq transformers, and specialized pairwise ranking models. The best performing approach successfully reorders documents up to 15 pages, with Kendall's tau ranging from 0.95 for short documents (2-5 pages) to 0.72 for 15 page documents. We observe two unexpected failures: seq2seq transformers fail to generalize on long documents (Kendall's tau drops from 0.918 on 2-5 pages to 0.014 on 21-25 pages), and curriculum learning underperforms direct training by 39% on long documents. Ablation studies suggest learned positional encodings are one contributing factor to seq2seq failure, though the degradation persists across all encoding variants, indicating multiple interacting causes. Attention pattern analysis reveals that short and long documents require fundamentally different ordering strategies, explaining why curriculum learning fails. Model specialization achieves substantial improvements on longer documents (+0.21 tau).

AI.tldr: A comparative study of five page-ordering methods for shuffled documents shows that specialized pairwise ranking with learned positional encodings best reorders up to 15 pages, while seq2seq transformers fail on longer documents; curriculum learning underperforms; model specialization improves long-document ordering.
AI.motivation: Restore the original page order of heterogeneous, concatenated PDFs (emails, legal texts, spreadsheets) where semantic cues are unreliable, using neural ordering models.
AI.method: Evaluate five methods (including pointer networks, seq2seq transformers, and specialized pairwise ranking models) on 5,461 shuffled Dutch FOI releases (WOO). Perform ablations on positional encodings, analyze attention patterns, and compare curriculum vs direct training.
AI.result: Best method reorders up to 15 pages with Kendall’s tau from 0.95 (2–5 pages) to 0.72 (15 pages). Seq2seq fails on longer documents (tau drops from 0.918 for 2–5 pages to 0.014 for 21–25 pages). Curriculum learning underperforms direct training by 39% on long documents. Ablations implicate learned positional encodings and multiple interacting causes; attention patterns show distinct strategies for short vs long documents. Model specialization yields a +0.21 tau gain on longer documents.
AI.conclusion: Specialized, carefully crafted ranking and representations are more robust for long, heterogeneous documents than generic seq2seq or curriculum strategies. The failures point to multiple interacting factors (positional encoding, attention behavior). Tailored models that account for page-level structure offer the strongest gains for long documents.

title: Divide, Harmonize, Then Conquer It: Shooting Multi-Commodity Flow Problems with Multimodal Language Models
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: The multi-commodity flow (MCF) problem is a fundamental topic in network flow and combinatorial optimization, with broad applications in transportation, communication, and logistics, etc. Nowadays, the rapid expansion of allocation systems has posed challenges for existing optimization engines in balancing optimality and tractability. In this paper, we present Pram, the first ML-based method that leverages the reasoning power of multimodal language models (MLMs) for addressing the trade-off dilemma -- a great need of service providers. As part of our proposal, Pram (i) quickly computes high-quality allocations by dividing the original problem into local subproblems, which are then resolved by an MLM-powered "agent", and (ii) ensures global consistency by harmonizing these subproblems via a multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm. Theoretically, we show that Pram, which learns to perform gradient descent in context, provably converges to the optimum within the family of MCF problems. Empirically, on real-world datasets and public topologies, Pram achieves performance comparable to, and in some cases even surpassing, linear programming solvers (very close to the optimal solution), and substantially lower runtimes (1 to 2 orders of magnitude faster). Moreover, Pram exhibits strong robustness (<10\% performance degradation under link failures or flow bursts), demonstrating MLM's generalization ability to unforeseen events. Pram is objective-agnostic and seamlessly integrates with mainstream allocation systems, providing a practical and scalable solution for future networks.

AI.tldr: Pram is an ML-based method that uses multimodal language models to solve the multi-commodity flow (MCF) problem by decomposing it into local subproblems solved by an MLM-powered agent, with global consistency ensured by multi-agent reinforcement learning. It comes with a convergence guarantee to the optimum within the MCF family, shows near-optimal performance and substantial speedups over LP solvers, and demonstrates robustness to link failures and bursts.
AI.motivation: There is a trade-off between optimality and tractability in allocating systems. Existing optimization engines struggle to balance accuracy with runtime in large-scale network flow problems. A framework that combines ML, especially multimodal reasoning, with optimization could yield fast, high-quality allocations.
AI.method: Decompose MCF into local subproblems. Use an MLM-powered agent to solve subproblems. Harmonize subproblems through a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework to achieve global consistency. Theoretical result: gradient-descent-like learning in context converges to the MCF optimum within the problem family. Empirical result: competitive to LP solvers, with 1–2 orders of magnitude runtime reductions and robustness to perturbations.
AI.result: The method converges to the optimum within the MCF problem family. In real datasets and public topologies, it achieves very close-to-optimal or surpassing LP solver performance, with runtimes 10–100x faster. It shows robustness under link failures or flow bursts with <10% degradation.
AI.conclusion: Pram is objective-agnostic and integrates with mainstream allocation systems, offering a scalable, practical solution for future networks, leveraging MLMs for rapid, reliable, and near-optimal allocations.

title: MoToRec: Sparse-Regularized Multimodal Tokenization for Cold-Start Recommendation
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have revolutionized recommender systems by effectively modeling complex user-item interactions, yet data sparsity and the item cold-start problem significantly impair performance, particularly for new items with limited or no interaction history. While multimodal content offers a promising solution, existing methods result in suboptimal representations for new items due to noise and entanglement in sparse data. To address this, we transform multimodal recommendation into discrete semantic tokenization. We present Sparse-Regularized Multimodal Tokenization for Cold-Start Recommendation (MoToRec), a framework centered on a sparsely-regularized Residual Quantized Variational Autoencoder (RQ-VAE) that generates a compositional semantic code of discrete, interpretable tokens, promoting disentangled representations. MoToRec's architecture is enhanced by three synergistic components: (1) a sparsely-regularized RQ-VAE that promotes disentangled representations, (2) a novel adaptive rarity amplification that promotes prioritized learning for cold-start items, and (3) a hierarchical multi-source graph encoder for robust signal fusion with collaborative signals. Extensive experiments on three large-scale datasets demonstrate MoToRec's superiority over state-of-the-art methods in both overall and cold-start scenarios. Our work validates that discrete tokenization provides an effective and scalable alternative for mitigating the long-standing cold-start challenge.

AI.tldr: MoToRec introduces a sparsity-regularized Residual Quantized VAE to tokenize multimodal item content into discrete, interpretable tokens for cold-start recommendations; it adds rarity-aware learning and a hierarchical multi-source graph encoder, achieving superior performance over SOTA on three large datasets.
AI.motivation: Cold-start and data sparsity in GNN-based recommender systems limit performance, especially for new items with little interaction history. Multimodal signals are noisy and entangled when data are sparse. Discretizing semantics into tokens could yield disentangled, scalable representations that improve cold-start recommendations.
AI.method: Develop Sparse-Regularized RQ-VAE to produce compositional semantic tokens. Introduce adaptive rarity amplification to prioritize cold-start items during training. Employ a hierarchical multi-source graph encoder to robustly fuse collaborative signals with multimodal content. Integrate tokenized representations into the recommender pipeline to improve predictions for both overall and cold-start scenarios.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three large-scale datasets shows MoToRec achieving superior performance over state-of-the-art methods in both overall and cold-start settings.
AI.conclusion: Discrete semantic tokenization via a sparsity-regularized VAE is an effective and scalable solution to the cold-start problem in multimodal GNN-based recommenders, yielding disentangled representations and improved recommendations.

title: Motion Capture is Not the Target Domain: Scaling Synthetic Data for Learning Motion Representations
summary: Synthetic data offers a compelling path to scalable pretraining when real-world data is scarce, but models pretrained on synthetic data often fail to transfer reliably to deployment settings. We study this problem in full-body human motion, where large-scale data collection is infeasible but essential for wearable-based Human Activity Recognition (HAR), and where synthetic motion can be generated from motion-capture-derived representations. We pretrain motion time-series models using such synthetic data and evaluate their transfer across diverse downstream HAR tasks. Our results show that synthetic pretraining improves generalisation when mixed with real data or scaled sufficiently. We also demonstrate that large-scale motion-capture pretraining yields only marginal gains due to domain mismatch with wearable signals, clarifying key sim-to-real challenges and the limits and opportunities of synthetic motion data for transferable HAR representations.

AI.tldr: Synthetic motion-data pretraining can improve HAR transferability when used with real data or scaled; but mocap pretraining alone offers limited gains due to sim-to-real domain mismatch.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity in wearable HAR and assess whether synthetic motion data from motion capture can enable scalable pretraining and transfer to real-world tasks.
AI.method: Pretrain motion time-series models on synthetic data derived from motion-capture representations; evaluate transfer to diverse HAR downstream tasks; compare mixed real+synthetic training versus synthetic-only; analyze scaling effects and the impact of domain mismatch (sim-to-real).
AI.result: Synthetic pretraining improves generalization when combined with real data or scaled sufficiently; large-scale mocap pretraining yields only marginal gains due to domain mismatch between synthetic motion and wearable signals.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic motion data can support transferable HAR representations, but effectiveness is limited by sim-to-real gaps. To maximize benefit, align synthetic and real data distributions, incorporate real data in training, and address sensor-domain differences; future work should focus on reducing domain mismatch and more realistic synthetic generation.

title: Token-Efficient Change Detection in LLM APIs
summary:
Remote change detection in LLMs is a difficult problem. Existing methods are either too expensive for deployment at scale, or require initial white-box access to model weights or grey-box access to log probabilities. We aim to achieve both low cost and strict black-box operation, observing only output tokens.
  Our approach hinges on specific inputs we call Border Inputs, for which there exists more than one output top token. From a statistical perspective, optimal change detection depends on the model's Jacobian and the Fisher information of the output distribution. Analyzing these quantities in low-temperature regimes shows that border inputs enable powerful change detection tests.
  Building on this insight, we propose the Black-Box Border Input Tracking (B3IT) scheme. Extensive in-vivo and in-vitro experiments show that border inputs are easily found for non-reasoning tested endpoints, and achieve performance on par with the best available grey-box approaches. B3IT reduces costs by $30\times$ compared to existing methods, while operating in a strict black-box setting.

AI.tldr: Black-box border-input tracking (B3IT) detects remote LLM changes using border inputs, matching grey-box performance at ~30x lower cost by observing only output tokens.
AI.motivation: Need scalable, low-cost, strict black-box remote change detection for LLMs without white-box access or log probabilities.
AI.method: Identify 'border inputs' where multiple top tokens exist; analyze model Jacobian and output Fisher information in low-temperature regimes; design B3IT scheme; validate via in-vivo/in-vitro experiments on non-reasoning endpoints.
AI.result: Border inputs enable strong change detection; B3IT achieves performance on par with best grey-box methods; reduces cost ~30x compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Border-input based black-box monitoring is a practical, scalable approach for LLM change detection; applicability to non-reasoning endpoints demonstrated; further work needed for broader endpoint coverage and reasoning tasks.

title: MerLin: A Discovery Engine for Photonic and Hybrid Quantum Machine Learning
comment: This work has been submitted to the 2026 IEEE World Congress on Computational Intelligence
summary: Identifying where quantum models may offer practical benefits in near term quantum machine learning (QML) requires moving beyond isolated algorithmic proposals toward systematic and empirical exploration across models, datasets, and hardware constraints. We introduce MerLin, an open source framework designed as a discovery engine for photonic and hybrid quantum machine learning. MerLin integrates optimized strong simulation of linear optical circuits into standard PyTorch and scikit learn workflows, enabling end to end differentiable training of quantum layers. MerLin is designed around systematic benchmarking and reproducibility. As an initial contribution, we reproduce eighteen state of the art photonic and hybrid QML works spanning kernel methods, reservoir computing, convolutional and recurrent architectures, generative models, and modern training paradigms. These reproductions are released as reusable, modular experiments that can be directly extended and adapted, establishing a shared experimental baseline consistent with empirical benchmarking methodologies widely adopted in modern artificial intelligence. By embedding photonic quantum models within established machine learning ecosystems, MerLin allows practitioners to leverage existing tooling for ablation studies, cross modality comparisons, and hybrid classical quantum workflows. The framework already implements hardware aware features, allowing tests on available quantum hardware while enabling exploration beyond its current capabilities, positioning MerLin as a future proof co design tool linking algorithms, benchmarks, and hardware.

AI.tldr: MerLin is an open-source discovery engine for photonic and hybrid quantum machine learning that integrates optimized linear optical circuit simulations into PyTorch/Scikit-Learn, enabling end-to-end differentiable training, systematic benchmarking, and reproducing 18 state-of-the-art QML works to establish reusable baselines and hardware-aware workflows.
AI.motivation: To move beyond isolated quantum ML proposals and enable systematic, empirical exploration across models, datasets, and hardware constraints in near-term photonic QML, addressing reproducibility and practical benchmarking.
AI.method: Integrates optimized strong simulation of linear optical circuits with standard ML tooling (PyTorch, scikit-learn) to enable end-to-end differentiable quantum layers. Provides modular, reproducible experiments, hardware-aware features, and a benchmark-centered workflow that reproduces a broad set of state-of-the-art photonic/hybrid QML approaches.
AI.result: Reproduces eighteen state-of-the-art photonic and hybrid QML works spanning kernel methods, reservoir computing, CNNs, RNNs, generative models, and modern training paradigms. Delivers reusable, modular experiments as a shared baseline suitable for ablations, cross-modality comparisons, and hybrid classical/quantum workflows.
AI.conclusion: MerLin positions itself as a future-proof co-design tool that links algorithms, benchmarks, and hardware, enabling practical evaluation of QML benefits and facilitating hardware-aware experimentation and reproducibility within established ML ecosystems.

title: Statistical Learning Analysis of Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: We study the training and performance of physics-informed learning for initial and boundary value problems (IBVP) with physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) from a statistical learning perspective. Specifically, we restrict ourselves to parameterizations with hard initial and boundary condition constraints and reformulate the problem of estimating PINN parameters as a statistical learning problem. From this perspective, the physics penalty on the IBVP residuals can be better understood not as a regularizing term bus as an infinite source of indirect data, and the learning process as fitting the PINN distribution of residuals $p(y \mid x, t, w) q(x, t) $ to the true data-generating distribution $δ(0) q(x, t)$ by minimizing the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the true and PINN distributions. Furthermore, this analysis show that physics-informed learning with PINNs is a singular learning problem, and we employ singular learning theory tools, namely the so-called Local Learning Coefficient (Lau et al., 2025) to analyze the estimates of PINN parameters obtained via stochastic optimization for a heat equation IBVP. Finally, we discuss implications of this analysis on the quantification of predictive uncertainty of PINNs and the extrapolation capacity of PINNs.

AI.tldr: PINN training framed as singular statistical learning; physics residuals act as data; analyzed with Local Learning Coefficient; implications for uncertainty quantification and extrapolation in heat-IBVP.
AI.motivation: Bridge physics-informed learning with statistical learning theory, clarifying the role of physics penalties and guiding uncertainty and extrapolation analyses.
AI.method: Reformulate PINN parameter estimation with hard BCs as KL-divergence minimization between the PINN residual distribution and the true data-generating distribution; apply singular learning theory tools (Local Learning Coefficient) to a heat equation IBVP.
AI.result: Demonstrates that PINN learning is a singular problem; uses Local Learning Coefficient to characterize SGD-estimated parameters for a heat equation IBVP.
AI.conclusion: Highlights implications for predictive uncertainty quantification and extrapolation capacity of PINNs, informing robustness and design of physics-informed models.

title: From Natural Language to Materials Discovery:The Materials Knowledge Navigation Agent
summary: Accelerating the discovery of high-performance materials remains a central challenge across energy, electronics, and aerospace technologies, where traditional workflows depend heavily on expert intuition and computationally expensive simulations. Here we introduce the Materials Knowledge Navigation Agent (MKNA), a language-driven system that translates natural-language scientific intent into executable actions for database retrieval, property prediction, structure generation, and stability evaluation. Beyond automating tool invocation, MKNA autonomously extracts quantitative thresholds and chemically meaningful design motifs from literature and database evidence, enabling data-grounded hypothesis formation. Applied to the search for high-Debye-temperature ceramics, the agent identifies a literature-supported screening criterion (Theta_D > 800 K), rediscovers canonical ultra-stiff materials such as diamond, SiC, SiN, and BeO, and proposes thermodynamically stable, previously unreported Be-C-rich compounds that populate the sparsely explored 1500-1700 K regime. These results demonstrate that MKNA not only finds stable candidates but also reconstructs interpretable design heuristics, establishing a generalizable platform for autonomous, language-guided materials exploration.

AI.tldr: Introduces MKNA, a language-driven agent that converts natural-language scientific intent into executable actions for data retrieval, property prediction, structure generation, and stability evaluation; autonomously extracts quantitative thresholds and design motifs from literature to guide hypothesis formation and discovery.
AI.motivation: To accelerate materials discovery by reducing reliance on expert intuition and expensive simulations, while producing interpretable, data-grounded design heuristics.
AI.method: A language-driven agent interfaces with literature and databases to perform retrieval, property prediction, structure generation, and stability assessment. It autonomously extracts quantitative thresholds and design motifs from evidence, enabling data-grounded hypothesis formation. Applied to high-Debye-temperature ceramics, it identifies a Theta_D > 800 K screening criterion, rediscovered canonical stiff materials, and proposes thermodynamically stable Be–C–rich compounds in a sparsely explored temperature window.
AI.result: The agent recovers known high-Debye-temperature materials (e.g., diamond, SiC, SiN, BeO), derives a literature-supported screening metric (Theta_D > 800 K), and proposes previously unreported Be–C–rich candidates that are stable in the 1500–1700 K range, demonstrating autonomous design-heuristic reconstruction and broad applicability.
AI.conclusion: MKNA demonstrates autonomous, language-guided materials exploration that yields interpretable design heuristics and stable candidate discovery, offering a generalizable platform for rapid, data-grounded materials discovery.

title: The Offline-Frontier Shift: Diagnosing Distributional Limits in Generative Multi-Objective Optimization
summary: Offline multi-objective optimization (MOO) aims to recover Pareto-optimal designs given a finite, static dataset. Recent generative approaches, including diffusion models, show strong performance under hypervolume, yet their behavior under other established MOO metrics is less understood. We show that generative methods systematically underperform evolutionary alternatives with respect to other metrics, such as generational distance. We relate this failure mode to the offline-frontier shift, i.e., the displacement of the offline dataset from the Pareto front, which acts as a fundamental limitation in offline MOO. We argue that overcoming this limitation requires out-of-distribution sampling in objective space (via an integral probability metric) and empirically observe that generative methods remain conservatively close to the offline objective distribution. Our results position offline MOO as a distribution-shift--limited problem and provide a diagnostic lens for understanding when and why generative optimization methods fail.

AI.tldr: Offline MOO with generative models underperforms on non-hypervolume metrics due to offline-frontier shift; IPM-based out-of-distribution sampling in objective space may alleviate it, framing offline MOO as distribution-shift-limited.
AI.motivation: Understand why generative approaches (e.g., diffusion models) do well on hypervolume but lag on other MOO metrics when trained on finite offline data, and diagnose fundamental limitations.
AI.method: Theoretical framing of offline-frontier shift; empirical comparison of generative methods versus evolutionary baselines across MOO metrics (e.g., generational distance). Analysis using an integral probability metric to characterize objective-space sampling and the offline distribution.
AI.result: Generative methods systematically underperform on GD/IGD-like metrics; the displacement of the offline frontier from the true Pareto front explains this weakness; generative models tend to stay close to the offline objective distribution; IPM-based OOD sampling can mitigate but remains challenging.
AI.conclusion: Offline MOO is distribution-shift–limited. Overcoming this requires deliberate out-of-distribution sampling in objective space. The work provides a diagnostic lens to understand when generative optimization fails and highlights the need to address dataset shift for broader metric success.

title: Asymmetric Prompt Weighting for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has driven recent advances in LLM post-training, in particular for reasoning. Policy optimization algorithms generate a number of responses for a given prompt and then effectively weight the corresponding gradients depending on the rewards. The most popular algorithms including GRPO, DAPO, and RLOO focus on ambiguous prompts, i.e., prompts with intermediate success probability, while downgrading gradients with very easy and very hard prompts. In this paper, we consider asymmetric prompt weightings that assign higher weights to prompts with low, or even zero, empirical success probability. We find that asymmetric weighting particularly benefits from-scratch RL (as in R1-Zero), where training traverses a wide accuracy range, and less so in post-SFT RL where the model already starts at high accuracy. We also provide theory that characterizes prompt weights which minimize the time needed to raise success probability from an initial level to a target accuracy under a fixed update budget. In low-success regimes, where informative responses are rare and response cost dominates, these optimal weights become asymmetric, upweighting low success probabilities and thereby accelerating effective-time convergence.

AI.tldr: Asymmetric prompt weighting in RL for LLM post-training improves learning efficiency, especially for from-scratch RL; theory derives optimal weights to minimize time to target accuracy under a fixed update budget; low-success regimes benefit from upweighting rare informative prompts.
AI.motivation: To overcome inefficiencies of existing gradient weighting schemes (GRPO, DAPO, RLOO) that downweight easy/hard prompts, by favoring prompts with low or zero empirical success probability to accelerate convergence, particularly in early training.
AI.method: Analyzes gradient weighting schemes within policy optimization for LLMs; derives theoretical characterization of optimal prompt weights under a fixed update budget; tests by comparing from-scratch RL vs post-SFT RL under asymmetric weighting.
AI.result: Asymmetric weights yield faster convergence in low-success regimes and more pronounced gains for from-scratch RL (R1-Zero) than for post-SFT RL; the theory provides explicit guidance on weight assignments to minimize time to reach target accuracy.
AI.conclusion: In sparse/low-success settings, boosting low-success prompts via asymmetric weighting can significantly speed up learning; practical implications for RLHF pipelines and prompt design; further work needed on estimation of success probabilities and robustness.

title: Just on Time: Token-Level Early Stopping for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion language models generate text through iterative refinement, a process that is often computationally inefficient because many tokens reach stability long before the final denoising step. We introduce a training-free, token-level early stopping approach that identifies convergence independently at each position. Our method leverages lightweight signals derived from the model's predictions and local context to dynamically determine when individual tokens can be finalized. This yields adaptive per-token freezing without task-specific fine-tuning, substantially reducing the total number of diffusion steps required. Across diverse benchmarks, spanning mathematical reasoning, general question answering, and scientific understanding, our approach achieves state-of-the-art efficiency gains while preserving generation quality.

AI.tldr: Training-free per-token early stopping for diffusion LMs yields adaptive token-level freezing, cutting steps dramatically while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion language models waste compute as many tokens reach stability before the final denoising step; a per-token convergence signal is needed without extra fine-tuning.
AI.method: Introduce a training-free, token-level early stopping mechanism that uses lightweight signals from the model's predictions and local context to decide per-token finalization at each step; enables dynamic per-token freezing without task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.result: Across math reasoning, general question answering, and scientific understanding benchmarks, achieves state-of-the-art efficiency gains with preserved generation quality by reducing total diffusion steps.
AI.conclusion: Per-token adaptive stopping is a practical, task-agnostic acceleration technique for diffusion models, enabling substantial speedups without sacrificing accuracy across diverse tasks.

title: TabICLv2: A better, faster, scalable, and open tabular foundation model
summary: Tabular foundation models, such as TabPFNv2 and TabICL, have recently dethroned gradient-boosted trees at the top of predictive benchmarks, demonstrating the value of in-context learning for tabular data. We introduce TabICLv2, a new state-of-the-art foundation model for regression and classification built on three pillars: (1) a novel synthetic data generation engine designed for high pretraining diversity; (2) various architectural innovations, including a new scalable softmax in attention improving generalization to larger datasets without prohibitive long-sequence pretraining; and (3) optimized pretraining protocols, notably replacing AdamW with the Muon optimizer. On the TabArena and TALENT benchmarks, TabICLv2 without any tuning surpasses the performance of the current state of the art, RealTabPFN-2.5 (hyperparameter-tuned, ensembled, and fine-tuned on real data). With only moderate pretraining compute, TabICLv2 generalizes effectively to million-scale datasets under 50GB GPU memory while being markedly faster than RealTabPFN-2.5. We provide extensive ablation studies to quantify these contributions and commit to open research by first releasing inference code and model weights at https://github.com/soda-inria/tabicl, with synthetic data engine and pretraining code to follow.

AI.tldr: TabICLv2 sets a new state-of-the-art in tabular foundation models for regression/classification, using a synthetic data pretraining engine, scalable attention, and the Muon optimizer to outperform tuned RealTabPFN-2.5 on TabArena and TALENT with good scalability and faster training.
AI.motivation: Address the need for stronger tabular foundation models with broader pretraining diversity, scalable architectures for longer/sequences, and efficient optimization to enable large-scale pretraining and strong zero-shot/per-context performance.
AI.method: Three pillars: (1) a synthetic data generation engine to maximize pretraining diversity; (2) architectural innovation including a scalable softmax-based attention to support larger datasets with efficient long-sequence pretraining; (3) optimized pretraining protocols, notably replacing AdamW with the Muon optimizer. Evaluation on TabArena and TALENT without tuning shows SOTA performance; ablations quantify contributions; plan to release inference code and model weights, with synthetic data engine and pretraining code subsequently.
AI.result: TabICLv2 surpasses the current SOTA RealTabPFN-2.5 on TabArena and TALENT without tuning; demonstrates strong generalization to million-scale datasets under 50 GB GPU memory; faster training than RealTabPFN-2.5 with moderate pretraining compute; extensive ablation studies validate each contribution.
AI.conclusion: TabICLv2 advances tabular foundation modeling by combining diverse synthetic pretraining data, scalable attention, and efficient optimization, achieving state-of-the-art results with practical scalability and releasing resources to support reproducibility.

title: Diffusion-Pretrained Dense and Contextual Embeddings
summary: In this report, we introduce pplx-embed, a family of multilingual embedding models that employ multi-stage contrastive learning on a diffusion-pretrained language model backbone for web-scale retrieval. By leveraging bidirectional attention through diffusion-based pretraining, our models capture comprehensive bidirectional context within passages, enabling the use of mean pooling and a late chunking strategy to better preserve global context across long documents. We release two model types: pplx-embed-v1 for standard retrieval, and pplx-embed-context-v1 for contextualized embeddings that incorporate global document context into passage representations. pplx-embed-v1 achieves competitive performance on the MTEB(Multilingual, v2), MTEB(Code), MIRACL, BERGEN, and ToolRet retrieval benchmarks, while pplx-embed-context-v1 sets new records on the ConTEB benchmark. Beyond public benchmarks, pplx-embed-v1 demonstrates strong performance on our internal evaluation suite, which focuses on real-world, large-scale search scenarios over tens of millions of documents. These results validate the models' effectiveness in production environments where retrieval quality and efficiency are critical at scale.

AI.tldr: Pplx-embed introduces multilingual embedding models using diffusion-pretrained backbones and multi-stage contrastive learning for scalable web retrieval, with v1 for standard retrieval and a context-aware variant that leverages global document context; both variants achieve strong benchmarks and show promising production-scale performance.
AI.motivation: To improve multilingual web-scale retrieval by leveraging bidirectional context through diffusion-based pretraining, enabling effective passage representations and long-document context handling for efficient large-scale search.
AI.method: A family of models trained with multi-stage contrastive learning on a diffusion-pretrained language model backbone. Utilizes bidirectional attention, mean pooling, and a late chunking strategy to preserve global context. Two model types: pplx-embed-v1 for standard retrieval and pplx-embed-context-v1 for contextualized embeddings that incorporate global document context.
AI.result: Pplx-embed-v1 achieves competitive results on MTEB (Multilingual, v2), MTEB Code, MIRACL, BERGEN, and ToolRet. Pplx-embed-context-v1 sets new records on ConTEB. In internal evaluations on large-scale search over tens of millions of documents, both models show strong production-ready performance in terms of retrieval quality and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The diffusion-pretrained, multi-stage contrastive approach effectively captures bidirectional and global document context, yielding high-quality multilingual embeddings suitable for production-scale retrieval. Providing both a standard and a contextualized variant offers flexibility for different retrieval scenarios.

title: Enhancing IMU-Based Online Handwriting Recognition via Contrastive Learning with Zero Inference Overhead
summary: Online handwriting recognition using inertial measurement units opens up handwriting on paper as input for digital devices. Doing it on edge hardware improves privacy and lowers latency, but entails memory constraints. To address this, we propose Error-enhanced Contrastive Handwriting Recognition (ECHWR), a training framework designed to improve feature representation and recognition accuracy without increasing inference costs. ECHWR utilizes a temporary auxiliary branch that aligns sensor signals with semantic text embeddings during the training phase. This alignment is maintained through a dual contrastive objective: an in-batch contrastive loss for general modality alignment and a novel error-based contrastive loss that distinguishes between correct signals and synthetic hard negatives. The auxiliary branch is discarded after training, which allows the deployed model to keep its original, efficient architecture. Evaluations on the OnHW-Words500 dataset show that ECHWR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, reducing character error rates by up to 7.4% on the writer-independent split and 10.4% on the writer-dependent split. Finally, although our ablation studies indicate that solving specific challenges require specific architectural and objective configurations, error-based contrastive loss shows its effectiveness for handling unseen writing styles.

AI.tldr: A training-time auxiliary branch with dual contrastive objectives improves edge-handwriting recognition without extra inference cost, achieving notable CER reductions on OnHW-Words500; gains are more pronounced in writer-dependent modes, and error-based contrastive loss enhances generalization to unseen styles.
AI.motivation: Overcome memory constraints on edge hardware in online handwriting recognition by boosting feature representation during training rather than increasing runtime complexity.
AI.method: Introduce a temporary auxiliary branch that aligns inertial sensor signals with semantic text embeddings during training. Use in-batch contrastive loss for general modality alignment and an error-based contrastive loss to separate correct signals from synthetic hard negatives. Discard the auxiliary branch after training; deploy the base model with unchanged architecture.
AI.result: CER reductions up to 7.4% (writer-independent) and 10.4% (writer-dependent) on OnHW-Words500. Ablation indicates configuration matters; error-based loss aids handling unseen writing styles.
AI.conclusion: ECHWR effectively enhances recognition under resource constraints, leveraging training-time alignment and error-based negatives; results suggest benefits for robustness to writer variation, though task-specific tuning is advised.
